rationales.D: T21 features brachycephaly, upslanting fissures, single palmar crease, AVSD and hypotonia; does not produce supravalvular AS or infantile hypercalcaemia. Would be correct with AVSD and T21 facies (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Turner features bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation, not supravalvular AS; lymphoedema/webbed neck/short stature, not hypercalcaemia or elfin facies. Would be correct in phenotypic female with coarctation (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Williams has supravalvular aortic stenosis with elfin facies; this child has dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis with Noonan facies. Would be correct with elfin facies and supravalvular AS (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Turner affects phenotypic females with bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation; this patient is male with PS. Would be correct only in a phenotypic female (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: Marfan features tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis and aortic root dilatation; short stature with webbed neck and PS does not fit. Would be correct with tall stature and lens dislocation (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Marfan).
rationales.E: T21 features upslanting fissures, single palmar crease and AVSD; downslanting fissures and pulmonary valve dysplasia do not fit. Would be correct with classic T21 features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Ghent criteria met (ectopia lentis plus aortic root Z-score over 2 with family history); reassurance and high-impact contact sport is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Marfan).
rationales.B: Sotalol is not the agent of choice in Marfan; atenolol/propranolol or losartan slow root dilatation, and contact/competitive sport must be avoided. Would never be correct (Source: AHA scientific statement).
rationales.C: Elective root replacement is indicated at >= 5.0 cm in adults, or >= 4.5 cm with family history of dissection/rapid expansion; at 4.4 cm with Z 3.8 medical therapy and surveillance are appropriate. Would be correct at >= 5.0 cm or rapid expansion (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Losartan (ARB), not ramipril (ACEi), is shown to slow root dilatation; competitive isometric/strenuous sport is restricted. Would be correct only if ramipril and competitive swimming were guideline-endorsed (they are not) (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Klinefelter has tall stature, small testes (not macroorchidism) and milder cognitive impact; FXTAS/FXPOI family pattern is not explained. Would be correct with 47,XXY phenotype (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.B: Williams (7q11.23 deletion) would have shown on prior microarray; elfin facies/supravalvular AS/hypercalcaemia and macroorchidism are inconsistent. Would never be correct given the negative microarray (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Prader-Willi has neonatal hypotonia, hyperphagia, obesity and small testes; macroorchidism does not fit. Would be correct with hypotonia and hypogonadism (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: TSC features hypopigmented macules, facial angiofibromas, cardiac rhabdomyomas and epilepsy; none present. Would be correct with seizures and skin findings (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.B: Haemodynamically stable and asymptomatic; transfusion reserved for symptomatic anaemia or Hb under 70 with CV compromise. Would be correct with symptomatic anaemia or Hb under 70 (Source: RCH CPG: Anaemia).
rationales.C: Ferritin unequivocally low and dietary history fits iron deficiency; haemoglobinopathy screening is only considered after iron therapy failure or by ethnic background. Would be correct after failed iron replacement (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: IV iron in a toddler is reserved for malabsorption, oral intolerance or non-response to oral therapy. Would be correct only after oral trial failure (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion).
rationales.E: Multivitamin iron (5 mg) is inadequate (required 3-6 mg/kg/day, ~36-72 mg for this 12 kg child) and excessive milk perpetuates deficiency. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency).

rationales.A: Emollients alone do not control established eczema with lichenification; appropriate TCS use is safe and effective. Would be correct only for very mild dry skin without inflammation (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema).
rationales.C: Potent TCS should not be used on the face in young children; boom-bust without proactive maintenance leads to repeated flares. Would never be correct in this age group on face (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for severe refractory eczema after optimised topical therapy; she has not had adequate site-matched TCS plus emollient. Would be correct only after failed maximised topical regimen (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Routine oral antibiotics do not improve non-infected eczema and drive resistance; bleach baths may help colonisation but are not maintenance monotherapy. Would be correct only for documented infected eczema (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Potent TCS on face with concurrent HSV is harmful; bleach baths do not treat HSV. Would never be correct in suspected eczema herpeticum (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: This is HSV not impetigo; mupirocin does not treat the virus, and continuing TCS over HSV can worsen dissemination. Would be correct in confirmed impetigo (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Honey-crusting and polymorphic pustules would suggest S aureus impetigo; monomorphic umbilicated vesicles and HSV contact indicate eczema herpeticum. Would be correct with classic impetigo features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Periorbital involvement, fever and poor intake in a 3-year-old with eczema herpeticum warrant admission for IV therapy and ophthalmology. Would be correct only for mild non-facial eczema herpeticum with good intake (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Overflow soiling with large painful stools and palpable faecal mass indicates impaction; dietary measures alone will not clear it. Would be correct as part of maintenance after disimpaction (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation).
rationales.B: Loperamide for overflow soiling worsens impaction by stopping the overflow without clearing the obstruction. Would never be correct in functional constipation with overflow (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Meconium within 48 hours, normal growth, no distension - functional constipation is the working diagnosis; treatment should not be delayed. Would be correct with red flags (delayed meconium, FTT, abdominal distension) (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Oral macrogol disimpaction is first-line; phosphate enemas risk hyperphosphataemia in young children and are reserved for failed oral disimpaction. Would be correct after failed oral disimpaction (Source: RCH CPG; AMH).

rationales.A: Delayed meconium over 48 hours, faltering growth, bilious vomiting and explosive PR are red flags for organic cause; discharge without further investigation is unsafe. Would be correct only without red flags (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation).
rationales.B: Anal stenosis has a narrow caudal anus on exam; here the anus is normally sited and obstruction is functional from aganglionic segment. Would be correct with anal stenosis on exam (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: CMPA causes loose stools, blood streaks or eczema, not delayed meconium and obstruction. Would be correct with blood-streaked stools and atopy (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Congenital hypothyroidism is detected on newborn heelprick and causes prolonged jaundice/lethargy, not delayed meconium with explosive PR. Would be correct with positive Guthrie (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Pharmacological appetite stimulation is not first-line in young children; address feeding behaviour, energy density and mealtime environment first. Would be correct only as specialist add-on after multidisciplinary first-line (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Hospital admission is for severe malnutrition, safeguarding concerns or failed community management. Would be correct with severe undernutrition or social concerns (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Investigations are history/exam-guided; only 1-2 percent of faltering growth is non-nutritional organic. Broad untargeted screen is low-yield. Would be correct only with specific red-flag features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Safeguarding is considered if history is inconsistent or other concerns exist; feeding history and dietary advice come first. Would be correct only with specific safeguarding red flags (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Restrictive diets are not recommended as primary; behavioural/environmental supports plus stimulants are first-line. Would be correct only with documented food trigger and dietitian oversight (Source: RACGP; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Stimulants are first-line in children without contraindications; atomoxetine is for stimulant intolerance, tics, anxiety or diversion risk. Would be correct with those specific indications (Source: AADPA ADHD).
rationales.D: Over 6 months of symptoms with functional impairment in 2 settings warrants treatment; behavioural strategies are necessary but not sufficient. Would be correct only with mild symptoms in one setting (Source: AADPA 2022).
rationales.E: Routine cardiology review is not required with unremarkable history/exam/ECG; only red flags (family sudden death, exertional syncope, abnormal ECG) mandate review. Would be correct only with red flags (Source: AADPA).

rationales.A: Multiple core ASD features plus M-CHAT-R/F at high-risk threshold; reassurance delays access to early intervention. Would be correct only with isolated mild language delay and normal social communication (Source: Autism CRC National Guideline).
rationales.C: Genetic testing (CMA, Fragile X) is arranged by the paediatrician alongside multidisciplinary assessment, not as a standalone first step. Would be correct as part of paediatrician-led workup (Source: Autism CRC).
rationales.D: ASD pharmacotherapy is reserved for specific severe behavioural symptoms after specialist assessment; not first-line at age 18 months. Would be correct only after specialist review for severe behaviour (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Speech pathology alone misses multidimensional ASD; multidisciplinary assessment with audiology and NDIS early intervention is needed. Would be correct only as adjunct, not sole referral (Source: Autism CRC).

rationales.A: Confidentiality has limits with risk of harm; involving a supportive parent with consent improves outcomes. Would be correct only if there were no immediate safety concern (Source: RACGP Adolescent Confidentiality).
rationales.B: Psychological therapy is first-line for mild-moderate adolescent depression; fluoxetine starts at 10 mg with close monitoring alongside therapy. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders).
rationales.C: Involuntary admission is for acute risk with intent/plan or inability to maintain safety. Passive ideation without plan, with family support, and willingness to engage warrants community management. Would be correct only with imminent risk and refusal to engage (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Self-harm with persistent low mood and passive suicidal ideation is a mental illness marker and elevated future suicide risk. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP; headspace).

rationales.A: Multiple MARSIPAN-AU high-risk criteria mandate inpatient admission. Would be correct only with low-risk vitals and electrolytes (Source: RANZCP; ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.B: Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive in eating disorders, not a substitute for medical stabilisation and family-based treatment. Would be correct as adjunct after stabilisation (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Aggressive refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome (fluid overload, hypophosphataemia, arrhythmia, Wernicke encephalopathy). Would never be correct in high-risk patient (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.D: She is medically unstable; medical stabilisation precedes or runs alongside psychiatric admission. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).

rationales.B: MCUG (catheter, ionising radiation) is not first-line after recurrent UTI in AU pathway; DMSA is preferred to stratify further imaging. Would be correct with hydroureter, abnormal voiding or DMSA-detected scarring (Source: KHA-CARI; RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Prophylaxis is considered after risk stratification (high-grade VUR, significant scarring); empirical prophylaxis without imaging is not first-line. Would be correct after DMSA shows significant scarring or high-grade VUR (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Cystoscopy is not indicated in straightforward recurrent UTI without anatomical concerns on US. Would be correct with bladder anomaly on US (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: After two febrile UTIs, deferred imaging misses scarring; DMSA at 4-6 months is recommended. Would be correct with single UTI and resolved clinical picture (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Deferring all vaccines leaves the immunocompromised mother more vulnerable to vaccine-preventable disease in the home; child should be fully immunised, including most live vaccines. Would never be correct as blanket policy (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.C: No inactivated MMR alternative exists on NIP; MMR vaccine viruses are not transmitted from vaccinees to contacts. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.D: Oral typhoid is not routinely indicated and not part of NIP catch-up; introduces a live oral vaccine inappropriately. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.E: MMR and varicella are NOT contraindicated in household contacts of immunocompromised people; he remains at risk if unprotected. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS; eviQ).

rationales.A: Withholding general care is unethical and not supported by RACGP/NCIRS; trusted continuity is a key driver of eventual acceptance. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP; NCIRS SKAI).
rationales.C: Vaccine hesitancy in an otherwise well-cared-for child is not itself medical neglect. Would be correct only with broader documented neglect (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.D: Alternative schedules prolong susceptibility, increase visits/pain, and reinforce immune-overload misconception. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS SKAI).
rationales.E: Court orders are a last resort after parental deadlock and other approaches have failed; initial step is shared GP-led discussion. Would be correct only after exhausted negotiation and ongoing risk to child (Source: NCIRS).

rationales.A: Bisphosphonates have no role in nutritional rickets and may impair mineralisation; deformity usually corrects with vitamin D and calcium repletion. Would never be correct in nutritional rickets (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Stoss therapy carries hypercalcaemia risk in young children and limits follow-up biochemistry; daily replacement is safer and standard in Australia. Would be correct only in selected older children with adherence concerns under specialist input (Source: APEG).
rationales.C: IV calcium is reserved for symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, ECG changes); he is asymptomatic. Would be correct with tetany, seizures or ECG changes (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Calcitriol is reserved for vitamin D-dependent rickets, CKD and hypoparathyroidism; nutritional deficiency uses cholecalciferol because 1-alpha-hydroxylation is intact. Would be correct in CKD or VDDR (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Steroids and antihistamines do not treat airway/CV collapse and have no role as the next escalation after adrenaline failure. Would be correct only as adjunct after definitive adrenaline (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.B: After two or more IM doses without response, refractory anaphylaxis is declared; IV infusion is preferred for sustained titratable effect. Would be correct only if IV access were unavailable (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.D: Glucagon is for refractory anaphylaxis in beta-blocked patients; this child is not on beta-blockers. Would be correct only with documented beta-blocker use (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.E: Hydroxocobalamin is the cyanide-toxicity antidote (smoke inhalation), no role in anaphylaxis. Would be correct only with smoke inhalation and suspected cyanide toxicity (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

rationales.A: Phenytoin is acceptable but EcLiPSE and ConSEPT trials and updated APLS prefer levetiracetam, especially under 2 years where phenytoin extravasation risk is higher. Would be correct in older children with appropriate venous access (Source: APLS Australia Status Epilepticus).
rationales.B: Phenobarbitone is the preferred second-line agent only in neonates; outside the neonatal period it is third-line. Would be correct in a neonate (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.C: More than two benzodiazepine doses increases respiratory depression without improving termination; algorithm advances to second-line antiepileptic. Would never be correct (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.E: Thiopentone induction is for refractory status after second-line agents have failed; jumping straight to RSI skips the algorithm. Would be correct only in refractory status (Source: APLS Australia).

rationales.A: Already failed three back-to-back nebulised doses plus systemic steroid; persisting risks decompensation. Would be correct only at an earlier step before escalation (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).
rationales.C: Aminophylline is reserved for failure of IV magnesium and IV salbutamol due to narrow therapeutic index and arrhythmia risk. Would be correct as third-line in PICU (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: IV salbutamol typically follows IV magnesium; potassium 3.0 and lactate 3.8 will worsen with more beta-agonist. Would be correct after IV magnesium failure (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Intubation in severe asthma risks bronchospasm, hypotension and barotrauma; reserved for impending arrest after maximal medical therapy. Would be correct only after maximal therapy and impending arrest (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Noradrenaline is first-line in warm shock (bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure); this child has cold shock with vasoconstriction. Would be correct in warm shock (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).
rationales.C: After 40-60 mL/kg in septic shock without response, further crystalloid risks pulmonary oedema; inotropes should be started. Would be correct only before reaching 40-60 mL/kg threshold (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric; FEAST trial).
rationales.D: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is for catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency; not the next step before any inotrope. Would be correct after inotrope started and shock persists (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).
rationales.E: Delaying inotrope for central access in fluid-refractory shock increases mortality; dilute peripheral or IO is recommended. Would never be correct (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).

rationales.A: Adding dextrose/reducing insulin is appropriate when glucose falls below ~14-17 mmol/L but does not address suspected cerebral oedema with bradycardia, hypertension and falling GCS. Would be correct at later glucose phase without cerebral oedema (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.B: Increasing fluid worsens cerebral oedema; PECARN FLUID supports cautious rehydration with hyperosmolar therapy when oedema suspected. Would never be correct in suspected oedema (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.D: Dexamethasone reduces vasogenic oedema (tumour, meningitis) but has no role in DKA-related cytotoxic/osmotic oedema. Would never be correct in DKA cerebral oedema (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.E: Prophylactic hyperventilation below pCO2 30 in DKA cerebral oedema worsens outcomes; intubation may follow but is not the first step before hyperosmolar therapy. Would be correct only after hyperosmolar therapy and clinical deterioration (Source: ISPAD DKA).

rationales.A: Adenosine is for stable SVT; this is haemodynamically unstable SVT requiring immediate synchronised cardioversion. Would be correct in stable SVT (Source: ANZCOR 12.7).
rationales.C: Unsynchronised defibrillation is for pulseless rhythms; delivering on a perfusing rhythm risks VF induction. Would be correct only in pulseless VT/VF (Source: ANZCOR 12.7).
rationales.D: Amiodarone is for refractory/recurrent SVT in stable patients or post-cardioversion; not before electrical therapy in unstable SVT. Would be correct as adjunct after cardioversion (Source: ANZCOR 12.7).
rationales.E: Verapamil is contraindicated under 1 year and unsafe with poor cardiac output (profound hypotension/arrest). Would never be correct in this patient (Source: ANZCOR 12.7).

rationales.A: Impending respiratory failure (hypoxia, exhaustion, paradoxical breathing); passive waiting is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.B: Repeat adrenaline is reasonable as a bridge but does not substitute for definitive airway when impending failure is present. Would be correct only as bridge alongside airway team activation (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.C: HFNC does not bypass critical subglottic narrowing and must not delay airway team. Would be correct only in milder distress without impending failure (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: RSI with paralysis risks losing the airway when bag-mask ventilation may be difficult; inhalational induction preserving spontaneous ventilation in theatre is standard. Would be correct only in catastrophic airway loss when inhalational induction impossible (Source: ANZCA airway guidance).

rationales.A: Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies only to single acute ingestions with known time; in staggered or unknown timing it cannot be used. Would be correct only for single acute ingestion with known time and level under treatment line (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).
rationales.B: ALT and INR rise late (24-36 h); waiting in a staggered overdose over 200 mg/kg risks missing the treatment window. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Activated charcoal is useful within 1-2 h of ingestion; here ingestions are 18 h and 4 h old, minimal benefit, and NAC must not be delayed. Would be correct within 1-2 h of single ingestion (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Oral methionine is not used in current Australian practice; IV NAC is the standard. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Large-volume crystalloid worsens dilutional coagulopathy and acidosis in haemorrhagic shock; blood products are the next resuscitation fluid. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Trauma).
rationales.C: TXA should be given empirically within 3 h of injury with significant bleeding; waiting for viscoelastic testing delays a time-critical intervention. Would be correct only outside the 3-h window (Source: ANZ Paediatric MTP).
rationales.D: TXA is not contraindicated in paediatric trauma; recommended within 3 h. Cryoprecipitate is used for fibrinogen under 1.5 g/L but is not the first product in MTP. Would never be correct (Source: ANZ Paediatric MTP).
rationales.E: Vasopressors as primary treatment of haemorrhagic shock mask ongoing bleeding and worsen tissue perfusion; volume with blood products is the priority. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Trauma).

rationales.A: Frenulum tear in a pre-mobile infant is a sentinel injury for NAI; never warrants outpatient reassurance. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).
rationales.B: Coagulation studies are part of workup but a normal screen does not exclude inflicted injury; discharge without protective action is unsafe. Would never be sufficient (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Mandatory reporting is required at the time of clinical suspicion, not deferred. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; state child protection legislation).
rationales.D: Discharging a pre-mobile infant with sentinel injury without statutory notification does not meet duty of care. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Underestimates the modified Parkland requirement and conflates maintenance with resuscitation fluid; AU paediatric burns adds maintenance ON TOP. Would never be correct (Source: ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines).
rationales.C: Hartmann's is preferred over 0.9 percent saline (chloride load); maintenance is given separately in children. Would never be correct (Source: ANZBA).
rationales.D: Colloid is added after the first 8-12 h in selected paediatric burns; crystalloid (Hartmann's) is primary in the first 24 h. Would be correct as adjunct after 12 h (Source: ANZBA).
rationales.E: Over-resuscitation (volumes well above 4 mL/kg/%TBSA) causes burn oedema and abdominal compartment syndrome; target urine is 1 mL/kg/h, not 2. Would never be correct (Source: ANZBA).

rationales.B: Croup has coryzal prodrome with barking cough and hoarseness, not drooling, muffled voice or toxic appearance. Would be correct in barking cough without drooling (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.C: FB aspiration follows witnessed choking with focal asymmetry; no choking history plus high fever and toxic appearance make this unlikely. Would be correct with witnessed choking and focal signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Bacterial tracheitis usually follows several days of croup-like illness; rapid single-afternoon onset and tripoding favour epiglottitis. Would be correct with preceding croup-like illness over days (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Retropharyngeal abscess usually presents with neck stiffness/torticollis and preceding URTI or dental focus; CT-before-airway in a tripoding child with impending obstruction is unsafe. Would be correct with neck stiffness, no impending obstruction (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Salbutamol shifts potassium but acts more slowly than calcium and does not protect the myocardium; calcium membrane stabilisation is the first step with ECG changes. Would be correct as second step after calcium (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia).
rationales.B: Bicarbonate has limited variable effect on potassium and worsens hypocalcaemia (already 1.78), risking tetany/arrhythmia. Would be correct only with severe metabolic acidosis and normocalcaemia (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.D: Resin binders are slow-acting and unsuitable for immediate intervention with ECG changes; carry GI necrosis risk. Would be correct only as adjunct for chronic hyperkalaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Loop diuretics need functional kidneys and are ineffective in oliguric AKI from TLS; cardiac protection and dialysis are needed. Would be correct only in non-oliguric AKI with preserved function (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Closing the duct in duct-dependent systemic circulation precipitates cardiovascular collapse; the goal is to keep the duct OPEN. Would never be correct in duct-dependent lesion (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation).
rationales.B: No narrow-complex tachycardia over 220 bpm with absent P waves; picture is duct-dependent systemic shock not SVT. Would be correct only in SVT (Source: ANZCOR 12.7).
rationales.C: Sildenafil is used in persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn, not duct-dependent systemic lesions; does not maintain ductal patency. Would never be correct here (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: High FiO2 promotes ductal closure and worsens systemic perfusion in duct-dependent systemic lesions. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Cardiology).

rationales.A: Vitamin K and prothrombinex reverse warfarin; dabigatran is a direct thrombin inhibitor not reversed by vitamin K. Would be correct in warfarin-treated patient (Source: ANZSBT Guidelines).
rationales.B: Andexanet alfa reverses factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban), not dabigatran. Would be correct in apixaban/rivaroxaban-treated patient (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: FFP does not reliably reverse direct thrombin inhibitors and volume-loads the patient. Would be correct only as bridge when idarucizumab unavailable (Source: ANZSBT).
rationales.D: Recombinant FVIIa is off-label, carries thrombotic risk especially with mechanical valve, and is not a specific reversal agent. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: ANZSBT).

rationales.A: Symptomatic hyperglycaemia with ketonaemia mandates same-day specialist paediatric review for insulin initiation and education. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD; RCH CPG: T1DM).
rationales.B: DKA-protocol fluid plus insulin is reserved for pH under 7.30 or HCO3 under 15; this child's pH 7.35 and HCO3 are above threshold. Would be correct with pH under 7.30 or HCO3 under 15 (Source: ISPAD).
rationales.D: Metformin is not first-line in suspected paediatric T1DM; insulin is required. Would be correct only in confirmed T2DM (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Glucose will not self-correct without exogenous insulin in T1DM. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Insulin should not start until at least 1 h after fluid resuscitation has begun, because early insulin increases cerebral oedema risk and rapid osmolar shifts. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).
rationales.B: IV insulin boluses are contraindicated in paediatric DKA (cerebral oedema risk); rapid deficit replacement under 48 h increases osmolar shifts. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD 2022).
rationales.C: Dextrose addition before glucose falls below 14-17 mmol/L is inappropriate; 0.15 units/kg/h exceeds the 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h ceiling. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).
rationales.E: Rapid 6-h deficit replacement and simultaneous insulin commencement substantially increase cerebral oedema risk; deficit is replaced over 48 h. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD 2022).

rationales.A: Bradycardia with hypertension and falling GCS is Cushing response indicating raised ICP from cerebral oedema; fluid expansion worsens oedema and rate must be REDUCED. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD 2022).
rationales.B: Glucose 14 is not the cause of declining GCS; stopping insulin worsens ketosis; picture points to cerebral oedema not hypoglycaemia. Would be correct only in confirmed hypoglycaemia (Source: ISPAD 2022).
rationales.D: Bicarbonate is an independent risk factor for DKA-associated cerebral oedema and is not indicated; pH already improving to 7.18. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD 2022).
rationales.E: LP in raised ICP risks coning and delays osmotherapy; the picture is cerebral oedema not meningitis. Would never be correct in suspected DKA cerebral oedema (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).

rationales.A: Oral glucose is contraindicated with reduced consciousness or active seizure (aspiration risk); parenteral glucagon is indicated. Would be correct only with alert child able to swallow (Source: APEG hypoglycaemia).
rationales.B: 1 mg glucagon is the dose for 25 kg or more; she is 22 kg. Insulin is contraindicated in hypoglycaemia. Would be correct in over-25-kg child without insulin error (Source: APEG; Hypokit dosing).
rationales.C: Severe hypoglycaemia with seizure needs immediate treatment; delay risks hypoxic injury and prolonged seizure. Would never be correct (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Hypoglycaemic seizure terminates with glucose, not benzodiazepine; midazolam first risks airway compromise without correcting cause. Would never be correct in hypoglycaemic seizure (Source: APLS Australia).

rationales.A: Pyloric stenosis gives hypochloraemic hypokalaemic ALKALOSIS; this infant has HYPERkalaemia and ACIDOSIS (opposite). Would be correct with classic pyloric biochemistry (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: GBS can present with shock but does not characteristically produce the specific Na-low, K-high, glucose-low pattern; electrolyte signature is the discriminator. Would be correct only with positive cultures and no electrolyte signature (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis).
rationales.C: Urea cycle defects produce respiratory ALKALOSIS with hyperammonaemia, not hyperkalaemic metabolic acidosis. Would be correct with high ammonia and respiratory alkalosis (Source: HMSANZ).
rationales.D: PHA1 shares the biochemistry but is much rarer; distinguished by elevated aldosterone with normal 17-OHP (opposite of CAH). Would be correct with normal 17-OHP and elevated aldosterone (Source: APEG).

rationales.A: She is shocked, hypoglycaemic and vomiting; oral therapy is inappropriate, parenteral hydrocortisone with IV resuscitation is required. Would never be correct in adrenal crisis (Source: RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis).
rationales.B: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid activity and is unsuitable for adrenal crisis; high-dose hydrocortisone provides both effects. Would be correct only in cerebral oedema (which is not the issue) (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: IM is acceptable if no IV access, but IV is preferred when access available; oral fludrocortisone does not address acute crisis and is not stress-dosed. Would be correct only without IV access (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Methylprednisolone is not preferred; hypertonic saline is not indicated as she has only mild hyponatraemia not cerebral oedema. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: GH provocation testing is second-line after exclusion of systemic disease, hypothyroidism, coeliac and Turner; first-line is broad screening. Would be correct after first-line normal (Source: RCH CPG; APEG).
rationales.C: Pituitary MRI is indicated after biochemical confirmation of GHD or with neurological features, not as first-line screen. Would be correct after confirmed GHD (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Addison's is not a typical cause of isolated short stature with normal weight; targeted adrenal testing is not first-line. Would be correct only with hyperpigmentation or salt-craving (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: IBD workup is targeted to GI symptoms or abnormal first-line bloods; endoscopy is invasive not screening. Would be correct with GI symptoms or anaemia/raised CRP (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: Pelvic US already shows pubertal pattern without ovarian mass; pelvic MRI is reserved for US abnormality or suspected peripheral cause. Would be correct only with US abnormality (Source: RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty).
rationales.C: Non-classical CAH causes premature adrenarche (pubic/axillary hair, body odour) without breast development; this child has Tanner B3 breast indicating gonadarche. Would be correct with isolated adrenarche features (Source: ESPE).
rationales.D: Treatment should not precede confirmation of diagnosis and underlying cause (including MRI in girls under 6-8 with central PP). Would never be correct without confirmation (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: MRI is done after central PP is biochemically confirmed (or in girls under 6 with central features), not as the first discriminator. Would be correct after biochemical confirmation (Source: ESPE).

rationales.A: Confirmed venous TSH 88 with fT4 5 is unequivocal CH; delaying treatment 3 weeks significantly worsens neurodevelopmental outcome. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Congenital Hypothyroidism).
rationales.C: 2 microg/kg/day is the adult-equivalent maintenance dose, markedly inadequate for neonatal CH (10-15 mcg/kg/day required). Would be correct only as adult maintenance, not neonatal initiation (Source: ESPE-ATA).
rationales.D: Treatment must not be delayed for imaging; scan can be done concurrently or after stabilisation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Oral levothyroxine is the standard; T3 is reserved for myxoedema coma. Would never be correct in neonatal CH (Source: ESPE-ATA).

rationales.A: Full adult replacement risks angina-like symptoms, tachyarrhythmia and behavioural symptoms in adolescents; titrate from lower dose. Would be correct only in severe hypothyroidism with myxoedema features (Source: RCH CPG: Hypothyroidism).
rationales.B: Carbimazole/propranolol treat hyperthyroidism; this is hypothyroidism (high TSH, low fT4). Would be correct in hyperthyroidism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Smooth diffuse goitre with anti-TPO is Hashimoto's; FNA is for discrete nodules or rapidly growing goitre. Would be correct with palpable discrete nodule (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: This is overt hypothyroidism (TSH 78, low fT4), not subclinical, requiring treatment. Would be correct only with subclinical (mildly raised TSH, normal fT4) (Source: ESPE).

rationales.A: PTU has a black-box warning for severe hepatotoxicity and acute liver failure; not first-line in paediatric Graves. Would be correct only with carbimazole intolerance, or first trimester pregnancy (Source: ESPE).
rationales.B: Initial therapy is medical with thionamide for 18-24 months; radioiodine offered after relapse or in older adolescents with persistent disease. Would be correct after relapse (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism).
rationales.C: Surgery is for thionamide failure/intolerance, very large goitre, or Graves ophthalmopathy where radioiodine unsuitable. Would be correct in those specific situations (Source: ESPE).
rationales.E: Inorganic iodine is used pre-operatively or in thyroid storm, not as chronic monotherapy. Would be correct only as pre-op or thyroid storm adjunct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Water deprivation test risks dangerous dehydration when there is already biochemical evidence of inappropriate dilute urine in hypernatraemia; diagnosis is already established. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice: DI).
rationales.C: Renal imaging is reasonable later if nephrogenic DI is confirmed by no desmopressin response; not first-line discriminator. Would be correct after confirmed nephrogenic DI (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: Random glucose 4.8 and urine SG 1.002 exclude osmotic diuresis; this is hypotonic polyuria not DM. Would be correct only with hyperglycaemia and glycosuria (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Calcium normal at 2.4; chronic hypercalcaemia is unlikely primary cause here; LCH-related central DI is most likely given history. Would be correct only with hypercalcaemia (Source: ESPE).

rationales.B: Hypertonic saline is for symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, severe altered mental state) at Na under 125; she has mild headache only and is alert. Would be correct with seizures or altered mental state at Na under 125 (Source: RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia).
rationales.C: Increasing fluid worsens dilutional hyponatraemia in SIADH; this is not cerebral salt wasting (which is hypovolaemic). Would be correct in CSW with hypovolaemia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Tolvaptan is rarely used in paediatrics, requires inpatient initiation, and is not first-line for transient post-meningitis SIADH. Would be correct only in chronic refractory SIADH (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Furosemide is sometimes adjunctive in chronic SIADH refractory to restriction; first-line is fluid restriction. Would be correct as adjunct after restriction fails (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Calcitriol plus phosphate is the regimen for X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets, not nutritional rickets. Would be correct in XLH with low phosphate and FGF-23 driven phosphaturia (Source: APEG/ANZBMS).
rationales.C: Single high-dose IM regimens carry hypercalcaemia risk and are no longer preferred; oral daily or stoss is safer and equally effective. Would be correct only when adherence is impossible (Source: RCH CPG: Vitamin D Deficiency).
rationales.D: PTH is elevated (secondary hyperparathyroidism in nutritional deficiency); FGF-23-mediated phosphaturia is suggested when 25-OH normal and PTH normal/low. Would be correct in XLH biochemistry (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Low-calcium/low-phosphate diets worsen rickets; the deficient nutrient is vitamin D and calcium needs to be supplied. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Orlistat has limited efficacy and significant GI side effects in adolescents; not first-line in AU paediatric practice. Would be correct only after metformin failure with high-fat diet (Source: NHMRC Obesity Guidance).
rationales.C: Bariatric surgery is considered after intensive medical/lifestyle therapy failure at BMI over 40 (or over 35 with comorbidities) with MDT assessment. Would be correct only with established intensive treatment failure (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.D: GLP-1 agonists are emerging but TGA paediatric approval and PBS subsidy are limited; metformin remains first-line. Would be correct in selected cases with T2DM under specialist input (Source: NHMRC; TGA).
rationales.E: Thyroid hormone in a euthyroid child is contraindicated and causes iatrogenic hyperthyroidism. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Symptom-based screening misses subclinical hypothyroidism (common and frequently asymptomatic in T21); biochemical surveillance is recommended. Would never be correct in T21 (Source: RCH CPG: Down Syndrome).
rationales.B: Six-monthly testing is recommended only in the first year (CH risk highest); from 12 months annual screening is sufficient. Would be correct only in the first year of life (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Three-yearly testing is too infrequent given high background incidence; routine autoantibodies are not standard. Would never be correct in T21 (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Endocrinology referral is for abnormal results or clinical concerns; uncomplicated annual screening is primary/general paediatric care. Would be correct only with abnormal TSH/fT4 (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Routine flex-ext films in asymptomatic T21 children are no longer recommended (poor predictive value). Would be correct only with symptoms/signs of cord compression (Source: RCH CPG: Down Syndrome).
rationales.C: MRI is reserved for symptoms/signs of cord compression (neck pain, torticollis, gait deterioration, incontinence, hyperreflexia); not routine pre-sport. Would be correct with neuro signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Normal exam does not exclude AAI and does not negate elevated T21 risk; trampolining/gymnastics/diving/contact rugby remain discouraged. Would never be correct as blanket clearance (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Blanket sport restriction is not recommended and deprives developmental/social benefit; many sports are encouraged with appropriate selection. Would never be correct as blanket restriction (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts plus hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy is leukaemia until proven otherwise; delay is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; ANZCHOG).
rationales.B: Empirical oral antibiotics without focus and without addressing marrow failure delays diagnosis. Would be correct only with documented infective focus and normal bloods (Source: ANZCHOG).
rationales.D: Nutritional workup does not explain thrombocytopenia, blasts, splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Would be correct only in pure microcytic anaemia without these features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Outpatient referral risks dangerous delay; needs same-day tertiary oncology assessment. Would never be correct (Source: ANZCHOG).

rationales.A: Symptom-based surveillance misses progressive aortic dilatation (frequently silent in Turner and a major mortality cause). Would never be correct (Source: Australasian Turner Syndrome Clinical Guideline).
rationales.B: 5-yearly imaging is insufficient given lifetime aortic root dilatation/dissection risk; more frequent imaging through childhood plus transition MRI is recommended. Would never be correct (Source: International Turner Syndrome Consensus).
rationales.C: BP and ECG do not exclude aortic root dilatation or evolving coarctation; imaging surveillance is required. Would never be correct as sole surveillance (Source: International Turner Syndrome Consensus).
rationales.E: Annual MRI in a young child often requires sedation and is not warranted in asymptomatic patient with normal recent echo. Would be correct only with abnormal echo or symptoms (Source: International Turner Syndrome Consensus).

rationales.A: Hydration alone without removing vitamin D and limiting dietary calcium will not address the underlying problem and risks progressive nephrocalcinosis. Would never be correct in Williams hypercalcaemia (Source: AAP Williams Syndrome Health Supervision).
rationales.B: Steroids are for refractory hypercalcaemia after dietary/supplement adjustment; first-line is removing vitamin D and calcium restriction. Would be correct only after first-line failure (Source: AAP Williams Syndrome).
rationales.C: Bisphosphonates are for severe/symptomatic/refractory hypercalcaemia with long-lasting bone effects in young children; not first-line in mild-to-moderate disease. Would be correct only with severe refractory hypercalcaemia (Source: AAP Williams Syndrome).
rationales.D: Loop diuretics promote calciuria but are reserved for severe symptomatic hypercalcaemia with adequate hydration; not first-line in mild outpatient disease. Would be correct in severe symptomatic hypercalcaemia (Source: AAP Williams Syndrome).

rationales.B: Valvuloplasty in Noonan is for severe PS (peak gradient over 60 mmHg) or symptomatic disease, with reduced efficacy given dysplastic valve. Would be correct with severe PS gradient (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: GH in Noonan is considered later in childhood after endocrinology assessment; not appropriate at 8 months with active feeding difficulties. Would be correct in older child after feeding stabilised (Source: APEG).
rationales.D: Routine prophylactic platelet transfusions are not indicated in asymptomatic infants; baseline coagulation/platelet function assessment is appropriate. Would be correct only with active bleeding (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Counselling alone does not address failure to thrive; immediate growth/feeding intervention is needed. Would be correct as part of broader plan, not alone (Source: Noonan Syndrome Consensus).

rationales.A: Whole-exome sequencing is not appropriate as first-line when a specific familial variant is known; cascade testing is more efficient and definitive. Would be correct only without known familial variant (Source: Marfan Foundation).
rationales.B: Multi-gene panels are first-line when no familial variant is known; with a confirmed variant, cascade is faster, cheaper and definitive. Would be correct only without known familial variant (Source: Marfan Foundation).
rationales.C: Symptom-based screening in a first-degree relative of confirmed FBN1 variant carrier is unsafe; root dilatation is usually asymptomatic until dissection. Would never be correct (Source: Marfan Foundation).
rationales.D: Medical therapy starts after diagnosis is made and aortic dimensions documented; empirical beta-blockade before confirming is premature. Would be correct after confirmed diagnosis (Source: Marfan Foundation).

rationales.A: Z-score increase from 2.5 to 3.2 over 12 months warrants both pharmacological aortic protection and high-intensity isometric/burst-Valsalva sport modification. Would never be correct (Source: Marfan Foundation; Bethesda).
rationales.C: Elective root replacement is at ~5.0 cm in adults (or smaller with rapid progression/family dissection); current diameter does not meet threshold. Would be correct at >=5.0 cm or rapid progression (Source: AHA).
rationales.D: Pharmacotherapy does not substitute for activity modification; competitive contact/isometric/burst-Valsalva sport is restricted regardless of medication. Would never be correct (Source: AHA exercise guidance).
rationales.E: Complete cessation is not recommended; moderate aerobic non-isometric exercise is encouraged for cardiovascular/psychosocial benefit. Would never be correct (Source: Bethesda/AHA).

rationales.A: Hypermobile EDS does not explain translucent skin, prominent venous pattern, spontaneous pneumothorax, easy bruising, or familial COL3A1 variant. Would be correct only in isolated hypermobility (Source: EDS Society Care Recommendations).
rationales.C: No marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation, no ectopia lentis, no high-arched palate; known familial COL3A1 not FBN1. Would never be correct (Source: Marfan Foundation).
rationales.D: Classical EDS features atrophic scarring and marked skin hyperextensibility; familial COL3A1 directs cascade not COL5A1/A2. Would be correct only with classical EDS features and family history (Source: EDS Society).
rationales.E: Catheter arteriography in vascular EDS carries high arterial dissection risk; non-invasive MRA/CTA is preferred. Would never be correct (Source: EDS Society).

rationales.A: Heterozygous females with full mutation often have variable milder phenotype from X-inactivation; premutation inheritance does not cause full Fragile X. Would never be correct (Source: National Fragile X Foundation).
rationales.B: Fragile X is X-linked, not autosomal recessive; this fundamentally changes carrier risk and reproductive counselling. Would never be correct (Source: National Fragile X Foundation).
rationales.C: Premutation alleles can expand on female-line transmission and cause clinical disease (FXPOI in ~20% of female carriers, FXTAS). Would never be correct (Source: National Fragile X Foundation).
rationales.D: Male premutation carriers transmit only stable premutation to daughters, never the full mutation directly; her father almost certainly carries a premutation. Would never be correct (Source: National Fragile X Foundation).

rationales.A: 2021 revised criteria allow diagnosis on two features and explicitly include 6+ CALMs plus skin-fold freckling without family history. Would never be correct (Source: 2021 Revised NIH/NF1 Criteria).
rationales.B: Molecular confirmation is one criterion but not required when two clinical criteria are present. Would be correct only with one clinical feature plus molecular variant (Source: 2021 NIH).
rationales.C: Threshold is 6+ CALMs over 5 mm pre-pubertally (or over 15 mm post-pubertally), not 10. Would never be correct (Source: 2021 NIH).
rationales.E: Affected first-degree relative is one of several alternative features, not required when two clinical features are present. Would be correct only with one clinical feature plus first-degree relative (Source: 2021 NIH).

rationales.A: Routine screening MRI is not recommended in asymptomatic NF1 children (most OPGs are indolent); MRI is for clinical/ophthalmic abnormality. Would be correct only with visual change or ophthalmic abnormality (Source: AAP NF1 Health Supervision).
rationales.B: Pre-verbal/young children cannot reliably report visual loss; scheduled ophthalmology detects OPG when intervention may preserve vision. Would never be correct (Source: AAP NF1).
rationales.D: Standard primary-care vision checks are not adequately sensitive for OPG in NF1; formal paediatric ophthalmology is required. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG: NF1).
rationales.E: CT delivers ionising radiation compounding NF1-associated second-tumour risk and is less sensitive than MRI for OPG. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Levetiracetam is not first-line for infantile spasms; RCT evidence supports vigabatrin (in TSC) and hormonal therapy otherwise. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile Spasms).
rationales.B: Hormonal therapy is first-line for infantile spasms without identified cause; in TSC-related spasms vigabatrin is preferred (RCT-superior). Would be correct in non-TSC infantile spasms (Source: International TSC Consensus 2021).
rationales.C: Valproate is not first-line and is avoided in young female infants due to teratogenicity; lower efficacy than hormonal/vigabatrin. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Ketogenic diet is recognised for refractory infantile spasms but not first-line; trial vigabatrin first in TSC. Would be correct after vigabatrin failure (Source: International TSC Consensus).

rationales.B: Although lesions regress, multiple rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with TSC and require workup. Would never be correct (Source: International TSC Consensus).
rationales.C: Williams causes supravalvular AS and elfin facies, not cardiac rhabdomyomas. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: Noonan cardiac involvement is pulmonary valve stenosis and HCM, not rhabdomyomas. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Rhabdomyomas typically peak in the third trimester and regress after birth; surgery is reserved for haemodynamic obstruction or refractory arrhythmia. Would be correct only with obstruction or arrhythmia (Source: International TSC Consensus).

rationales.B: 22q11.2 deletion is sub-microscopic and is not detected by standard karyotype; CMA or targeted FISH is required. Would never be correct (Source: HGSA).
rationales.C: CH screening does not address the syndromic combination of cardiac anomaly, hypocalcaemia and thymic hypoplasia. Would be correct only with isolated CH (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Maternal vitamin D deficiency can cause neonatal hypocalcaemia but does not explain cardiac/thymic/facies. Would be correct only with isolated hypocalcaemia (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Cranial US does not investigate the unifying syndromic diagnosis. Would be correct only with intracranial concern (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Pyloric stenosis is never a surgical emergency; operating on an alkalotic, hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic infant risks post-anaesthetic apnoea (compensatory CSF alkalosis). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis).
rationales.C: Projectile non-bilious vomiting with palpable olive and hypochloraemic alkalosis is pyloric stenosis, not GORD. Would be correct in mild reflux without electrolyte disturbance (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Vomiting is non-bilious; upper GI contrast is for suspected malrotation with bilious vomiting. Would be correct with bilious vomiting (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: CMPA does not produce hypochloraemic alkalosis or palpable olive. Would be correct with blood-streaked stools and dermatitis (Source: ASCIA).

rationales.A: Confirmed intussusception with red-currant stool requires immediate reduction; delay risks ischaemia and perforation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).
rationales.C: Surgery is for failed enema, peritonism, perforation, or pathological lead point; no peritonism here and air enema has higher success/lower morbidity. Would be correct with peritonism or failed enema (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: US is gold-standard; CT adds radiation and delay without changing management. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty on US (Source: RANZCR; RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Conservative observation of confirmed ileocolic intussusception causes progressive ischaemia; reduction within 24 h has best success. Would never be correct in confirmed cases (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Watch-and-wait for bilious neonatal vomiting can result in complete midgut loss within 4-6 h of established volvulus. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Malrotation).
rationales.C: USS whirlpool sign is supportive but absence does not exclude malrotation; upper GI contrast is definitive. Would be correct only as adjunct, not gold-standard (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Plain AXR may show double-bubble or paucity of distal gas but is insufficient to confirm/exclude malrotation. Would be correct only as initial screen with upper GI to follow (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Meconium ileus is a differential but in a term infant with meconium passed at 18 h, malrotation is more likely. Would be correct only with strong meconium ileus features (CF family history, microcolon suspicion) (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: PAS 7, WCC 14.8, CRP 38 and migratory pain is high-probability appendicitis; discharge is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Appendicitis).
rationales.B: CT is for diagnostic uncertainty after US or when US is non-visualising in larger children; US is first-line to minimise paediatric radiation. Would be correct only after non-visualising US (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.D: Antibiotic-only management is a research approach in selected uncomplicated cases, not standard AU practice; confirmation must precede non-operative trial. Would be correct only in research protocols (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: MRI is not routinely available out-of-hours and is not first-line; US gives best speed-to-diagnosis. Would be correct only with CT contraindication and US non-visualising (Source: RANZCR).

rationales.A: Torsion is clinical; US must not delay exploration. Salvage falls from over 90 percent within 6 h to under 10 percent beyond 24 h. Would never be correct in classic torsion presentation (Source: RCH CPG; USANZ).
rationales.B: Picture (high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn, sudden onset, no urinary symptoms) is torsion not epididymo-orchitis. Antibiotics waste ischaemic time. Would be correct only with epididymo-orchitis features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Manual detorsion may be attempted while preparing theatre but does not replace surgical exploration and fixation. Discharge without fixation risks re-torsion. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: USANZ).
rationales.E: Adding 90 min of transfer when local capability exists pushes total ischaemic time over 6 h. Would be correct only when local paediatric surgical capability is unavailable (Source: USANZ).

rationales.A: Soap-bubble (Neuhauser) is meconium ileus, not duodenal atresia or volvulus. Would be correct in meconium ileus (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.B: Pneumatosis and portal venous gas are NEC findings, clinically and radiographically distinct. Would be correct in NEC (Source: RCH CPG: NEC).
rationales.D: Free gas indicates perforation, not uncomplicated atresia or volvulus. Would be correct in perforation (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.E: Corkscrew is a fluoroscopic finding on upper GI contrast in malrotation with volvulus, not a plain-film sign. Would be correct on upper GI contrast, not on plain film (Source: RANZCR).

rationales.A: CF can cause meconium ileus but does not explain chronic explosive PR decompression or current enterocolitis. Would be correct as adjunct workup in suspected CF (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Anorectal manometry shows absent RAIR but is technically difficult in young children and supportive only; biopsy is gold-standard. Would be correct as supportive test in older children (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Contrast enema may show transition zone and reversed recto-sigmoid ratio but is supportive only; biopsy is required. Empirical laxatives miss the disease. Would be correct as initial radiological assessment, not definitive (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Calprotectin is for suspected IBD, not Hirschsprung; stool MCS may identify enterocolitis pathogens but does not diagnose aganglionosis. Would be correct in suspected IBD (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes must commence immediately on NEC suspicion. Waiting for cultures delays critical therapy. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Established NEC mandates immediate NBM; continuing feeds worsens ischaemic injury. Probiotics are prophylactic in some units but no role in established NEC. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Surgery is for perforation (free gas, fixed loop, deterioration despite medical management). Bell IIB without perforation is medical with surgical standby. Would be correct only with perforation or clinical deterioration (Source: RCH CPG NEC).
rationales.E: Peritoneal drainage is a salvage technique for unstable ELBW with perforation; not a substitute for antibiotics or definitive laparotomy. Would be correct only as salvage in unstable ELBW (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Honey contraindication is for under-12 months (botulism risk); this child is 22 months. Helicopter is faster than 2-h road transfer for an oesophageal battery. Would be correct only under 12 months (Source: RCH CPG; NHMRC Infant Feeding).
rationales.B: Any oesophageal button battery is a surgical emergency regardless of current symptoms; delay risks perforation, TOF and aorto-oesophageal fistula. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; ACCC).
rationales.C: Induced emesis is contraindicated (aspiration, further oesophageal injury); ipecac is no longer recommended by PIC for any ingestion. Would never be correct (Source: PIC).
rationales.D: Watchful waiting is unsafe for oesophageal battery; 4-h delay pushes beyond 2-h necrosis threshold. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Blood/mucus in stool, eczema, atopic family history and faltering growth are red flags for CMPA, not GORD. PPI trials should not delay elimination diet. Would be correct in uncomplicated GORD without atopy or blood (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.C: Soy is not first-line under 6 months (phytoestrogens) and 10-15 percent CMPA cross-reacts. Extensively hydrolysed formula is first-line. Would be correct in older infants after EHF failure (Source: ASCIA; NHMRC).
rationales.D: Non-IgE-mediated CMPA (the likely subtype) is not detected by IgE/SPT. Diagnosis is by elimination and re-challenge. Would be correct only in suspected IgE-mediated (immediate reactions) (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.E: Diluting formula reduces caloric/electrolyte intake and worsens faltering growth; gripe water has no role in CMPA. Would never be correct (Source: NHMRC).

rationales.A: Mandatory reporting requires notification to statutory child protection service (DCJ in NSW, DFFH in VIC, etc); police notification is additional not substitute. Would never be correct (Source: AIFS).
rationales.B: Any bruise in a pre-mobile infant is a red flag; TEN-4 FACES mandates investigation. Discharge without workup misses ~30 percent of subsequent severe abuse. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG; AIFS).
rationales.C: Coagulation studies are part of workup but cannot replace skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and notification. Bleeding disorders and NAI are not mutually exclusive. Would never be correct alone (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Mandatory reporting acts on reasonable belief, not certainty; waiting risks further injury and breaches legal threshold. Would never be correct (Source: AIFS).

rationales.A: GPs are appropriately trained to prescribe contraception to competent adolescents; delay exposes the patient to unintended pregnancy. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Mandating parental involvement breaches the right to confidential health care and drives patients away. Gillick competence allows confidential prescribing. Would be correct only if competence not established (Source: RACGP).
rationales.C: NSW age of consent is 16, but consensual sex between two 15-year-olds of similar maturity without exploitation/coercion does not automatically meet mandatory reporting threshold. Would be correct with significant age gap or exploitation (Source: NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).
rationales.D: Disclosure to parents without consent breaches confidentiality; reserved for risk-of-significant-harm scenarios the patient cannot/will not address. Would never be correct as routine (Source: RACGP).

rationales.A: Exclusive enteral nutrition is first-line induction for Crohn's, but is started AFTER endoscopic confirmation, not as substitute. Would be correct after diagnosis confirmed (Source: RCH CPG IBD; ESPGHAN).
rationales.B: Empirical antibiotics without endoscopy delay diagnosis and risk masking activity; stool studies are already negative. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG IBD).
rationales.C: Capsule endoscopy is for suspected isolated small bowel disease after negative ileocolonoscopy plus small bowel imaging; contraindicated where stricture risk exists. Would be correct only after negative initial endoscopy and imaging (Source: ESPGHAN).
rationales.D: MR enterography is preferred over CT in paediatric IBD to minimise lifetime radiation. Would be correct only if MR unavailable and urgent imaging needed (Source: RANZCR; eviQ).

rationales.B: Initiating GFD before biopsy in Australia is not recommended outside the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway with full criteria met. Would be correct only under specialist no-biopsy pathway with two-sample criteria (Source: ESPGHAN; RACGP).
rationales.C: Withdrawing gluten before biopsy makes diagnostic biopsy unreliable; gluten challenge is for prior inadequate evidence or diagnostic uncertainty. Would be correct only after prior GFD started without diagnosis (Source: ESPGHAN).
rationales.D: Symptomatic child with strongly positive serology, positive EMA and faltering growth must not have diagnosis delayed; treatment drives growth recovery. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP).
rationales.E: IgG anti-gliadin has been superseded by anti-tTG/EMA; faecal fat is rarely used in modern coeliac diagnosis. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP; ESPGHAN).

rationales.A: Oral NSAID monotherapy is inadequate for severe (9/10) sickle pain; strong opioid within 30 min alongside multimodal is required. Would be correct only for mild pain (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease).
rationales.B: Bolus fluids are not first-line in uncomplicated VOC and risk pulmonary oedema/ACS; maintenance fluids do not replace timely opioid. Would be correct only for confirmed hypovolaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: He is afebrile with no infective focus; empirical antibiotics are for sickle patients at 38 degC or higher. Would be correct with fever 38 or higher (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Exchange transfusion is for ACS, stroke, multi-organ failure or refractory priapism, not uncomplicated VOC. Would be correct in those indications (Source: BSH; RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Opioid titration alone misses ACS diagnosis; excess sedation worsens hypoventilation and atelectasis. Would never be correct in evolving ACS (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease).
rationales.C: Fluid boluses are contraindicated in ACS (worsen pulmonary oedema); fluids should be cut to 2/3 maintenance and treatment escalated. Would never be correct in evolving ACS (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: No wheeze or known asthma to support salbutamol as primary; ward-level observation without antibiotics or transfusion planning is inappropriate. Would be correct only in confirmed bronchospasm (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: CTPA is for genuinely suspected PE; new infiltrate, fever and hypoxia after sickle admission is diagnostic of ACS. Would be correct only with suspicion of PE not ACS (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Splenic sequestration has sudden spleen enlargement over 2 cm from baseline, brisk reticulocyte response and hypovolaemia signs; this child has unchanged spleen and SUPPRESSED reticulocytes. Would be correct with rising spleen and reticulocytosis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Hyperhaemolysis has jaundice, dark urine, raised LDH/bilirubin and brisk reticulocytosis 1-3 weeks post-transfusion; last transfusion was 4 months ago and retics suppressed. Would be correct only within 1-3 weeks of transfusion (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Iron deficiency develops over weeks/months and would not drop Hb by half in 24 h; retics in chronic IDA are typically normal not profoundly suppressed. Would be correct only in chronic gradual anaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Marrow infarction causes severe pain not asymptomatic anaemia, with leucocytosis and appropriate reticulocyte response. Would be correct only with bone pain and leucocytosis (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: IVIG is for ITP with significant mucosal bleeding or pre-procedure; cutaneous-only does not meet threshold. Would be correct with wet purpura or active mucosal bleeding (Source: RCH CPG: ITP).
rationales.C: Corticosteroids require bone marrow exam to exclude leukaemia and are for moderate bleeding, not skin-only. Would be correct after BMA excludes leukaemia and bleeding is moderate (Source: ASH 2019 ITP).
rationales.D: Platelet transfusion in ITP is rapidly consumed by autoantibodies; reserved for life-threatening bleeding such as ICH. Would be correct only in life-threatening bleeding (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: BMA is only needed if atypical features (HSM, lymphadenopathy, multi-line cytopenias, abnormal film) or before steroids; classic ITP picture does not need it. Would be correct only with atypical features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Wet purpura with prolonged active epistaxis crosses threshold for inpatient pharmacotherapy. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: ITP).
rationales.C: Transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed by anti-platelet antibodies; reserved for life-threatening bleeding (ICH, major GI). Would be correct only in life-threatening bleeding (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: TXA is a useful adjunct for mucosal bleeding but does not raise platelets; inadequate as monotherapy at this severity. Would be correct as adjunct to IVIG (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Splenectomy is for chronic refractory ITP after 12+ months of failed medical therapy. Would be correct only in refractory chronic ITP (Source: ASH 2019).

rationales.A: IVIG monotherapy is inadequate in life-threatening ICH (24-48 h to raise platelets); concurrent platelet transfusion and high-dose corticosteroid are needed. Would never be correct in ICH (Source: RCH CPG: ITP).
rationales.B: Platelet transfusion alone is rapidly consumed; IVIG and methylprednisolone are required adjuncts. Would never be correct alone in ICH (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: TXA may be cautious adjunct but is not first-line for ICH in ITP and does not raise platelets. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: ASH 2019).
rationales.E: Emergency splenectomy is not first-line response to ITP-related ICH; reserved for chronic refractory disease. Would never be correct as emergency intervention (Source: ASH 2019).

rationales.A: Ceftriaxone lacks reliable Pseudomonas cover and is not acceptable empirical monotherapy for febrile neutropenia. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.B: Misses anaerobes and does not provide reliable broad Gram-negative cover; vancomycin only added for suspected line sepsis or mucositis-related strep risk. Would be correct only as add-on after definitive cover (Source: eviQ).
rationales.D: Outpatient oral regimens are for selected low-risk patients in research protocols, not standard for high-risk ALL on intensive chemo with ANC 0.1. Would never be correct in high-risk neutropenia (Source: eviQ).
rationales.E: Meropenem is for sepsis, suspected ESBL, first-line failure or specific complicated infections, not first-line when pip-taz is suitable. Would be correct in septic shock or ESBL risk (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Antibiotic delivery within 60 min takes priority over line removal; removal is decided later with oncology. Would never be correct as first step (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Neutropenia).
rationales.C: Empirical antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging; CT for typhlitis is after stabilisation only with abdominal signs. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Pip-taz was given 8 days ago for similar episode and child is in shock with line erythema; cover should be broadened to meropenem plus vancomycin. Would be correct only on first presentation without prior exposure or line concern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: G-CSF is not part of the acute septic resuscitation bundle and has no role in the first hour. Would be correct only as later adjunct (Source: eviQ).

rationales.A: Antibiotics and watchful waiting are unsafe; blast count and pancytopenia require admission and urgent specialist review same day. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Suspected Childhood Cancer).
rationales.B: Outpatient timelines miss the critical window for managing tumour lysis and infection in suspected leukaemia; same-day transfer is required. Would never be correct (Source: Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway).
rationales.D: Iron studies do not explain pancytopenia with blasts and lymphadenopathy; iron delays diagnosis. Would be correct in isolated microcytic anaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Imaging the nodes does not change immediate management; blood film and BMA at the tertiary centre give diagnosis. Would be correct as adjunct, not deferral of referral (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Allopurinol blocks new urate but does not lower existing urate; adding potassium to fluids in established TLS is dangerous. Would be correct for prophylaxis pre-induction, not for established TLS (Source: eviQ TLS).
rationales.C: Calcium is for symptomatic hypocalcaemia or ECG instability; routine calcium can precipitate calcium phosphate crystals with high phosphate. Would be correct only with hypocalcaemic tetany/arrhythmia (Source: eviQ).
rationales.D: Urinary alkalinisation is no longer recommended (calcium phosphate precipitation, xanthine deposition); rasburicase superseded this. Would never be correct (Source: eviQ; Coiffier consensus).
rationales.E: Fluid restriction worsens TLS nephropathy; high-risk TLS needs aggressive hyperhydration with strict UO monitoring. Would never be correct (Source: eviQ).

rationales.A: Asymptomatic chronic iron deficiency at Hb 86 in stable child does not warrant transfusion; oral iron is appropriate and rapid transfusion in chronic IDA risks volume overload. Would be correct only with symptoms or severe anaemia under 70 (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency).
rationales.B: Picture is benign without lymphadenopathy/organomegaly/multi-line cytopenias; microcytic indices with low ferritin point to IDA. Would be correct with red flags (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Electrophoresis/occult blood are for unexplained or treatment-failure cases or high-risk ethnic background. Would be correct after iron trial failure (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: IV iron is for oral intolerance/failure, ongoing loss exceeding intake, or malabsorption; oral is first-line. Would be correct after oral trial failure (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Failed 3-month oral iron trial with persistently low ferritin, falling centiles and intermittent loose stools; continuing without investigation misses underlying coeliac. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Endoscopy is the test if serology positive or per no-biopsy pathway; not first investigation. Would be correct after positive serology (Source: ESPGHAN).
rationales.D: Electrophoresis is for unexplained microcytosis once iron is normal; her ferritin remains low. Would be correct only after iron repletion fails (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Meckel scan is for painless overt GI bleed; absent here. Would be correct with painless overt bleed (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Empirical antibiotics are for tender/fluctuant/rapidly enlarging nodes with bacterial features; none here, and a trial delays biopsy. Would be correct with bacterial features (Source: RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).
rationales.B: US may help characterise but does not diagnose lymphoma; 6-week observation with red flags (size, supraclavicular, B symptoms, raised LDH) is unsafe. Would never be correct with red flags (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: FNA cannot reliably diagnose/subtype lymphoma (architecture required); excisional biopsy is standard. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: eviQ).
rationales.D: Infective serology is reasonable for atypical reactive lymphadenopathy but does not justify deferring biopsy when red flags are present. Would be correct in parallel only (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Persistent candidiasis is the surface feature of likely SCID; fluconazole alone misses the underlying immunodeficiency and time-critical transplant pathway. Would never be correct (Source: ASCIA Primary Immunodeficiency).
rationales.C: Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, FTT, persistent candidiasis and severe lymphopenia together are not normal at any age; urgent specialist evaluation required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Recurrent Infections).
rationales.D: Live vaccines (MMR, rotavirus, BCG, varicella) are contraindicated in suspected SCID and cause disseminated infection. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.E: Maternal-acquired HIV antibody persists under 18 months regardless of status; HIV PCR is the test of choice. Would be correct only in older children (Source: ASHM).

rationales.A: Reduced GCS with anisocoria contraindicates immediate LP (herniation risk); CSF is deferred and antibiotics not delayed. Would be correct with normal GCS and no focal signs (Source: RCH CPG: Meningitis).
rationales.B: Antibiotics correct but dexamethasone should be given just before or with first antibiotic dose in confirmed bacterial meningitis over 3 months. Would be correct without dexamethasone indication (e.g. neonate) (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Benzylpenicillin monotherapy is inadequate in AU paediatric bacterial meningitis (pneumococcal resistance); ceftriaxone with vancomycin if local resistance is required. Would be correct only in confirmed Neisseria meningitidis with susceptible isolate (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Delaying antibiotics until after imaging/LP worsens outcomes; empirical antibiotics with dexamethasone must precede imaging when LP is deferred. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates and very young infants (bilirubin displacement, calcium incompatibility); cefotaxime is preferred. Would be correct in over-1-month infants (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.C: Benzylpenicillin doesn't cover Listeria as monotherapy; triple therapy is not standard. AU neonatal sepsis cover is ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin with cefotaxime if meningitis suspected. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Flucloxacillin plus gentamicin does not cover Listeria, GBS or many Gram-negative urinary pathogens, and lacks CSF penetration. Would be correct only in suspected staph osteomyelitis without sepsis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Any febrile infant under 3 months requires full septic workup and parenteral antibiotics until cultures negative at 36-48 h. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).

rationales.A: Suspected IMD is notifiable immediately on clinical suspicion in all AU jurisdictions; waiting for PCR delays prophylaxis. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA Invasive Meningococcal Disease SoNG).
rationales.B: Chemoprophylaxis is targeted to close contacts within 7 days before symptom onset (household, intimate kissing, childcare under 6, healthcare with significant exposure), not 'all' contacts. Would never be correct as a blanket measure (Source: CDNA).
rationales.C: Vaccination of the index case does not negate prophylaxis for close contacts; IMD is urgently notifiable (hours, not days). Would never be correct (Source: CDNA).
rationales.E: Rifampicin is second-line (compliance, drug interactions, resistance); ciprofloxacin single-dose is first-line in non-pregnant contacts. Would be correct only with quinolone contraindication (Source: CDNA).

rationales.B: Anti-NMDAR is a differential, but immunotherapy without first excluding/treating HSV is dangerous (steroids worsen untreated HSV). Would be correct only after HSV PCR negative and clinical features pathognomonic for NMDAR (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
