rationales.C: CSF is lymphocytic with normal glucose - bacterial meningitis is unlikely; HSV must be empirically covered without waiting for PCR. Would be correct only with neutrophilic CSF and low glucose (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: EEG aids diagnosis but is not a reason to delay empirical aciclovir in suspected encephalitis. Would be correct only as adjunct to confirmed treatment (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: No specific antiviral for JEV and no Murray Valley exposure described; JEV is supportive care with vaccination for prevention. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).

rationales.A: Dose too low; Therapeutic Guidelines and AMH Children's specify 25-30 mg/kg eight-hourly for paediatric CAP to cover pneumococci with reduced penicillin susceptibility. Would be correct at higher dose, not lower (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Macrolides are added for atypical infection (school-age, persistent fever, first-line failure), not monotherapy first-line for typical lobar CAP. Would be correct in atypical pneumonia presentation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Augmentin is reserved for aspiration pneumonia or amoxicillin failure; clavulanate is unnecessary and adds side effects. Would be correct for aspiration pneumonia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Cefalexin has poorer activity than amoxicillin against penicillin-non-susceptible pneumococcus; not first-line for CAP. Would be correct only with confirmed penicillin allergy and non-severe disease (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: CT is for suspected necrotising pneumonia, abscess, or non-diagnostic US or for operative planning; not first-line due to paediatric radiation. Would be correct after non-diagnostic US (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.B: Lateral decubitus films are largely obsolete; chest US is more accurate at distinguishing simple from complicated effusion. Would never be correct in modern practice (Source: RCH CPG: Pneumonia).
rationales.C: Diagnostic tap may be performed but in paediatric empyema imaging-guided assessment is first; pleural pH/biochem is not first investigation. Would be correct as adjunct, not first investigation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Bronchoscopy is for suspected foreign body, atypical organisms in immunocompromised, or persistent atelectasis. Would be correct only in those situations (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Roxithromycin is not preferred for under-1-month or first-line in pertussis; azithromycin is preferred (5-day course, better evidence base). Would be correct in older children with macrolide alternative (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: TMP-SMX is second-line only when macrolides contraindicated and not used under 2 months (kernicterus risk). Would be correct only over 2 months with macrolide contraindication (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Ceftriaxone has no role in pertussis (macrolide-sensitive organism) and avoided under 1 month. Would never be correct in pertussis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Antibiotics beyond 21 days of cough don't alter clinical course but are still given within 3 weeks to reduce transmission, especially in infants. Would never be correct in this presentation (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

rationales.A: Induration 12 mm in a non-BCG child with smear-positive household contact exceeds the 10 mm threshold; treat as LTBI without repeat. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA TB SoNG).
rationales.B: In AU, Mantoux 10 mm+ in non-BCG (or 5 mm+ in HIV/immunosuppression) is positive. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA TB SoNG).
rationales.C: IGRA has reduced sensitivity in under-5s and is not preferred sole test; Mantoux remains test of choice for young children. Would be correct in older children (Source: CDNA TB SoNG).
rationales.D: Rifampicin 4 months monotherapy is alternative but AU default is isoniazid 6-9 months with pyridoxine for paediatric LTBI. Would be correct only with INH intolerance or resistance (Source: CDNA TB SoNG).

rationales.B: Empirical PEP not appropriate when source has sustained undetectable VL (U=U); GP prescribing of PEP is discouraged in favour of specialist. Would be correct only with detectable source VL (Source: ASHM PEP Guidelines).
rationales.C: Gillick-competent adolescent consents to confidential HIV testing/treatment without parental involvement; mandatory disclosure is incorrect. Would never be correct (Source: RACGP).
rationales.D: Antibody-only tests miss the window period; 4th-gen Ag/Ab assay detects p24 earlier. Would never be correct as sole test (Source: ASHM).
rationales.E: PEP remains available across Australia (tenofovir-emtricitabine plus third agent, 28 days, within 72 h), just not indicated in U=U scenarios. Would never be correct (Source: ASHM PEP).

rationales.A: Acute haemolysis raises reticulocytes, bilirubin and LDH; here retics are markedly reduced and LDH/bili are baseline. Would be correct with rising retics and bilirubin (Source: RCH CPG: SCD).
rationales.B: Sequestration causes rapid splenic enlargement and Hb drop in young children; this child has no splenomegaly and HbSS spleens typically autoinfarct by school age. Would be correct with rising spleen in younger child (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: IDA is microcytic and develops over weeks; her MCV is 86 and Hb dropped acutely. Would be correct only with chronic gradual microcytic anaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: ACS requires new pulmonary infiltrate with fever/chest pain/hypoxia; clear chest, no chest pain, no fever, SpO2 98 percent. Would be correct only with those features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: She is over 9 months and MMR within 72 h is preferred PEP; NHIG/IVIG is for under 9 months, pregnant susceptibles, and significantly immunocompromised. Would be correct only under 9 months (Source: CDNA Measles SoNG; NCIRS).
rationales.C: Susceptible exposed contacts should be offered PEP (MMR within 72 h or NHIG/IVIG within 6 days); waiting for serology delays effective prophylaxis. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA).
rationales.D: No specific antiviral for measles; supportive care plus vitamin A in selected cases. Would never be correct (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.E: She is already 13 months, maternal antibody waned, and she is unvaccinated; she needs PEP. Would be correct only in younger infant with maternal immunity (Source: NCIRS).

rationales.A: Varicella vaccine is live and contraindicated in significantly immunocompromised patients on chemo. Would be correct only in immunocompetent contacts within 5 days (Source: NCIRS Varicella Vaccine).
rationales.B: Prophylactic oral aciclovir from day 7 post-exposure is an alternative when VZIG unavailable; VZIG preferred in significantly immunocompromised. Would be correct as alternative when VZIG unavailable (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.C: Immediate aciclovir treats established varicella, not pre-symptomatic prophylaxis; VZIG is preferred PEP in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Would be correct only with established lesions (Source: NCIRS).
rationales.D: Observation alone misses the window for passive immunisation; VZIG within 96 h (up to 10 days) attenuates disease. Would never be correct in significantly immunocompromised contact (Source: NCIRS).

rationales.B: AU dose for AOM when antibiotics indicated is amoxicillin 30 mg/kg eight-hourly for 5 days, not 15 mg/kg; and antibiotics are not first-line in low-risk children. Would be correct only at correct dose for high-risk indication (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Augmentin is for amoxicillin failure, recent amoxicillin use, or recurrent AOM, not first uncomplicated episode. Would be correct only with prior amoxicillin failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Cefaclor has poor activity against penicillin-non-susceptible pneumococcus and risks serum-sickness-like reactions; not first-line. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Topical quinolone drops are for AOM with perforation, otorrhoea through grommet, or chronic suppurative OM; TM intact here. Would be correct only with perforation/grommet/CSOM (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Oral and outpatient are for uncomplicated preseptal cellulitis without orbital involvement; painful eye movements, ophthalmoplegia, proptosis or vision change mark orbital cellulitis. Would be correct only in preseptal cellulitis without orbital signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Flucloxacillin monotherapy does not cover sinus pathogens (pneumococcus, Strep anginosus, anaerobes) and Haemophilus driving orbital cellulitis. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Imaging required but antibiotics must not be delayed; CT sinuses/orbits with contrast is the standard urgent imaging, not MRI (more rapidly available, identifies subperiosteal collection). Would be correct as adjunct, not delaying antibiotics (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Allergic eyelid swelling is bilateral, itchy, non-tender, with no vision/movement effect; this is bacterial orbital cellulitis. Would be correct only in allergic angioedema with bilateral involvement (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Cefepime monotherapy acceptable for stable febrile neutropenia but patient is in shock with prolonged neutropenia; AU paediatric guidance adds aminoglycoside. Would be correct only in stable febrile neutropenia (Source: eviQ; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Ceftriaxone does not cover Pseudomonas; antipseudomonal cover is mandatory in febrile neutropenia. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Meropenem is for second-line: ESBL colonisation, severe sepsis not responding to first-line, or significant beta-lactam allergy. Would be correct only in those specific indications (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Antibiotics must be given within 60 min (ideally 30 min in septic shock); each hour of delay increases mortality. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Neutropenia).

rationales.A: All four Kocher criteria met giving ~99 percent probability of septic arthritis; discharge with analgesia risks irreversible cartilage damage. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Limp).
rationales.B: Withholding antibiotics and aspiration in 4-of-4 Kocher risks irreversible cartilage and femoral head damage; time-to-washout correlates with outcome. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Oral therapy and outpatient are inappropriate for high-probability septic arthritis of the hip; IV after aspiration is mandated and oral switch is only after cultures known. Would be correct only as step-down after IV course (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: MRI has role with suspected osteomyelitis after equivocal initial assessment but should not delay aspiration and antibiotics in 4-of-4 Kocher patient with confirmed effusion. Would be correct only with equivocal Kocher and effusion uncertainty (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Obligate external rotation on hip flexion and widened physis are pathognomonic of SUFE; delay risks stable-to-unstable slip with much higher AVN risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE).
rationales.B: Continued weight-bearing risks progression to unstable slip; child must be made non-weight-bearing and referred urgently. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Perthes is 4-8 years with femoral head fragmentation, not physeal widening; this 12-year-old with raised BMI and physeal widening has SUFE. Would be correct in 4-8-year-old with classic Perthes XR (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Referred knee pain is classic for hip pathology in adolescents; hip exam and pelvic XR have localised the lesion. Would never be correct as next step (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: MRI/orthopaedics are for atypical (unilateral, nocturnal pain, locking, persistent past skeletal maturity); typical bilateral apophysitis does not need them. Would be correct with atypical features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Casting is not recommended for Osgood-Schlatter and causes deconditioning; relative rest preserves strength and growth. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Intralesional steroid near an open apophysis risks soft tissue atrophy, depigmentation, growth disturbance. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Plain films not routine for typical Osgood-Schlatter; tuberosity fragmentation is a normal variant. Would be correct only with atypical features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Cobb 28 degrees in skeletally immature girl (Risser 2, recent menarche) has significant progression risk and needs specialist follow-up. Would be correct only in skeletally mature patient with stable Cobb under 25 (Source: AAOS Scoliosis Guidelines).
rationales.B: 20-40 degree curves in skeletally immature meet threshold for orthopaedic referral and possible bracing; surveillance alone risks missing progression. Would be correct only in skeletally mature with stable Cobb under 25 (Source: AAOS).
rationales.D: Surgery is for Cobb over 45-50 degrees that continues to progress, not 28; bracing is first-line at this Cobb. Would be correct at Cobb over 45-50 with progression (Source: AAOS).
rationales.E: MRI is for atypical features (left thoracic, rapid progression, neuro signs, painful, juvenile under 10, cutaneous stigmata); right thoracic curve in adolescent is typical adolescent idiopathic scoliosis. Would be correct with atypical features (Source: AAOS).

rationales.A: Positive Ortolani is a clinically dislocated reducible hip, not a benign click; same-admission orthopaedic referral is mandatory. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DDH).
rationales.B: Plain XR is unreliable before 4-6 months (femoral heads not ossified); US is the neonatal imaging modality. Would be correct in older infant over 4-6 months (Source: RCH CPG: DDH).
rationales.D: Double-nappying is not evidence-based and delays definitive Pavlik harness in a frankly dislocated hip. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: 6-week screening is for at-risk infants with normal clinical exam; positive Ortolani requires immediate referral. Would be correct only with risk factors and normal exam (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: US is best up to 4-6 months while femoral heads are unossified; by 8 months the ossific nucleus obscures landmarks. Would be correct only under 4-6 months (Source: RCH CPG: DDH).
rationales.B: MRI is for postoperative assessment or atypical/complex cases; not first-line for suspected late-presenting DDH. Would be correct in postop assessment (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: CT carries unnecessary radiation and is for surgical planning in older children with complex deformity. Would be correct in operative planning (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Bone scintigraphy has no routine role in DDH; it is for osteomyelitis or occult bone pathology. Would never be correct in suspected DDH (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Kocher 0/4 gives ~2 percent probability of septic arthritis; clinically very unlikely. Would be correct with elevated Kocher score (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Limp).
rationales.C: Perthes is 4-8 years with insidious painless limp and characteristic XR (subchondral fracture, fragmentation); at age 3 with acute post-URTI onset, it's unlikely. Would be correct in 4-8-year-old with XR changes (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: SUFE is adolescent (10-15 years, often overweight, peripubertal); rare in 3-year-old without endocrine predisposition. Would be correct in adolescent (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: ALL presents with night pain, systemic symptoms, cytopenias and disproportionately raised inflammatory markers; this child has benign profile. Would be correct with red flags (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Watchful waiting is for well low-risk non-Indigenous children over 2 years; Aboriginal children have markedly elevated risk of chronic suppurative OM and require antibiotic treatment. Would be correct only in non-Indigenous low-risk child (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Oral cephalosporins are not preferred first-line for AOM in AU; amoxicillin has adequate pneumococcal cover and 3-day course is insufficient for high-risk children. Would be correct as second-line with adequate duration (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Topical quinolone drops are for AOM with discharge through perforation/grommet or CSOM, not intact-TM AOM. Would be correct only with perforation/grommet/CSOM (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: IM benzathine benzylpenicillin is for ARF secondary prophylaxis and GAS syndromes, not first-line AOM. Would be correct only in ARF prophylaxis indication (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Acute mastoiditis with fluctuant subperiosteal abscess; oral therapy is inadequate, urgent IV antibiotics, imaging and ENT input are mandatory. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Mastoiditis).
rationales.B: Flucloxacillin does not adequately cover pneumococcus and other AOM pathogens driving mastoiditis; broader cover and urgent ENT input are required. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: No signs of meningitis (no neck stiffness/photophobia/altered GCS); LP not required. Intracranial complications excluded by CT. Would be correct only with meningitis signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Imaging required but must not delay IV antibiotics and ENT; CT temporal bones with contrast is first-line (rapidly available, characterises bone). Would be correct only as adjunct, not delaying treatment (Source: RANZCR).

rationales.A: OME is not acute infection and routine antibiotics are not recommended; do not change long-term outcomes. Would never be correct in OME (Source: RCH CPG: Otitis Media with Effusion).
rationales.B: Intranasal steroids and antihistamines not recommended for OME (no meaningful improvement in hearing/effusion). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Bilateral OME over 3 months with hearing loss over 25 dB and language/behaviour impact crosses threshold for ENT referral. Would be correct only without hearing loss or language impact (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Hearing aids are alternative when surgery contraindicated or declined; grommets remain first-line surgical management. Would be correct only with surgical contraindication (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: JNA affects adolescent males with unilateral nasal obstruction and severe recurrent epistaxis with mass; digit-trauma anterior bleeding in 6-year-old is not the JNA picture. Would be correct in adolescent male with mass (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks septal perforation; cautery is unilateral and second-line after failed conservative therapy. Would be correct as unilateral second-line (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Packing is uncomfortable in children and is for ongoing bleeding not controlled by first aid; not appropriate as outpatient management. Would be correct only with active uncontrolled bleeding in ED (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Oral TXA has no role as first-line for mucosal-trauma epistaxis with normal coagulation. Would be correct only as second-line adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: First branchial cleft anomalies are rare with peri-auricular sinus tracts related to facial nerve; typical pre-auricular pit at the helical root is not this. Would be correct only with first branchial cleft anomaly anatomy (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: An infected pre-auricular sinus requires antibiotic treatment and ENT referral for definitive excision; observation alone risks abscess progression. Would be correct only for asymptomatic non-discharging pit (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Squeezing spreads infection and damages adjacent tissues; systemic antibiotics with surgical drainage if needed is appropriate. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Operating on acutely inflamed sinus increases recurrence (obscured planes); excision is deferred until acute infection settled. Would be correct after acute infection resolved (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Paradise criteria require 7 episodes in 12 months, 5/year for 2 years, or 3/year for 3 years; 4 episodes in 12 months does not meet criteria. Would be correct only when Paradise criteria met (Source: ENT Society Australia).
rationales.C: Long-term penicillin prophylaxis is for ARF secondary prevention, not recurrent bacterial tonsillitis without ARF. Would be correct only with ARF indication (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Asymptomatic GAS carriage is common in children and does not warrant treatment; swabs not indicated in asymptomatic children outside ARF-prevention. Would be correct only in symptomatic episode or ARF prevention (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: 4 tonsillitis episodes in a well-grown child does not meet criteria for immunodeficiency workup; referral threshold is recurrent serious infections at multiple sites. Would be correct with multi-site recurrent serious infections (Source: ASCIA).

rationales.A: Leukocoria is never normal and mandates same-day to next-day paediatric ophthalmology to exclude retinoblastoma. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Leukocoria).
rationales.B: Optometry deferral risks unacceptable delay; retinoblastoma is curable when caught early but devastating when missed. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: MRI is for staging once retinoblastoma is diagnosed; first step is paediatric ophthalmology EUA, not pre-emptive imaging. Would be correct only after EUA-confirmed diagnosis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Patching is for confirmed amblyopia AFTER the underlying lesion has been identified and treated; occluding now could delay diagnosis. Would never be correct as first step (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Topical chloramphenicol alone is inadequate for gonococcal ophthalmia (corneal penetration, disseminated infection risk); systemic therapy required. Would never be correct in gonococcal ophthalmia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Chlamydial conjunctivitis presents at 5-14 days with mucopurulent discharge and less severe chemosis; this hyperacute first-week purulent presentation is gonococcal. Would be correct in chlamydial pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Chemical conjunctivitis is in first 24-48 h, mild and self-limiting; silver nitrate is not used in Australia. Would never be correct in this presentation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Neonatal HSV presents with vesicular skin/keratitis with dendritic ulcer/systemic illness, treated with IV high-dose aciclovir not topical. Would be correct only with HSV-specific findings (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Admission, IV broad-spectrum and contrast CT are for orbital (postseptal) cellulitis: pain on eye movement, restricted motility, proptosis, reduced acuity or RAPD; absent here. Would be correct only with orbital signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Topical and warm compresses do not penetrate the lid soft tissues adequately for cellulitis; systemic antibiotics required. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: No fluctuant collection described; preseptal cellulitis without abscess is managed medically. Would be correct only with palpable fluctuant abscess (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Systemic steroids not indicated for periorbital cellulitis and may obscure progression to orbital cellulitis. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: No true strabismus on Hirschberg or cover-uncover; this is pseudoesotropia and surgery is not indicated. Would never be correct in pseudoesotropia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Patching is for confirmed amblyopia after specialist diagnosis; occluding a child without amblyopia is harmful. Would never be correct without confirmed amblyopia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Atropine penalisation is for confirmed amblyopia; no role with normal vision and no strabismus. Would be correct only with diagnosed amblyopia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: 6th nerve palsy gives limited abduction and incomitant esotropia; full eye movements and negative cover-uncover exclude this. Would be correct only with limited abduction (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: 20 mL/kg boluses now discouraged in favour of 10 mL/kg with reassessment (fluid overload risk); vancomycin alone misses Gram-negatives in septic arthritis. Would be correct in some adult/older child protocols at 20 mL/kg (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.C: No evidence of anaemia or blood loss; crystalloid is first-line. MRI may complement but must not delay antibiotics and source control. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Shock and confirmed septic arthritis - observation delays time to antibiotics and source control. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Fluid resuscitation must not be deferred; ceftriaxone alone has gaps for severe staphylococcal infection and is not preferred monotherapy. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis lacks evidence of carditis and has additive (not migratory) pattern; child has objective carditis on echo. Would be correct only without carditis and additive arthritis (Source: RHDAustralia 2020 Jones).
rationales.C: JIA requires 6 weeks of persistent arthritis and is not associated with new pansystolic mitral murmur and elevated strep serology. Would be correct only with 6+ weeks chronic arthritis (Source: ACR).
rationales.D: HSP requires palpable purpura on dependent areas plus arthritis/abdominal pain/renal involvement; absent here. Would be correct with palpable purpura and IgA features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Indigenous Lyme disease is not established in Australia; no relevant travel history, migratory polyarthritis plus carditis plus positive strep fits ARF. Would never be correct in Australia (Source: NHMRC Lyme statement).

rationales.A: Severe recurrent unilateral epistaxis with nasal obstruction and visible posterior mass must not be treated as benign anterior bleeding. Would be correct only in true anterior Little area bleeding (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: JNA is exceptionally vascular and clinic biopsy can cause life-threatening haemorrhage; imaging precedes biopsy and biopsy done under GA at theatre. Would never be correct (Source: ENT Society Australia).
rationales.D: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks perforation and does not address posterior nasopharyngeal mass. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: TXA is an adjunct only; does not substitute for ENT assessment and imaging of suspected vascular tumour. Would never be correct (Source: ENT Society Australia).

rationales.A: SBR 340 micromol/L at 72 h in a term baby crosses the AU phototherapy threshold on the postnatal nomogram; conservative monitoring risks rising levels. Would never be correct above threshold (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice).
rationales.B: Routine cessation of breastfeeding is not recommended; continuing feeds with phototherapy is preferred. Would never be correct as routine (Source: RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Exchange transfusion is for SBR approaching 450 micromol/L despite intensive phototherapy or for acute bilirubin encephalopathy. Would be correct at SBR approaching 450 or with neuro signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Conjugated fraction 8 is normal (under 20 percent of total or under 25 micromol/L) and does not indicate cholestatic workup. Would be correct only with elevated conjugated fraction (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: BSL 1.8 is below operational threshold 2.6 for at-risk infants and requires active intervention. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Hypoglycaemia).
rationales.C: Enteral feeding is for BSL 2.0-2.6 in asymptomatic at-risk infants; below 2.0 requires IV dextrose. Would be correct at BSL 2.0-2.6 (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Glucagon is for refractory hypoglycaemia where IV access delayed/impossible and requires hepatic glycogen stores; not first-line in late-preterm IDM with IV access. Would be correct only with delayed IV access (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Buccal dextrose gel plus feeding is for BSL 2.0-2.6 in asymptomatic infants, not below 2.0; recheck always required. Would be correct at BSL 2.0-2.6 (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Routine tracheal suction of vigorous meconium-stained newborns is no longer recommended and may delay resuscitation. Would be correct only in non-vigorous newborn with airway obstruction (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).
rationales.C: PPV is indicated only with apnoea, gasping, or HR under 100, none of which apply. Would be correct in those situations (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).
rationales.D: Intubation is for ineffective bag-mask or specific indications; prophylactic CPAP/intubation not indicated in vigorous term newborn. Would be correct only with failed PPV (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).
rationales.E: Intrapartum oro/nasopharyngeal suctioning on the perineum is no longer recommended and does not reduce MAS. Would never be correct (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

rationales.A: Continuing feeds in confirmed NEC (Bell IIA with pneumatosis) is contraindicated and risks progression to perforation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: NEC).
rationales.B: Advancing feeds is contraindicated; oral aminoglycosides not indicated for established NEC; bowel rest with parenteral broad-spectrum is standard. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Surgery is for pneumoperitoneum, fixed bowel loop, palpable mass or deterioration despite medical therapy; Bell IIA without perforation is medical with surgical review on standby. Would be correct only with perforation or deterioration (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Cefotaxime monotherapy does not adequately cover anaerobes; surgical review is mandatory at diagnosis. Would never be correct as sole therapy (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Cefotaxime monotherapy is not standard empirical cover for EOS in AU; synergistic penicillin plus aminoglycoside is preferred. Would be correct only with cefotaxime added to a penicillin-aminoglycoside backbone with meningitis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Flucloxacillin targets Staph aureus and is for late-onset sepsis with line infection, not first-line cover for EOGBS and E. coli. Would be correct for late-onset with line sepsis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Vanc plus cefotaxime is for late-onset hospital-acquired sepsis with meningitis suspicion or MRSA risk; not standard for early-onset community-presenting. Would be correct in late-onset (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Intrapartum cefazolin does not exclude sepsis with clinical deterioration; empirical antibiotics must be started. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Symptomatic congenital CMV warrants prompt antiviral; delaying to 12 months misses the window when valganciclovir improves outcomes. Would never be correct in symptomatic disease (Source: RCH CPG: Congenital CMV).
rationales.C: Six weeks IV ganciclovir was older regimen, superseded by 6 months oral valganciclovir (non-inferior, better neurodevelopmental/audiological). Would be correct only if oral not tolerated (Source: AAP Red Book).
rationales.D: CMV hyperimmune globulin is not standard postnatal therapy for symptomatic congenital CMV; oral valganciclovir is recommended. Would never be correct postnatally (Source: AAP Red Book).
rationales.E: Hearing rehab is part of care but does not replace antiviral, which improves hearing trajectories. Would never be correct alone (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Single-dose IM benzathine is for infants with normal examination/investigations and adequately treated mothers; this infant has proven congenital syphilis. Would be correct only with normal exam/labs and treated mother (Source: Australian STI Guidelines).
rationales.B: Azithromycin is not recommended for congenital syphilis (resistance, CNS penetration); parenteral penicillin is only acceptable treatment. Would never be correct (Source: Australian STI Guidelines).
rationales.C: Treatment should not be delayed pending all confirmatory tests when clinical/serological/radiological findings establish probable congenital syphilis. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone has limited data in neonatal congenital syphilis and is not first-line; penicillin desensitisation should be performed if true allergy. Would be correct only with documented severe penicillin allergy after desensitisation failure (Source: Australian STI Guidelines).

rationales.A: Breastfeeding is encouraged in stable methadone-maintained mothers and reduces NAS severity; switching to formula is not indicated. Would never be correct as routine (Source: RCH CPG: NAS).
rationales.B: Phenobarbital is adjunctive for polysubstance exposure or refractory NAS, not first-line monotherapy for opioid NAS where opioids are the agent of choice. Would be correct as adjunct in polysubstance NAS (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Naloxone is contraindicated in babies of opioid-dependent mothers (precipitates severe acute withdrawal/seizures). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Persistent Finnegan 8+ requires inpatient monitoring and consideration of pharmacotherapy, not community follow-up. Would be correct only with Finnegan under 8 (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Passive cooling to 35.5 degC does not provide target therapeutic range; servo-controlled cooling to 33-34 is required. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: ANZNN Cooling Protocol).
rationales.B: Normothermia is not the goal in confirmed moderate/severe HIE within 6 h; therapeutic hypothermia is evidence-based neuroprotection. Would never be correct (Source: ANZNN).
rationales.D: Selective head cooling is alternative but duration is 72 h not 48, and AU/NZ units predominantly use whole-body cooling. Would be correct only with selective head cooling protocol at correct duration (Source: ANZNN).
rationales.E: Cooling must start within 6 h to be neuroprotective; waiting for MRI misses the window. Would never be correct (Source: Cochrane HIE).

rationales.A: PGE1 is for duct-dependent CHD; structurally normal heart with PPHN does not benefit and PGE1 can worsen pulmonary congestion. Would be correct in duct-dependent CHD (Source: RCH CPG: PPHN).
rationales.C: HFOV is useful adjunct to recruit atelectatic lung in MAS and may combine with iNO, but does not replace pulmonary vasodilator therapy in established PPHN. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: ECMO is for refractory PPHN with OI persistently over 25-40 despite iNO and HFOV; bypassing iNO at OI 22 is premature. Would be correct at OI over 25-40 despite optimised therapy (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Oral sildenafil is adjunctive or weaning therapy after iNO; not first-line in acute severe PPHN. Would be correct as weaning therapy (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.B: Failed hyperoxia test indicates fixed right-to-left shunt; continued oxygen alone is inadequate and risks collapse if duct closes. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Cyanotic CHD).
rationales.C: iNO is for PPHN with right-to-left ductal shunting on echo and structurally normal heart; presumptive PPHN before echo may worsen ductal-dependent CHD. Would be correct after echo confirms PPHN (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Fluid bolus does not address the underlying shunt physiology; delaying echo risks ductal closure. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Closing duct in duct-dependent lesion is catastrophic; indomethacin is for haemodynamically significant PDA in preterms without duct-dependent CHD. Would be correct only in haemodynamically significant PDA without ductal-dependency (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Diazepam is not first-line in neonatal seizures and rectal route is unreliable; phenobarbitone IV gives sustained levels and proven efficacy. Would never be correct in neonates (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures).
rationales.B: Ionised calcium 1.25 is within normal range; empirical calcium is not indicated and can cause arrhythmia if extravasated. Would be correct only with documented hypocalcaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Pyridoxine is for refractory neonatal seizures unresponsive to standard antiepileptics; should not delay phenobarbitone. Would be correct as second-line after phenobarbitone failure (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: This baby does not meet cooling criteria (no perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia, normal cord gas, normal APGARs). Would be correct only with HIE features (Source: ANZNN).

rationales.A: AU threshold for surfactant in preterm RDS is sustained FiO2 over 0.3 (under 28 weeks) or 0.3-0.4 (28-32 weeks); ignoring 0.35 in 32-weeker risks worsening RDS. Would be correct only with FiO2 under threshold (Source: RCH CPG: Surfactant).
rationales.B: Prolonged ventilation after surfactant increases BPD risk in spontaneously breathing preterm; less invasive strategy preferred. Would be correct only in apnoea or failed CPAP (Source: ANZNN).
rationales.C: Postnatal corticosteroids in this context are inappropriate; antenatal steroids must be given to mother pre-delivery, and postnatal dexamethasone is for ventilator-dependent infants at risk of BPD. Would never be correct here (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Nebulised surfactant remains investigational and not recommended outside trials in AU; tracheal delivery is required for proven efficacy. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: MMR is not given under 6 months (poorly immunogenic due to maternal antibody); infants under 6 months exposed to measles should receive NHIG, but the option as written specifies MMR which is wrong. Would be correct if NHIG were specified for the 5-month-old (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.B: MMR five days before exposure is too recent for protective immunity but a repeat dose within 72 h may still help; NHIG is not first-line here. Would be correct as repeat MMR for that contact (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.D: MMR is contraindicated in severely immunocompromised including those on induction chemo; this child should receive NHIG. Option as written is incorrect. Would be correct if NHIG were specified (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.E: Two documented MMR doses confer presumptive immunity; no further intervention/booster required. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

rationales.A: Varicella vaccine is live attenuated and CONTRAINDICATED in severely immunocompromised including those on induction chemo. Would be correct only in immunocompetent contacts (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.C: Antiviral prophylaxis (days 7-14) is adjunct or alternative when ZIG unavailable; ZIG is preferred first-line. Would be correct when ZIG unavailable (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.D: Waiting for the rash forgoes the proven benefit of ZIG given before viraemia; disseminated varicella in immunocompromised carries significant mortality. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.E: Pre-treatment varicella IgG was negative documenting susceptibility; waiting for repeat serology delays ZIG and reduces effectiveness. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

rationales.A: Sequestration causes sudden anaemia with tender enlarging spleen, typically before autoinfarction; no splenomegaly here. Would be correct in younger child with rising spleen (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Hyperhaemolysis has markedly raised LDH/bilirubin with HIGH reticulocytes; reticulocytes are profoundly suppressed at 0.2 percent here. Would be correct only with reticulocytosis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: VOC has pain (chest/abdomen/limbs), absent here, and would not produce reticulocytopenia. Would be correct with pain crisis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: IDA develops over weeks/months, does not drop Hb by 33 g/L in days, and does not cause dramatic reticulocyte suppression. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: HSP is a clinical diagnosis in classical presentations; biopsy is for atypical cases. Would be correct only with atypical features (Source: RCH CPG: HSP).
rationales.B: Afebrile, alert, with classic HSP morphology; meningococcaemia begins on trunk with rapid spread and shock signs. Would be correct only with meningococcal features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Prophylactic steroids do NOT prevent HSP nephritis; may be used for severe abdominal pain or arthritis. Would be correct only with severe abdominal pain/arthritis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Renal biopsy is for nephrotic-range proteinuria, persistent significant proteinuria, hypertension or rising creatinine. Would be correct with those features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: LP is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability, coagulopathy, or rapidly evolving purpura; CT-then-LP delays the only intervention that changes outcome. Would be correct only in stable patient with normal coags (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Delaying antibiotics in suspected meningococcal sepsis worsens mortality; cultures drawn only if access rapid, antibiotics must NOT wait. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Routine steroids are not the immediate priority and do not replace urgent antibiotics; stress-dose steroids may be considered later for refractory shock. Would be correct only later as adjunct in refractory shock (Source: ANZICS).
rationales.E: Even fully immunised children can develop IMD from non-vaccine strains or vaccine failure; the picture mandates immediate empirical therapy. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA IMD SoNG).

rationales.B: Scarlet fever has sandpaper rash with flexural accentuation, not polymorphous rash with extremity changes; duration over 5 days, conjunctivitis, and oedematous hands point to Kawasaki. Would be correct in scarlet fever pattern (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: No neck stiffness, no altered conscious state; systemic mucocutaneous features are inconsistent with bacterial meningitis. Would be correct only with meningitis features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Child with 5 days fever and these mucocutaneous findings cannot be discharged; delayed treatment increases coronary aneurysm risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).
rationales.E: sJIA has quotidian fevers, salmon-pink evanescent rash and arthritis; mucocutaneous picture is Kawasaki. Steroid monotherapy without IVIG is wrong. Would be correct only with sJIA features (Source: ACR).

rationales.A: Localised application misses unseen mites and is a common failure cause; whole-body (neck-down over 2 years) with 7-day repeat and concurrent contact treatment is required. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: New distribution (finger webs, wrists, peri-umbilical, genitalia) and family contagion are not eczema; topical steroid will not eradicate the infestation. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Contact dermatitis follows the shape of contact (e.g. metal stud); no burrows, peri-umbilical/genital involvement or family clustering. Would be correct only with contact-shaped pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: No honey-coloured crusts or pustules to suggest impetigo; distribution does not fit secondary infection of usual eczema sites. Would be correct only with impetiginised features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Pityriasis rosea management; mycology positive here excludes pityriasis. Would be correct only with mycology-negative herald-patch-Christmas-tree pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Oral antifungals are for extensive disease, scalp/hair (tinea capitis), nails, or topical failure; not for limited tinea corporis. Would be correct with extensive or scalp involvement (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Topical steroid on tinea (tinea incognito) reduces inflammation transiently but worsens fungal growth and atypical spread; antihistamines do not treat infection. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Microscopy confirms fungal cause; antibacterial therapy inappropriate. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: SSSS affects younger children (under 5), spares mucosae, no target lesions or drug history; carbamazepine is also an aromatic anticonvulsant that cross-reacts with lamotrigine in SCAR. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: EM has three-zone target lesions, acral, usually post-HSV; this case is dusky macules with sloughing and positive Nikolsky pointing to SJS. Half-dose lamotrigine continues the offending drug. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: HSV-associated EM has classic targets and minimal-to-modest mucosal involvement; diffuse dusky macules with bullae and Nikolsky in a child on a high-risk drug fits SJS. Would be correct only in HSV-associated EM (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: SJS has 5-15 percent mortality with delayed care and sight-threatening ocular sequelae; outpatient management is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Imaging must NOT delay surgical exploration in suspected nec fasc; diagnosis is surgical and operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Atypical KD can mimic but pain disproportionate to skin findings, lactic acidosis, AKI, dusky soft tissue over healing varicella point to invasive bacterial supra-infection. Would be correct only with KD-specific features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Disseminated varicella in immunocompetent child is rare; focal painful thigh with anaesthesia and toxin-mediated picture point to bacterial supra-infection. Would be correct only with disseminated VZV (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Scarlet fever has sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue, not shock/lactic acidosis/AKI/pain disproportionate to skin. Would be correct in scarlet fever pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Bag specimens have ~60 percent contamination and Kass threshold cannot be applied; a positive bag must be confirmed. Would never be confirmatory; only useful as screening to be confirmed (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).
rationales.B: Catheter is for unwell infants needing immediate antibiotics or after failed clean catch; invasive and not first-line in well child whose mother can wait. Would be correct only in septic infant or failed clean catch (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: SPA is gold standard but invasive/painful and reserved for failed less-invasive collection or immediate sterile sample in septic infant. Would be correct in septic infant when other methods fail (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Nappy pad/cotton wool is unreliable for culture (contamination, cell lysis); not recommended for confirmatory specimens. Would never be confirmatory (Source: NICE NG224).

rationales.A: Outpatient oral is not appropriate in under-3-months with culture-suggestive UTI (high bacteraemia/meningitis risk). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).
rationales.B: Antibiotics must not be withheld pending formal culture once urine microscopy is positive in this age group. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Short stay with oral does not meet under-3-months pathway; IV plus LP are mandated regardless of clinical appearance. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: LP cannot be deferred in under-3-months with proven UTI (bacteraemia/meningitis risk), and ceftriaxone monotherapy omits Listeria cover. Would be correct only in over-3-months with selective workup (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: US is poor for detecting cortical scarring and low-grade VUR, both of which change long-term management after recurrent/atypical UTI. Would be correct only as initial screen (Source: NICE NG224).
rationales.C: MCUG is invasive (catheterisation, contrast); reserved for under-6-months, atypical/recurrent UTI with abnormal US, or family history of VUR. Would be correct only in those specific groups (Source: KHA-CARI).
rationales.D: MAG3 evaluates differential function and drainage in suspected obstruction; her US shows no hydronephrosis. Would be correct only with hydronephrosis/obstruction suspicion (Source: NICE NG224).
rationales.E: CT delivers high radiation and is not first-line for scarring in children; DMSA gives functional cortical information with lower radiation. Would never be correct first-line (Source: RANZCR).

rationales.A: Bag urine cannot reliably exclude UTI: nitrites require 4+ h bladder dwell (often negative in frequent voiders) and not all uropathogens reduce nitrates. Would never be correct (Source: NICE NG224; RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Empirical antibiotics should not be started on bag urine alone; reliable confirmatory sample is required first. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Negative nitrites do not exclude UTI in infants (short bladder dwell, non-nitrate-reducing uropathogens). Would never be correct (Source: NICE NG224).
rationales.D: Bag samples have ~60 percent contamination and do not meet Kass criteria; bag-urine culture cannot reliably exclude infection. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Postrenal AKI requires sonographic evidence of obstruction (hydronephrosis, distended bladder); US is normal and bladder fills appropriately. Would be correct with hydronephrosis (Source: RCH CPG: AKI).
rationales.B: ATN typically has FENa over 2 percent, urine osmolality near plasma, and muddy brown granular casts; this child's urine is concentrated with FENa 0.4 percent. Would be correct with FENa over 2 and casts (Source: KDIGO 2012).
rationales.D: AIN typically has pyuria, WBC casts, eosinophiluria and drug history; sediment bland and no offending medication. Would be correct with drug history and eosinophiluria (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.E: Glomerulonephritis has haematuria, RBC casts and proteinuria, often hypertension; sediment bland and no proteinuria/HTN. Would be correct with nephritic sediment (Source: KDIGO 2021).

rationales.A: Definitive valve ablation is eventual treatment but only after resuscitation and bladder drainage; immediate priority is decompression. Would be correct only after stabilisation (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology).
rationales.B: Diuretics are contraindicated in postrenal AKI with downstream obstruction (worsens upper tract pressure) and won't lower K until obstruction relieved. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2012).
rationales.D: Nephrostomy is for failed bladder drainage, ureteric obstruction or supravesical pathology; bladder catheterisation is first-line in PUV. Would be correct only after failed bladder drainage (Source: EAU Paediatric).
rationales.E: Hyperkalaemia stabilisation is appropriate adjunctively but does not address the obstructive cause; without bladder drainage AKI and acidosis won't resolve. Would be correct as adjunct alongside catheter (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: First-presentation typical nephrotic in 1-12 years does not require biopsy; biopsy is for atypical features (age, haematuria, hypertension). Would be correct with atypical features (Source: KDIGO 2021 Nephrotic Syndrome).
rationales.B: Salt restriction is supportive but disease-modifying treatment with corticosteroids is required; furosemide alone risks worsening hypovolaemia. Would never be correct as primary (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.C: IV methylprednisolone pulses and cytotoxic agents are for steroid-resistant or frequently relapsing disease, not first presentation. Would be correct in steroid-resistant disease (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.E: ACEi and statins are not first-line in steroid-sensitive paediatric NS, and routine LMWH is not recommended unless additional thrombotic risk. Would be correct with thrombotic risk factors (Source: KDIGO 2021).

rationales.A: Failure to remit after 4-6 weeks daily prednisolone defines steroid resistance; prolonging exposes the child to side effects without benefit. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.B: Calcineurin inhibitors are second-line but should be initiated AFTER biopsy and genetic results, since monogenic causes respond poorly. Would be correct after biopsy and negative genetic panel (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.D: Rituximab is for frequently-relapsing/steroid-dependent and selected SRNS, only after biopsy and confirmation of histology/genetic status. Would be correct after biopsy in selected cases (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.E: ACEi is adjunctive antiproteinuric in SRNS but immunosuppression cannot be ceased abruptly without confirmed histology. Would be correct as adjunct, not as primary withdrawal (Source: KDIGO 2021).

rationales.B: Macroscopic haematuria with dysmorphic cells and RBC casts is shared across all three glomerulonephritides and does not discriminate. Would never be discriminating (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.C: Synpharyngitic haematuria (1-2 days post-pharyngitis) is the hallmark of IgA nephropathy; PSGN has 1-3 week latency. Would be correct for IgA nephropathy (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.D: Palpable purpura with arthralgia is defining for IgA vasculitis (HSP), not PSGN. Would be correct for HSP (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Elevated serum IgA supports IgA nephropathy not PSGN; PSGN is characterised by streptococcal markers and complement consumption. Would be correct only for IgA nephropathy (Source: KDIGO 2021).

rationales.A: Platelet transfusion in STEC-HUS may exacerbate microvascular thrombosis and is avoided unless active major bleeding or planned invasive procedure. Would be correct only for active bleeding (Source: Australasian Paediatric Nephrology).
rationales.B: Plasma exchange is for TTP and atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS where it has not shown benefit; reserved for diagnostic uncertainty pending ADAMTS13. Would be correct in TTP or atypical HUS (Source: Australasian Paediatric Nephrology).
rationales.C: Antibiotics for STEC are associated with increased HUS progression risk (toxin release) and not recommended once HUS is established. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: HUS).
rationales.E: Antimotility agents are contraindicated in STEC (prolong toxin contact) and increase HUS risk/severity. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: HUS).

rationales.A: Medical hyperkalaemia management provides temporary shift only and does not address overload/acidosis/anuria; failure to respond is itself a dialysis indication. Would be correct only as initial bridge to RRT (Source: KDIGO 2012 AKI).
rationales.B: Diuretics are unlikely effective in established anuric AKI and not a substitute for RRT when AEIOU indications are met. Would be correct only in non-anuric AKI (Source: KDIGO 2012).
rationales.C: Further fluid in established anuric HUS with rising weight, HTN and pulmonary oedema worsens overload. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2012).
rationales.E: Resonium is slow and risks intestinal necrosis in unwell paediatric patients and does not address acidosis/overload. Would be correct only as adjunct in non-emergent setting (Source: KDIGO 2012).

rationales.B: The HUS triad is common to all HUS subtypes (STEC, complement-mediated, secondary). Would never be discriminating (Source: RCH CPG: HUS).
rationales.C: Hypertension occurs in both STEC and atypical HUS through volume and renin pathways. Would never be discriminating (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Normal ADAMTS13 excludes TTP but does not distinguish STEC from atypical HUS. Would discriminate from TTP, not between STEC and atypical (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.E: Atypical HUS can be triggered by URTI but so can STEC; URTI alone is non-discriminating. Would never be discriminating (Source: Australasian Paediatric Nephrology).

rationales.A: Corticosteroids have no role in uncomplicated PSGN, which is supportive while complement normalises. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: PSGN).
rationales.B: Biopsy is for atypical features (low C3 over 8 weeks, progressive AKI, nephrotic-range proteinuria, diagnostic uncertainty); classic clinical/serological PSGN does not need biopsy. Would be correct with atypical features (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.C: Plasma exchange is not indicated in uncomplicated PSGN; ACEi can worsen AKI in acute oliguric phase and is not first-line antihypertensive in PSGN. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.D: Severe HTN over 99th centile with AKI warrants admission for monitoring, antihypertensive therapy and recognition of complications. Would be correct only in mild PSGN without severe HTN/AKI (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Hypospadias contraindicates neonatal circumcision because the foreskin is required as tissue for urethroplasty. Would never be correct in any hypospadias (Source: RCH CPG: Hypospadias).
rationales.C: Even partial circumcision compromises the prepuce needed for urethroplasty. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Distal hypospadias with chordee requires paediatric urology assessment and family counselling, even with normal voiding. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: DSD workup is for proximal hypospadias, hypospadias with undescended testis or bifid scrotum, not isolated distal hypospadias with descended testes. Would be correct only in proximal/cryptorchid presentations (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Surgery cannot be planned until sex of rearing is determined; proximal hypospadias with non-palpable gonad is DSD until proven otherwise. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DSD).
rationales.C: This is proximal hypospadias with cryptorchidism and bifid scrotum - highest-risk for DSD. Would never be correct in this presentation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Glucocorticoid replacement is for confirmed/strongly suspected CAH with salt wasting; empirical steroids mask diagnosis. Would be correct only in confirmed/suspected adrenal crisis (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Outpatient deferral risks missing salt-wasting CAH crisis in week 2 and delays MDT DSD workup. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Truly retractile testis stays in the scrotum after manipulation; this one does not, defining undescended/ascending testis requiring referral. Would be correct only with truly retractile testis (Source: RACS).
rationales.C: Delaying beyond 18 months increases subfertility and malignancy risk; modern paediatric anaesthesia in infants is safe. Would never be correct (Source: RACS).
rationales.D: Hormonal therapy has low success/high relapse rates and is not recommended first-line in AU. Would never be correct (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology).
rationales.E: US does not reliably locate non-palpable testis and does not change surgical plan; imaging delays definitive management. Would never be correct (Source: RACS).

rationales.B: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in phenotypic male newborn is DSD until proven otherwise and risks missing salt-wasting CAH in week 2; outpatient deferral is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DSD).
rationales.C: Laparoscopy is eventual investigation for confirmed non-palpable testes but only after DSD has been excluded; operating without karyotype risks intervention on a virilised female. Would be correct only after DSD excluded (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Empirical steroid/mineralocorticoid is for biochemically confirmed/strongly suspected adrenal crisis; empirical without workup masks diagnosis. Would be correct only in confirmed crisis (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Bilateral non-palpable gonads is never normal; salt-wasting CAH can be lethal in week 2 if missed. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Imaging in the first 48 h underestimates hydronephrosis (physiological neonatal oliguria); routine MCUG not indicated for isolated SFU 1-2. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCR; RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Early scanning and routine MCUG are both inappropriate for isolated mild-moderate antenatal hydronephrosis. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.C: Three-month deferral risks missing significant uropathy; SFU 2 should be assessed by 6 weeks. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Symptom-driven imaging misses asymptomatic obstructive uropathy and VUR; all antenatally detected hydronephrosis needs postnatal confirmation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Torsion typically has sudden severe pain, nausea, high-riding horizontal testis and absent cremasteric reflex; this presentation does not fit. Would be correct in classic torsion presentation (Source: USANZ).
rationales.C: Epididymo-orchitis has diffuse epididymal tenderness and positive urinalysis and is uncommon prepubertally without urinary anomaly. Would be correct with positive urinalysis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Idiopathic scrotal oedema has painless/mildly tender pink scrotal swelling extending to perineum; no focal upper-pole tenderness or blue dot. Would be correct with that morphology (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Incarcerated hernia has tender groin lump extending into scrotum often with vomiting; examination here is intratesticular with no inguinal mass. Would be correct with groin lump (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Recurrent stereotyped acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution and contralateral bell-clapper is classic for intermittent torsion; normal Doppler does not exclude it. Would never be correct (Source: USANZ).
rationales.B: Observation delays definitive treatment and exposes the testis to recurrent torsion-detorsion injury; contralateral bell-clapper and history mandate operative exploration. Would never be correct (Source: USANZ).
rationales.C: MRI is not imaging of choice in suspected torsion and delays surgical exploration. Would never be correct as first step (Source: USANZ).
rationales.E: Negative urinalysis, no urethral discharge, no sexual activity and contralateral bell-clapper make epididymo-orchitis unlikely; antibiotics delay definitive management. Would be correct only with positive urinalysis and STI risk (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: No anaphylaxis features (urticaria, angioedema, hypotension) despite peanut history; adrenaline is not indicated here. Would be correct only with confirmed anaphylaxis features (Source: ASCIA Acute Anaphylaxis).
rationales.B: Aminophylline is third-line in paediatric severe asthma, only after IV magnesium and IV salbutamol have failed. Would be correct as third-line in PICU (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute).
rationales.C: Intubation in severe asthma is fraught with complications (bronchospasm, hypotension, barotrauma) and reserved for absolute respiratory arrest or exhaustion. Would be correct only with impending arrest after maximal medical therapy (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Asthmatic children may be mildly dehydrated, but fluid boluses do not relieve bronchospasm. Would be correct only for confirmed hypovolaemia, not primary asthma treatment (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Breast milk jaundice presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; this infant's conjugated fraction is severely elevated indicating cholestasis (biliary atresia until proven otherwise). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice).
rationales.B: Phototherapy is only for unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; in cholestasis it is ineffective and causes bronze baby syndrome. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Empirical treatment without ruling out time-critical surgical biliary atresia is inappropriate and delays definitive care. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: HIDA scan is part of workup but US must be performed first to assess structural anatomy; timeline must be urgent, not routine outpatient. Would be correct only after urgent US (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Activated charcoal is effective within 2 h of ingestion (4 h for sustained release); 7-h unknown window makes it unlikely beneficial. Would be correct within 1-2 h of ingestion (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Rumack-Matthew nomogram is strictly for single known-time acute ingestion; cannot be used in staggered or unknown timing. Would be correct only with single known-time ingestion (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Waiting for hepatotoxicity to manifest (24-72 h) misses the critical NAC window to prevent damage entirely. Would never be correct (Source: Australian PIC).
rationales.E: Bicarbonate is for TCA or severe salicylate overdoses; SSRIs and ibuprofen do not cause QRS widening. Would be correct only in TCA toxicity (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: While fracture needs orthopaedic management, discharging ignores highly specific NAI signs and places child at extreme risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse).
rationales.B: OI is a rare differential, but corner fracture with torso bruising strongly suggests abuse; investigating rare metabolic causes must not delay mandatory child protection workup. Would be correct only after safeguarding workup excludes abuse (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: A 50 cm fall onto carpet generates insufficient kinetic energy for a metaphyseal corner fracture (requires violent shearing/twisting). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Head imaging may be required as part of NAI workup for infants under 12 months with abuse signs, but the immediate next step is admission and notification. Would be correct as part of admission workup, not first step (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Fluid-refractory shock persists after 40 mL/kg crystalloid; further boluses increase pulmonary oedema risk without improving perfusion. Would never be correct after 40 mL/kg failure (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).
rationales.B: Intubating a profoundly shocked child before restoring vascular tone is high risk for peri-intubation arrest. Would be correct only after inotrope established and stabilisation (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.D: Colloids are not superior to crystalloids in initial sepsis resuscitation; shock is fluid-refractory, requiring vasoactive agents not more volume. Would never be correct (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).
rationales.E: Corticosteroids are for catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, AFTER adrenaline/noradrenaline started. Would be correct only after inotrope (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric).

rationales.A: Bronchodilators are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis under 12 months; pathology is mucus plugging and oedema, not bronchospasm. Would never be correct under 12 months (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).
rationales.B: Steroids do not change outcomes in bronchiolitis (not croup or asthma). Would be correct only in croup or asthma (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Antibiotics not indicated without focal bacterial features (no consolidation, no toxic appearance). Would be correct only with focal pneumonia features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Feeding 50 percent of usual meets AU admission threshold; discharging is unsafe. Would be correct only above 50 percent feeding (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: No focal bacterial features and antibiotics do not change outcomes in typical bronchiolitis. Would be correct only with focal bacterial features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Imaging and broad antibiotics are not routine in typical bronchiolitis. Would be correct only with consolidation or sepsis features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Bronchodilators are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months. Would be correct only in older viral wheeze (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Adrenaline plus steroid is the croup pathway, not bronchiolitis (no barking cough or stridor). Would be correct in croup (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).

rationales.A: Biochemical reassessment is important but new neurologic deterioration during DKA prompts immediate cerebral oedema treatment, not observation. Would never be correct in suspected cerebral oedema (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.C: Excessive fluid may worsen cerebral oedema risk; neuro decline in this context should not be treated as dehydration. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.D: Imaging must not delay treatment when cerebral oedema is clinically suspected. Would be correct as adjunct after hyperosmolar therapy started, not before (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).
rationales.E: Dextrose may be added later to allow continued insulin, but stopping insulin risks worsening ketosis and does not address acute neuro decline. Would be correct as add-on, not as primary management (Source: ISPAD DKA).

rationales.B: AXR can show obstruction but is not the best first test for suspected intussusception; empirical gastroenteritis misses surgical emergency. Would be correct only as adjunct after US (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).
rationales.C: Upper GI contrast is for bilious vomiting concerning for malrotation; this is colicky pain with red-mucus stool. Would be correct in malrotation pattern (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: CT is not first-line in children with suspected intussusception due to radiation; US is preferred. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty after US (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.E: Episodic colicky pain with red mucus is not gastroenteritis; stool testing delays diagnosis of surgical emergency. Would be correct only in confirmed infectious gastroenteritis (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Fever with severe neutropenia is a medical emergency; waiting for repeat counts delays time-critical antibiotics. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Neutropenia).
rationales.C: Oral therapy and discharge are not appropriate for febrile child with profound neutropenia and raised inflammatory markers. Would be correct only in low-risk febrile neutropenia within research protocols (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Line removal is not the first step in line-preserved febrile neutropenia presentation; cultures obtained promptly, antibiotics immediately. Would be correct only with documented line sepsis after stabilisation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: G-CSF does not replace urgent empirical antibiotics; the immediate priority is antimicrobial therapy. Would be correct only as adjunct after antibiotics (Source: eviQ).

rationales.A: Insulin is essential in DKA but fluids and careful resuscitation come first/alongside; insulin without addressing intravascular depletion is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).
rationales.C: SC insulin is not appropriate for this degree of acidosis, dehydration, and altered mental state; requires hospital-based DKA management. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Bicarbonate is not routinely used in paediatric DKA; reserved for very specific circumstances. Would be correct only in life-threatening hyperkalaemia or pH under 6.9 (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.E: K is elevated but typical DKA management focuses on controlled rehydration and insulin with monitoring; no ECG-instability indication for calcium. Would be correct only with ECG-instability (Source: ISPAD DKA).

rationales.B: Routine bronchodilator trials are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis (no reliable improvement). Would be correct only in older viral wheeze (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Mild hypercapnia may occur with worsening bronchiolitis and does not suggest bacterial pneumonia; no focal findings or septic features. Would be correct with focal pneumonia features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Prematurity increases severity risk but this infant does not show impending respiratory arrest, severe acidosis or exhaustion. Would be correct only with impending failure (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Persistent tachypnoea, increased WOB, poor intake and oxygen requirement make discharge unsafe. Would be correct only without those features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: LP is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability and should not delay treatment of suspected meningococcal sepsis. Would never be correct in shock (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Imaging and cultures are secondary in unstable septic shock; delaying antibiotics increases mortality in IMD. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Purpura, shock physiology, prolonged cap refill and elevated lactate strongly support meningococcal sepsis rather than encephalitis. Would be correct only with encephalitis features without shock/purpura (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: IM benzylpenicillin may be considered in remote settings but this critically unwell child requires immediate IV therapy. Would be correct only in remote pre-hospital settings (Source: CDNA IMD SoNG).

rationales.A: Improved SpO2 alone does not indicate stability; rising CO2 and fatigue suggest impending failure requiring escalation. Would never be correct with rising CO2 (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).
rationales.B: Quieter chest with worsening hypercapnia and fatigue is severe airflow limitation, not improvement; discharge unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: No focal infective findings; the major issue is worsening ventilatory failure from severe asthma. Would be correct only with focal pneumonia features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Tachycardia is common after beta-agonists in severe distress; bronchodilators should not be ceased in deteriorating severe asthma. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Young febrile infants under 3 months require inpatient assessment and parenteral antibiotics due to high invasive bacterial infection risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).
rationales.C: Delaying antibiotics in a potentially septic young infant is unsafe (rapid deterioration risk). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Renal imaging may be considered later but should not delay urgent evaluation and treatment of febrile UTI in a young infant. Would be correct as adjunct after stabilisation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Fever contributes to tachycardia but lethargy and poor perfusion in a young infant require immediate sepsis-focused management. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Systemically unwell with suspected meningitis and possible sepsis - treatment should not be delayed for LP; early antibiotics are time-critical. Would never be correct in unstable patient (Source: RCH CPG: Meningitis).
rationales.C: Observation alone is unsafe with fever, meningism, and reduced consciousness; delay increases bacterial meningitis/sepsis risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Not a benign viral syndrome; drowsiness, neck stiffness, photophobia, and haemodynamic abnormality require urgent treatment. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: CT is not first step in unstable child with suspected meningitis unless specific raised ICP signs or focal neurology. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Posture and pain fit pulled elbow, but bruise and concerning social context mean clinician should not stop at reduction alone; broader injury and safeguarding assessment required. Would be correct only without bruise/concerning context (Source: RCH CPG: Child Protection).
rationales.C: Absence of deformity does not exclude fracture or inflicted injury; bruise and inconsistent mechanism require further assessment. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Analgesia alone is inadequate with possible fracture or safeguarding concern; delay could miss significant injury. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Viral synovitis is lower-limb or systemic picture and does not explain focal arm bruising after questionable mechanism. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Intermittent severe pain, lethargy, bilious vomiting, red currant jelly stool strongly suggest intussusception not gastroenteritis. Would be correct only in proven gastroenteritis (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).
rationales.C: US and air enema are preferred first-line investigations/management; CT is not first-line. Would be correct only after non-diagnostic US (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Bilious vomiting suggests obstruction but does not confirm perforation; non-operative reduction remains appropriate without peritonitis. Would be correct only with peritonitis or perforation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Episodic pain and red currant jelly stool are more consistent with intussusception than primary bacteraemia. Would never be correct as primary (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Thrombocytosis develops later in KD and treatment should not be delayed when clinical criteria are met. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).
rationales.C: Measles has cough, coryza, conjunctivitis; this child has classic Kawasaki mucocutaneous and extremity findings. Would be correct in measles prodrome (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Combination of conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity swelling, rash and prolonged fever is far more consistent with Kawasaki than bacterial sepsis. Would be correct in confirmed sepsis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Treatment is guided by clinical diagnosis and should occur promptly to reduce coronary aneurysm risk, not delayed for echo confirmation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Fluid restriction may be later management but a child who has seized from severe hyponatraemia needs urgent active correction. Would be correct only in asymptomatic SIADH (Source: RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia).
rationales.C: Oral salt is too slow for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with seizure; emergency requires controlled IV correction. Would be correct only in mild chronic hyponatraemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Febrile seizure does not explain Na 119 mmol/L; biochemical abnormality is the key driver. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Desmopressin would worsen water retention; not treatment for symptomatic hyponatraemic seizure. Would be correct only in central DI (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Preceding viral illness is common in IgA vasculitis and does not indicate severe disease. Would never be a marker for monitoring escalation (Source: RCH CPG: HSP).
rationales.B: Palpable purpura is characteristic but not by itself a marker of severe disease. Would never be correct as the discriminating feature (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Transient arthralgia and periarticular swelling are common and self-limited. Would never be the discriminator (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Abdominal pain is common in IgA vasculitis though severe/persistent pain may need intussusception workup; renal findings are more concerning here. Would be correct only with severe persistent abdominal pain (Source: RCH CPG).
