rationales.A: Sudden severe pain, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis strongly suggest torsion requiring surgery; antibiotics for epididymitis miss the surgical emergency. Would be correct only with positive urinalysis and STI risk (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Scrotum).
rationales.B: Imaging should not delay surgical review when torsion suspicion is high; viability declines with time. Would be correct only with low clinical suspicion (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Manual detorsion may be attempted by experienced clinicians but does not replace urgent surgical exploration and fixation. Would be correct only as bridge to theatre (Source: EAU Paediatric).
rationales.E: Lack of fever does not reduce torsion concern; delayed treatment risks irreversible ischaemia. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Neuro deterioration with headache, bradycardia, hypertension and reduced consciousness during DKA is cerebral oedema, not inadequate insulin. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.C: Delaying treatment risks progression of cerebral oedema and neurological injury. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD DKA).
rationales.D: Although infection may precipitate DKA, the temporal deterioration during treatment with Cushing physiology strongly suggests cerebral oedema. Would be correct only with infective focus and stable neurology (Source: RCH CPG: DKA).
rationales.E: LP is inappropriate in suspected raised ICP and delays urgent treatment. Would never be correct in this scenario (Source: ISPAD DKA).

rationales.B: Routine bronchodilators are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis (limited benefit). Would be correct only in older viral wheeze (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Corticosteroids not routinely recommended for uncomplicated bronchiolitis. Would be correct only in croup or asthma (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Routine CXR not indicated in typical bronchiolitis and may increase unnecessary antibiotic use. Would be correct only with focal consolidation suspicion (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Persistent hypoxia and poor feeding warrant inpatient supportive management rather than discharge. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Features requiring admission: SpO2 91 percent persistently, moderate respiratory distress, feeding 50 percent of usual. Discharge unsafe. Would never be correct with admission criteria met (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).
rationales.B: Bronchodilators NOT recommended for bronchiolitis (viral inflammation/mucus plugging, not bronchospasm). Would be correct only in older viral wheeze with prior salbutamol response (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis; viral PCR, CXR, bloods are not routinely indicated. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty or sepsis concerns (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Corticosteroids do not improve outcomes in bronchiolitis. Would be correct only in croup/asthma (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Clinical appearance does not exclude meningitis in this age; LP is mandatory. Cefotaxime alone misses Listeria. Would be correct only over 8 weeks meeting low-risk criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).
rationales.C: SBI risk in febrile neonates is 10-15 percent; observation and discharge is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Ceftriaxone avoided in neonates; IM single-dose inadequate; LP cannot be deferred. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone avoided in neonates; AU regimen is ampicillin/benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime for 7d-under-4-week infants. Aciclovir reserved for suspected HSV. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: MDI-spacer is acceptable delivery but severe/life-threatening asthma (SpO2 90, unable to speak, marked recession) requires OXYGEN-DRIVEN nebuliser. Would be correct only in mild-moderate asthma (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma).
rationales.C: IV hydrocortisone is alternative not superior to oral pred; bronchodilator must NOT be delayed for CXR which is not routine. Would be correct only if oral not tolerated (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: IV magnesium is SECOND-line after failed initial inhaled therapy. Would be correct as second-line after failure (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Salbutamol 2.5 mg is for 2-5 years; a 7-year-old requires 5 mg; ipratropium must be ADDED from start in severe disease. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Nebulised adrenaline is for moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest; this child has mild croup. Would be correct only with stridor at rest plus marked recession (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.C: Oral prednisolone is less effective than single-dose dexamethasone for croup and requires multiple doses; immediate discharge without observation is unsafe. Would be correct only if dexamethasone unavailable (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Croup is a clinical diagnosis; lateral neck XR not indicated for routine assessment. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Humidified O2 not indicated when SpO2 is 95 percent; admission not routine for mild-moderate croup that responds to dexamethasone. Would be correct only with persistent stridor at rest (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: IV fluids are for SEVERE dehydration or failed enteral; this child has moderate dehydration and should be managed enterally. Would be correct in severe dehydration (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).
rationales.C: Child has been unable to tolerate oral fluids for 6 h with persistent vomiting; encouragement alone is unlikely to succeed. Would be correct only in tolerating oral intake (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Ondansetron may reduce vomiting but moderate dehydration requires SUPERVISED rehydration (50 mL/kg over 4 h), not just antiemetic. Would be correct as adjunct to NG ORS (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Stool culture not routinely indicated unless bloody diarrhoea, recent travel, prolonged symptoms; empirical antibiotics not standard for paediatric viral gastroenteritis. Would be correct only in bloody diarrhoea or invasive infection (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Mycoplasma becomes more common in school-aged children, but pneumococcus remains the most common bacterial cause of CAP across paediatric ages. Would be correct only with atypical features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Augmentin not routinely required for uncomplicated CAP in fully immunised child; amoxicillin alone provides adequate pneumococcal cover. Would be correct in aspiration pneumonia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: While many paediatric LRTIs are viral, this child has clinical signs of bacterial pneumonia (focal crackles, bronchial breath sounds). Would be correct only in pure viral LRTI without consolidation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Cefalexin has unreliable pneumococcal activity vs amoxicillin; not first-line. Would be correct only with confirmed mild penicillin allergy (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Adrenaline is for moderate/severe croup with stridor at rest and significant WOB; this child has mild croup (Westley 1). Would be correct in stridor at rest with marked recession (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Prednisolone is less effective than single-dose dexamethasone for croup and requires multiple doses. Would be correct only if dexamethasone unavailable (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Nebulised budesonide is alternative but more resource-intensive and no more effective than oral dexamethasone. Would be correct only when oral not tolerated (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Croup is viral; antibiotics not indicated in uncomplicated croup. Would be correct only with bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Salbutamol not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; infant meets admission criteria. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Hypertonic saline not recommended for routine bronchiolitis in AU/NICE. Would be correct only in selected research protocols (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Routine imaging and broad antibiotics not indicated in typical bronchiolitis. Would be correct only with focal pneumonia or sepsis features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Corticosteroids not effective in bronchiolitis (not croup pathway). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Routine LP after simple febrile seizure in immunised, well, neurologically normal child without meningism is not indicated. Would be correct only with meningism or persistent altered conscious state (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).
rationales.B: Anticonvulsant prophylaxis is not recommended; recurrence risk does not justify prophylaxis and it does not alter later epilepsy risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Neuroimaging not indicated for simple febrile convulsions with normal neuro exam. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs or complex febrile seizure (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Rescue benzodiazepines are for prolonged or complex febrile seizures, not first simple febrile seizure. Would be correct only after prolonged or recurrent complex febrile seizures (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Despite cervical lymphadenopathy and rash mimicking bacterial infection, prolonged fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue and extremity changes are Kawasaki. Would be correct only in confirmed bacterial lymphadenitis (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).
rationales.B: Aspirin alone does not reduce coronary aneurysm risk; must be combined with IVIG. Would never be correct as monotherapy (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Corticosteroids not first-line for KD; may be rescue therapy for IVIG-resistant disease. Would be correct only after IVIG failure or in high-risk patients (Source: AHA Kawasaki).
rationales.E: KD is leading cause of acquired heart disease in developed countries; untreated 25 percent coronary aneurysm risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: AXR may show obstruction or target sign but is neither sensitive nor specific enough; normal XR does not rule out intussusception. Would be correct only as adjunct after US (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception).
rationales.C: Contrast enema is diagnostic and therapeutic but US should be performed first to confirm diagnosis and exclude perforation. Would be correct after US (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: CT delivers significant radiation and is not first-line; US is preferred. Would be correct only with US non-diagnostic (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.E: Surgery is for failed enema reduction, perforation, or instability; not diagnostic modality. Would be correct only after failed enema or perforation (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Biopsy not required in classic nephrotic (1-10 years, oedema, heavy proteinuria, normal BP, no macroscopic haematuria). Would be correct only with atypical features (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.C: Diuretics alone do not treat the underlying disease and may cause electrolyte disturbance/hypovolaemia. Would be correct as adjunct for severe oedema (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.D: IV albumin and frusemide are for severe symptomatic oedema (scrotal, respiratory compromise, ascites); this child has manageable oedema. Would be correct only with severe oedema (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.E: ACEi is for persistent proteinuria in steroid-resistant NS, not first-line initial. Would be correct only after steroid resistance documented (Source: KDIGO 2021).

rationales.A: 0.45 percent saline NOT used in initial paediatric DKA (cerebral oedema risk from rapid osmolar shifts); 0.9 percent saline with K is recommended and deficit replaced over 48 h. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD; RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Large rapid boluses increase cerebral oedema risk; deficit is replaced over 48 h, not 24 h. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD).
rationales.D: Fluid resuscitation precedes insulin; insulin within first hour increases CV collapse and cerebral oedema risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Bicarbonate is not routinely recommended in paediatric DKA; may worsen intracellular acidosis and cerebral oedema risk. Would be correct only in life-threatening hyperkalaemia or pH under 6.9 (Source: ISPAD).

rationales.A: LP is contraindicated in haemodynamically unstable patients with suspected meningococcal disease; risks herniation and delays life-saving treatment. Would be correct only after stabilisation in stable patient (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: CT before LP is for focal neuro signs, papilloedema, or GCS under 9; immediate priority here is antibiotics and resuscitation. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs in stable patient (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Adjunctive dexamethasone is for confirmed Hib/pneumococcal meningitis given before/with first antibiotic; in shocked patients antibiotics and resuscitation take precedence. Would be correct only in stable patient with confirmed bacterial meningitis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Notification is urgent but must NOT delay treatment; antibiotics first, notification in parallel. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA IMD).

rationales.A: Infant is HIGH RISK (under 60 days, preterm born, BRUE); home monitor and outpatient referral are inadequate. Would never be correct in high-risk BRUE (Source: AAP BRUE Guideline).
rationales.C: Full septic workup is for clinical signs of sepsis or maternal risk factors; this infant is well-appearing. Would be correct only with sepsis features (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE).
rationales.D: Polysomnography not first-line for BRUE; may be considered for recurrent/severe events. Discharging high-risk infant home is unsafe. Would be correct as outpatient adjunct in recurrent BRUE (Source: AAP).
rationales.E: Single BRUE in preterm infant with plausible history does not automatically require child protection referral. Would be correct only with safeguarding red flags (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Insufficient to capture rebound; adrenaline lasts ~2 h and rebound can occur after that. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Croup).
rationales.B: Adrenaline effect lasts up to 2 h and rebound can occur 1-2 h after that, so 2 h is insufficient. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Six hours is sometimes used with clinical concern but is not the standard minimum requirement. Would be correct only with persistent clinical concern (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Without adequate observation, rebound stridor can occur at home; observation is required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Omitting LP in a 6-week-old is risky; meningitis can present without meningism. Antibiotics given empirically. Would never be correct in under-8-week infant (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).
rationales.C: Well appearance does not exclude SBI in a young infant; febrile infant under 8 weeks should be investigated. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Normal inflammatory markers do not reliably exclude SBI in this age group; LP is recommended. Would be correct only in over-8-weeks meeting all low-risk criteria (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Waiting for clinical signs of meningitis may delay treatment; LP should be part of initial workup. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: IV salbutamol is third-line after magnesium failure or in life-threatening asthma; magnesium tried first. Would be correct as third-line (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: After 3 doses without improvement, further inhaled therapy alone is unlikely sufficient; escalation needed. Would be correct only with early response (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: IV fluids may be given for hydration but will not relieve bronchospasm; magnesium is the specific bronchodilator. Would be correct as adjunct, not next step (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Pneumothorax is differential but immediate priority is escalation of bronchodilator therapy; CXR can follow stabilisation. Would be correct only with high pneumothorax suspicion (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

rationales.A: Maternal intrapartum fever 38+ and prolonged ROM are EOS risk factors even with negative GBS; observation alone is insufficient. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: EOS).
rationales.B: LP is not routine for EOS in well-appearing infants without meningeal signs; blood culture and CRP are appropriate. Would be correct only with meningeal signs (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Reasonable but maternal fever and prolonged ROM with negative GBS increases risk of non-GBS organisms; many guidelines recommend empirical therapy. Would be correct only without maternal fever (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Early discharge with observation only is not recommended given maternal fever; infant is moderate risk. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Oral prednisolone alone has not been shown to prevent or treat established HSP nephritis; may be used for severe abdominal pain or joint symptoms. Would be correct only for severe abdominal pain (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.C: Mild urinary abnormalities may be monitored but nephrotic-range proteinuria requires specialist referral and investigation. Would be correct only with mild proteinuria (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: ACEi is used for persistent proteinuria in CKD; in acute HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria, it is adjunct, not primary. Would be correct as adjunct after specialist review (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.E: IV methylprednisolone may be used after biopsy confirms crescentic nephritis but is not the first step; first is nephrology referral and biopsy. Would be correct only after biopsy confirms crescentic disease (Source: KDIGO).

rationales.A: Oral PEG is for chronic constipation in CF; in acute DIOS with bilious vomiting and palpable mass, more aggressive bowel clearance is needed. Would be correct in chronic CF constipation (Source: RCH CPG: CF).
rationales.C: NAC is adjunctive to thin tenacious mucus but not primary therapy; oral Gastrografin is mainstay. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Lactulose and phosphate enemas are not effective for DIOS (inspissated material in distal ileum/caecum, not simple constipation). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Metoclopramide is prokinetic but does not resolve impaction; NG suction relieves vomiting but does not treat obstruction. Would be correct only as supportive adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: In Aboriginal children AOM should be treated with antibiotics due to high risk of persistent infection, CSOM, and long-term hearing impact. Would never be correct in Aboriginal child (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Amoxicillin is first-line for non-Indigenous children; Aboriginal children, especially otitis-prone, often need amoxicillin-clavulanate for broader cover. Would be correct only in non-Indigenous low-risk (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Augmentin is recommended for those with recent amoxicillin or recurrent AOM, but duration for Aboriginal children with chronic features should be 7 days, not 5. Would be correct with 7-day duration (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: IM ceftriaxone is reserved for severe infections, oral failure, or compliance concerns; not first-line for uncomplicated AOM. Would be correct only in those specific situations (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Azithromycin eliminates organism and limits spread but does not alter clinical course once paroxysmal phase started; primary issue is leukocytosis-driven pulmonary hypertension. Would be correct in early-phase pertussis (Source: CDNA Pertussis).
rationales.C: No evidence IVIG alters course of pertussis; management is supportive plus antibiotics. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Corticosteroids not recommended for pertussis and may worsen immunosuppression; no proven benefit. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: CPAP may be used for apnoea but immediate life-threatening problem is extreme leukocytosis causing pulmonary hypertension; CPAP does not address pathology. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: OME has retracted/neutral TM without acute symptoms; this child has acutely bulging immobile TM with fever. Would be correct in dull retracted TM without fever (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Antibiotics not mandatory for every AOM in low-risk over-2s; AU-specific cut-offs: bilateral disease, otorrhoea, severe symptoms, age under 2 with bilateral. Would be correct only with those features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: CSOM requires persistent perforation with discharge over 6 weeks; this is acute with intact TM. Would be correct in chronic perforation with discharge (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Otitis externa shows canal inflammation with normal TM; this child has bulging immobile TM. Would be correct in pure canal disease (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: IV dextrose is for severe (under 1.5 mmol/L) or symptomatic hypoglycaemia, or after failure of feed plus dextrose gel. Would be correct in severe/symptomatic hypoglycaemia (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.C: BSL 2.2 is below 2.6 threshold and requires intervention, not passive observation. Would never be correct (Source: Queensland Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.D: Maintenance IV is for persistent/severe hypoglycaemia after failure of feed plus gel. Would be correct after failure of first-line (Source: Queensland).
rationales.E: Glucagon is for refractory hypoglycaemia where IV access delayed/failed. Would be correct only with delayed/failed IV access (Source: Queensland).

rationales.B: Montelukast is second-line add-on but ICS dose escalation is preferred first. Would be correct only if ICS not tolerated or as add-on after escalation (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: LABA can be added in over-6s if ICS alone insufficient but ICS dose increase is tried before LABA. Would be correct after ICS escalation fails (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: Short oral steroid course is for acute exacerbations, not chronic control. Would be correct only for exacerbation (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Waking 2+ nights/week and reliever over 2 times/week indicates poor control. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

rationales.B: Constitutional delay does not have dysmorphic features like webbed neck. Would be correct only with normal exam and family history (Source: RCH CPG: Short Stature).
rationales.C: Isolated GHD causes proportionate short stature without dysmorphism. Would be correct without dysmorphic features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Noonan can have webbed neck and short stature but typically includes cardiac anomalies (pulmonary stenosis) and normal karyotype. Would be correct with PS and normal karyotype (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Hypothyroidism causes growth failure but not the dysmorphic features described. Would be correct without dysmorphism (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Sleep study confirms severity, especially with risk factors or uncertainty, but clear history/exam warrants direct ENT referral. Would be correct with diagnostic uncertainty (Source: RCH CPG: OSA).
rationales.C: Intranasal steroids may reduce adenoidal size in mild OSA but with kissing tonsils and clear symptoms, surgical referral is more appropriate. Would be correct in mild OSA without tonsillar hypertrophy (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: No acute infection present; tonsils are chronically enlarged. Antibiotics not indicated. Would be correct in acute tonsillitis (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: OSA symptoms warrant intervention; not normal. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Phenobarbitone is second-line, usually added if morphine alone insufficient. Would be correct as add-on (Source: RCH CPG: NAS).
rationales.C: Naloxone is contraindicated in neonatal opioid withdrawal (precipitates acute withdrawal/seizures). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Finnegan over 8 on two consecutive scores is indication for pharmacological treatment; supportive care alone is insufficient. Would be correct only with Finnegan under 8 (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Clonidine is sometimes adjunct but not first-line; morphine is standard. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Eczema herpeticum presents with monomorphic punched-out vesicles, often systemic symptoms; this child has pustules suggesting staphylococcal infection. Would be correct with grouped vesicles and HSV contact (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Short course stronger topical steroid may be used, but pustules indicate infection requiring antibiotic before escalating steroid. Would be correct only after infection treated (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Systemic immunosuppression is for severe refractory atopic dermatitis; inappropriate before treating acute bacterial superinfection. Would be correct only after failed maximised topicals without infection (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Swabs may be taken but treatment must not be delayed; empirical antibiotics started immediately. Would be correct as adjunct alongside immediate antibiotic (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: MRI not routinely required for oligoarticular JIA diagnosis; clinically based. Would be correct only with diagnostic uncertainty or atypical features (Source: RCH CPG: JIA).
rationales.C: Pericarditis is associated with systemic JIA, not oligoarticular. Would be correct only in systemic JIA (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Prolonged duration (6 weeks) and lack of migratory arthritis/cardiac signs make ARF unlikely. Would be correct in suspected ARF with carditis (Source: RHDAustralia).
rationales.E: Isolated joint involvement, normal WCC and positive ANA are consistent with JIA; leukaemia would show systemic symptoms and haematological abnormalities. Would be correct only with cytopenias or systemic features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: UC does not cause perianal fistula and is continuous without skip lesions; perianal fistula essentially excludes UC. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: IBD).
rationales.C: Chronicity (3 months), weight loss, anaemia, fistula and endoscopic pattern point to Crohn's, not acute infection. Would be correct only with acute presentation and confirmed pathogen (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Coeliac does not produce bloody diarrhoea or perianal fistula. Would be correct only with serology-positive coeliac picture (Source: RACGP).
rationales.E: Behcet has recurrent oral/genital ulcers (absent here); endoscopic distribution and perianal fistula favour Crohn's. Would be correct with classic Behcet triad (Source: ACR).

rationales.B: Aplastic anaemia is pancytopenic but does NOT have circulating blasts or hepatosplenomegaly. Would be correct only without blasts or organomegaly (Source: ANZCHOG).
rationales.C: ITP shows ISOLATED thrombocytopenia with normal Hb and WCC and no organomegaly. Would be correct only in isolated thrombocytopenia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Osteomyelitis is focal with raised WCC, not pancytopenia with blasts. Would be correct in focal bone infection (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: JIA causes joint swelling and stiffness, not blasts on film. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: ASQ-3 is general developmental screen not autism-specific; in a child with multiple ASD red flags an ASD-specific tool plus audiology is required. Would be correct as general screen only without ASD red flags (Source: Autism CRC).
rationales.C: CARS is a diagnostic instrument used by trained clinicians as part of multi-disciplinary assessment, not first-line GP screening. Would be correct only in specialist MDT (Source: Autism CRC).
rationales.D: WPPSI is a cognitive battery not ASD screen; cognitive assessment is part of later MDT diagnostic, not GP screening step. Would be correct in later MDT (Source: Autism CRC).
rationales.E: Documenting ASD-specific screen and audiology strengthens referral and ensures correct triage/NDIS eligibility; direct referral without screening is suboptimal. Would never be correct as direct without documentation (Source: Autism CRC).

rationales.B: IV insulin is for DKA or severe hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar states, not stable new diagnosis. Would be correct in DKA (Source: RCH CPG: T1DM).
rationales.C: Metformin not indicated for T1DM; insulin is mandatory. Would be correct in confirmed T2DM (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Basal alone is insufficient; mealtime boluses needed to cover carbohydrate intake. Would be correct only as bridging or in T2DM (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Premixed insulins less flexible and not recommended for initial paediatric management in AU where basal-bolus is standard. Would be correct only in specific clinical situations (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Salbutamol is adjunct for bronchospasm but adrenaline is first-line for anaphylaxis and should be repeated; steroids have delayed onset. Would be correct as adjunct after adrenaline (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.C: Antihistamines are second-line adjuncts and do not reverse laryngeal oedema or hypotension; adrenaline is priority. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.D: Persistent symptoms after one dose require further adrenaline and prolonged observation, not discharge. Would never be correct (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.E: Oxygen alone will not treat anaphylaxis; further adrenaline needed. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: ASCIA).

rationales.A: Infant meets RCH admission criteria (SpO2 persistently under 92, feeding 50 percent); discharge unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Salbutamol has no proven benefit in bronchiolitis under 12 months. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Bronchiolitis is clinical diagnosis; routine imaging and broad antibiotics not indicated. Would be correct only with focal pneumonia or sepsis features (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Corticosteroids not effective in bronchiolitis. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Hypotonic fluids not appropriate for initial volume resuscitation in pyloric stenosis; isotonic required to correct hypochloraemia first; K only added once urine output established. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis).
rationales.B: Ringer's lactate has less chloride than 0.9 percent saline; may not rapidly correct severe hypochloraemic alkalosis. Would be correct as alternative when 0.9 saline not preferred (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: K should not be added until urine output confirmed and serum K known; hyperkalaemia risk. Initial resuscitation should be chloride-rich isotonic. Would be correct only after urine output confirmed (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Hypotonic fluids inadequate for chloride depletion and may worsen hyponatraemia. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Montelukast is second-line add-on but ICS escalation preferred initially. Would be correct only after ICS escalation or as add-on if LABA contraindicated (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: LABA can be added in over-6s if moderate-dose ICS alone insufficient but ICS escalation is first step. Would be correct after ICS escalation fails (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: Waking 2+ nights/week and reliever over 2 times/week indicates poor control. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Oral steroids are for acute exacerbations not chronic control. Would be correct only for exacerbation (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

rationales.A: IVIG does not reverse established complete heart block in neonatal lupus; antibody injury is permanent once block established. Would never be correct in established block (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Postnatal steroids do not reverse established complete heart block; not standard for asymptomatic infant. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Block is permanent; only skin rash resolves with antibody clearance. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Temporary pacing is for symptomatic bradycardia or ventricular rate under 50 with haemodynamic compromise. Would be correct only with symptoms or HR under 50 (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: DAPT is for medium-sized aneurysms; for giant aneurysms (8+ mm or z over 10) systemic anticoagulation with warfarin is preferred. Would be correct in medium aneurysms (Source: AHA Kawasaki).
rationales.C: High-dose aspirin is anti-inflammatory in acute phase, not thromboprophylaxis for giant aneurysms; systemic anticoagulation is required. Would never be correct (Source: AHA Kawasaki).
rationales.D: Second IVIG may be used for persistent fever or inflammation but does not reduce established aneurysm size or thrombosis risk; this child is afebrile. Would be correct in persistent inflammation (Source: AHA Kawasaki).
rationales.E: Aspirin alone is insufficient for thrombosis prevention in giant aneurysms. Would never be correct (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

rationales.A: Bag urine has high contamination rates and is screening only; under-3-months requires hospital admission for parenteral antibiotics with sterile sample. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).
rationales.C: Bag urine has too high false-positive rate to direct treatment; sterile sampling required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Urinalysis strongly suggests UTI; untreated infant UTI risks pyelonephritis and scarring. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: LP not routinely required for isolated UTI in well-appearing 3-month-old; considered if sepsis/meningitis suspected. Would be correct only with sepsis features (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Nephrogenic DI: urine does not concentrate after water deprivation OR after desmopressin (kidney resistant). Would be correct in nephrogenic pattern (Source: RCH CPG: DI).
rationales.C: Primary polydipsia: water deprivation leads to appropriate urine concentration; serum osmolality remains normal. Would be correct in normal water deprivation response (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Diabetes mellitus has hyperglycaemia and glucosuria, not dilute urine with high serum osmolality and normal glucose. Would be correct with hyperglycaemia (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: SIADH has concentrated urine and hyponatraemia (opposite of this presentation). Would be correct in SIADH biochemistry (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Steroids alone are not sufficient for severe HSP nephritis; nephrology input required. Would be correct only in mild HSP nephritis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: ACEi is for persistent proteinuria but does not address acute glomerular inflammation; biopsy-guided therapy needed. Would be correct as adjunct after biopsy (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.D: UPCR over 200 mg/mmol is nephrotic-range proteinuria, severe and unlikely to resolve without intervention. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: These investigations are for structural abnormalities, not HSP nephritis. Would be correct only with suspected structural anomaly (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Surgery is for failed enema, perforation or peritonitis. Would be correct only after enema failure or peritonitis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Untreated intussusception does not reliably resolve and risks ischaemia and perforation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Surgical (open or lap) reduction is second-line in AU after failed enema or when contraindicated. Would be correct after failed enema (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Mechanical reduction is required; antibiotics alone do not reduce intussusception. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: PEG is for chronic constipation, not acute DIOS which requires osmotic agent that hydrates the inspissated material. Would be correct in chronic CF constipation (Source: RCH CPG: CF).
rationales.C: NAC is adjunct to thin mucus but not primary therapy; Gastrografin is standard. Would be correct as adjunct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Lactulose and phosphate enemas not effective for DIOS. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Prokinetics and bowel rest do not resolve obstruction; Gastrografin needed. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Pertussis does not cause bronchospasm; cough is toxin-mediated. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis).
rationales.C: IVIG has not been shown to alter pertussis course. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Steroids have no proven benefit and may worsen immunosuppression. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: CPAP may support breathing but does not address life-threatening pulmonary hypertension from hyperleukocytosis. Would be correct as adjunct only (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Activated charcoal is effective only within 1-2 h of ingestion; at 10 h no role. Would be correct only within 1-2 h (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Normal initial ALT does not exclude impending hepatotoxicity; level is above nomogram line and NAC must start promptly. Would never be correct (Source: Australian PIC).
rationales.D: Transplantation is for established fulminant failure unresponsive to NAC, not initial presentation with normal LFTs. Would be correct only with established fulminant failure (Source: King's College criteria).
rationales.E: Psychiatric review is essential after medical stabilisation; immediate priority is NAC. Would be correct in parallel, not as primary (Source: Australian PIC).

rationales.B: IV salbutamol is third-line after IV magnesium failure or in life-threatening asthma. Would be correct as third-line (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: Aminophylline has narrow therapeutic index; considered after magnesium and salbutamol infusion failure. Would be correct as fourth-line (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: After 3 nebulisers without improvement, further inhaled therapy unlikely sufficient; escalation indicated. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: Intubation is last resort with significant risk; medical therapy with IV magnesium should be attempted first. Would be correct only with impending arrest (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook).

rationales.B: Standard protocol is 33.5 degC for 72 h, not 32 degC for 24 h; deeper cooling has not shown benefit and increases adverse effects. Would never be correct (Source: ILCOR).
rationales.C: Moderate HIE with abnormal aEEG is indication for therapeutic hypothermia which improves outcomes. Would be correct only in mild HIE without indication (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy).
rationales.D: Evidence supports 72 h at 33.5 degC; shorter durations or higher temperatures not standard. Would never be correct (Source: TOBY Trial; ILCOR).
rationales.E: Phenobarbitone is for clinical/electrographic seizures, but primary neuroprotective is hypothermia. Would be correct only with seizures (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy).

rationales.B: Perianal fistulae rarely close with azathioprine alone; biologics are far more effective. Would never be correct (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN).
rationales.C: Fistulotomy alone in Crohn's may result in non-healing and incontinence risk; combined medical therapy with biologics is standard. Would be correct only in non-Crohn's simple fistula (Source: RCH CPG: IBD).
rationales.D: Seton placement controls drainage but definitive closure requires biologic therapy. Would be correct as adjunct, not definitive (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Colostomy is last resort for severe refractory disease; medical therapy with biologics is effective in most. Would be correct only after biologic failure (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Lactulose is for hepatic encephalopathy not urea cycle disorders; hyperammonaemia here is genetic urea cycle defect, not liver failure. Would be correct in hepatic encephalopathy (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia).
rationales.C: NAC is for paracetamol toxicity, not urea cycle disorders. Would be correct in paracetamol overdose (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Haemodialysis often needed for very high ammonia but nitrogen-scavenging drugs are first-line medical therapy; dialysis is adjunctive. Would be correct as adjunct, not sole therapy (Source: HMSANZ).
rationales.E: Neomycin is non-absorbable antibiotic used in hepatic encephalopathy, not urea cycle defects. Would be correct only in hepatic encephalopathy (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Levetiracetam already given as second-line; another similar agent is less likely effective and risks toxicity. Would be correct only if first second-line agent not yet tried (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.C: Propofol infusion is avoided in children for prolonged sedation due to propofol-infusion syndrome risk. Would be correct only briefly for short-duration sedation (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.D: Paraldehyde is rarely used now and not recommended when IV access available. Would be correct only without IV access (Source: APLS Australia).
rationales.E: Magnesium is for eclampsia or hypomagnesaemia, neither present. Would be correct in eclampsia (Source: APLS Australia).

rationales.B: Microscopic haematuria can occur in MCD and is not absolute biopsy indication, especially in typical age group. Would be correct only with persistent macroscopic haematuria (Source: KDIGO 2021).
rationales.C: MCD is most common in younger children but 10 years is within typical range; age alone does not mandate biopsy. Would be correct only outside typical age (under 1 or over 12) (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.D: Hypertension can occur in MCD and is not definitive biopsy indication unless severe or persistent. Would be correct only with persistent severe HTN (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.E: Heavy proteinuria is the definition of nephrotic syndrome and does not independently indicate biopsy. Would never be correct as biopsy indication (Source: KDIGO).

rationales.B: Na and K matter but the primary indices of alkalosis correction are chloride and bicarbonate. Would be correct only as one of several parameters (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis).
rationales.C: pH may normalise before chloride deficit is fully corrected; chloride and bicarbonate are the specific indices. Would never be sufficient alone (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Base excess tracks bicarbonate but does not address chloride deficit specifically. Would never be sufficient alone (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Creatinine reflects dehydration but is not the key operative-readiness parameter. Would never be sufficient alone (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Routine tracheal suctioning for meconium is not indicated if perineal suction adequate and no airway obstruction. Would be correct only with non-vigorous infant and obstructed airway (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).
rationales.C: Chest compressions are for HR under 60 after 30 sec effective ventilation; HR is 100 here. Would be correct only with HR under 60 (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).
rationales.D: Adrenaline is for HR under 60 despite ventilation and compressions; HR is 100 here. Would be correct only with HR under 60 (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).
rationales.E: Infant remains significantly cyanotic with SpO2 70 percent, requiring PPV with O2, not just blow-by. Would be correct only with mild cyanosis and good HR (Source: ANZCOR 13.4).

rationales.B: Glucose 18 is not hypoglycaemia and no adrenal crisis; cerebral oedema requires osmotherapy. Would be correct only in hypoglycaemia or adrenal crisis (Source: ISPAD).
rationales.C: Routine hyperventilation not recommended; brief hyperventilation only as temporising during herniation. Osmotherapy is immediate intervention. Would be correct only briefly during herniation (Source: ISPAD).
rationales.D: Rapid fluid administration can worsen cerebral oedema; rate should be REDUCED, not increased. Would never be correct (Source: ISPAD).
rationales.E: Insulin reduction alone does not address cerebral oedema; immediate osmotherapy required. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: ISPAD).

rationales.B: Duodenal atresia presents in neonatal period with bilious vomiting on day 1. Would be correct in neonatal presentation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.C: Annular pancreas typically presents in infancy or early childhood. Would be correct only in young age (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: EoE presents with dysphagia and food impaction, not duodenal-level obstruction. Would be correct with dysphagia/impaction (Source: RACP).
rationales.E: MALS causes coeliac artery compression and post-prandial epigastric pain but does not cause duodenal cut-off. Would be correct in median arcuate compression (Source: RACP).

rationales.B: UDCA is used after surgery to promote bile flow but does not affect the need for timely Kasai. Would be correct as post-op adjunct (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia).
rationales.C: IVIG not used in biliary atresia. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Biopsy is part of workup but should not delay surgery. Would be correct as part of workup, not delay (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Biliary atresia is not typically genetic; priority is early surgery. Would never be the critical factor (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Simple transfusion can increase blood viscosity and risk further sickling if HbS remains high; exchange preferred in severe ACS. Would be correct only in mild ACS (Source: NHLBI).
rationales.C: Hydroxyurea is long-term therapy, not acute treatment for ACS. Would be correct for chronic prevention (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: IVIG not indicated for ACS. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: With worsening respiratory failure, more aggressive therapy needed (exchange transfusion). Would be correct only in mild ACS (Source: NHLBI).

rationales.B: Mannitol effective osmotherapy but causes osmotic diuresis and can worsen hypotension; hypertonic saline preferred in shock. Would be correct in normotensive patient (Source: RCH CPG: TBI).
rationales.C: Prophylactic aggressive hyperventilation can cause cerebral ischaemia; osmotherapy is first-line. Would be correct only briefly during herniation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: ICP monitor and barbiturate coma are later steps after initial stabilisation, neurosurgical consultation and surgical decompression. Would be correct after stabilisation (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Steroids not indicated in TBI and may worsen outcomes. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: VATS is effective but more invasive; reserved for failure of fibrinolytic therapy or severe disease. Would be correct after fibrinolytic failure (Source: RCH CPG: Empyema).
rationales.C: Antibiotics alone insufficient for loculated empyema; mechanical drainage needed. Would be correct only in mild non-loculated disease (Source: BTS).
rationales.D: Second drain is not first-line; fibrinolysis should be attempted first. Would be correct only after fibrinolysis failure (Source: BTS).
rationales.E: Corticosteroids not routinely indicated in empyema. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Methylene blue has case reports in refractory anaphylactic shock but is not first-line rescue in AU; vasopressin preferred. Would be correct as later salvage (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.B: Glucagon is useful in beta-blocker patients with anaphylaxis; no beta-blocker history here. Would be correct with beta-blocker history (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.C: Corticosteroids have delayed onset (hours) and do not provide immediate haemodynamic support. Would be correct only as adjunct, not for acute haemodynamics (Source: ASCIA).
rationales.E: Higher adrenaline doses may cause arrhythmias and are less effective in anaphylactic vasoplegia; vasopressin provides different mechanism. Would be correct only at moderate doses (Source: ASCIA).

rationales.B: Oesophageal button battery is emergency; observation not appropriate. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Button Battery).
rationales.C: Inducing vomiting is contraindicated (re-impaction, further injury). Would never be correct (Source: NCPC).
rationales.D: Glucagon not recommended for button battery impaction; endoscopic removal required. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Carbonated beverage is dangerous and not recommended. Would never be correct (Source: NCPC).

rationales.B: Phenotypic overlap with KD but age (7, older than typical KD), lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia (not thrombocytosis), markedly elevated inflammatory markers and recent SARS-CoV-2 exposure favour MIS-C. Would be correct in classic KD age 6 months-5 years (Source: RCH CPG: MIS-C).
rationales.C: Sepsis is always differential but mucocutaneous features, cardiac dysfunction, markedly elevated markers and recent SARS-CoV-2 exposure make MIS-C far more likely. Would be correct with positive cultures (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: ARF has migratory polyarthritis, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules or Sydenham chorea with preceding GAS evidence. Would be correct with Jones criteria met (Source: RHDAustralia).
rationales.E: HLH typically has higher ferritin (over 5000), cytopenias in 2+ lineages, and haemophagocytosis on BMA. MIS-C shares some HLH features but management differs. Would be correct with HLH-2004 criteria met (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.B: Dose escalation for low trough without significant ATI; with HIGH neutralising antibodies, escalation unlikely to work and risks infusion reactions. Would be correct only with low/absent ATI (Source: ESPGHAN).
rationales.C: Ustekinumab is valid alternative but AU practice reserves out-of-class switching until after second anti-TNF failure. Would be correct after second anti-TNF failure (Source: ESPGHAN).
rationales.D: Steroids may bridge symptoms but do not address underlying immunogenic loss of response. Would be correct only as bridge to new biologic (Source: ESPGHAN).
rationales.E: Surgery is for medically refractory or complicated disease; multiple medical options remain. Would be correct after biologic failures (Source: ESPGHAN).

rationales.B: Antipsychotics may worsen dyskinesias and do not address underlying autoimmune process. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Autoimmune Encephalitis).
rationales.C: Aciclovir should be commenced empirically in suspected viral encephalitis, but positive anti-NMDAR, EEG extreme delta brush and ovarian teratoma confirm autoimmune cause. Would be correct as initial empirical until PCR results (Source: Titulaer 2013).
rationales.D: Plasma exchange is option but typically combined with corticosteroids, not alone; tumour removal is essential. Would be correct as adjunct, not sole (Source: Titulaer 2013).
rationales.E: Rituximab is second-line for refractory cases, not first-line. Would be correct after first-line failure (Source: Titulaer 2013).

rationales.B: AIH typically has higher transaminases, positive autoantibodies, and does not cause Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia. Would be correct with autoantibodies and no haemolysis (Source: RCH CPG: ALF).
rationales.C: No history of paracetamol ingestion and haemolytic anaemia is not typical. Would be correct with paracetamol history (Source: Australian PIC).
rationales.D: Hep A can cause ALF but does not cause haemolytic anaemia; low ALP more typical of Wilson. Would be correct with positive serology and no haemolysis (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Budd-Chiari has ascites, hepatomegaly and abdominal pain but haemolysis and characteristic Wilson biochemistry are absent. Would be correct with abdominal pain and ascites (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Jaundiced infant with early-onset hyperbilirubinaemia and DAT positivity needs active management, not delayed outpatient. Would be correct only with low level and no haemolysis (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice).
rationales.C: Phenobarbitone not standard next treatment for early pathological neonatal jaundice. Would be correct only in specific situations (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: Exchange transfusion is for much more severe hyperbilirubinaemia or failure of phototherapy. Would be correct only at exchange threshold (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: Poor intake can contribute but does not explain DAT-positive haemolysis; supplementation does not replace treatment for pathological cause. Would be correct only with isolated dehydration (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: MARSIPAN-AU and NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit list HR under 40, postural BP drop over 20 mmHg, T under 35.5 degC, and QTc over 450 ms as red flags mandating inpatient medical admission. Would never be correct (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.C: Acute medical instability (bradycardia, hypothermia, electrolyte derangement, hypoglycaemia) must be corrected on paediatric medical ward first; psychiatric units cannot manage acute instability. Would be correct only after medical stabilisation (Source: ANZAED).
rationales.D: Short-stay observation does not provide structured refeeding, electrolyte replacement, or sustained cardiac telemetry; her QTc 470 and multiple electrolyte derangements require inpatient. Would never be correct (Source: ANZAED).
rationales.E: High-calorie refeeding in severely underweight, hypophosphataemic patient precipitates refeeding syndrome; MARSIPAN-AU recommends starting low (5-20 kcal/kg/day). Would never be correct (Source: ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU).

rationales.A: Advancing calories during refeeding syndrome worsens electrolyte shifts; MARSIPAN-AU recommends halting advancement and replacing electrolytes. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit; MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.B: Refeeding oedema is driven by insulin-mediated Na/water retention and capillary leak; loop diuretics worsen hypokalaemia/hypomagnesaemia and are contraindicated. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.D: Ceasing nutrition prolongs starvation and worsens prognosis; advice is to continue nutrition (often dropping calories modestly) while aggressively correcting electrolytes. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).
rationales.E: Refeeding oedema is not driven by hypoalbuminaemia and resolves with time and electrolyte correction; albumin transfusion not indicated and carries overload risk. Would never be correct (Source: MARSIPAN-AU).

rationales.B: CBT-ED is first-line for adult bulimia, BED, and adult anorexia, but RANZCP ranks FBT first for adolescent anorexia where family involvement possible. Would be correct in adult anorexia (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Olanzapine has modest weight-gain adjunctive evidence in adult anorexia, not first-line for adolescents; psychotherapy (FBT) addresses core illness. Would be correct only as adjunct in adults (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Medically stable with supportive family; outpatient FBT preferred with admission reserved for medical instability or FBT failure. Would be correct only with medical instability (Source: NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit).
rationales.E: Generic supportive counselling lacks structured refeeding framework FBT provides; classified as inadequate for AN. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: SSRI monotherapy is inferior to CBT-ED; fluoxetine (not sertraline) is only SSRI with bulimia evidence at 60 mg, and adjunctive per RANZCP. Would be correct only as add-on to CBT-ED, with fluoxetine at correct dose (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.B: Olanzapine is not recommended pharmacotherapy for bulimia; sedation and metabolic effects without efficacy data. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Medically stable (mild hypokalaemic alkalosis manageable as outpatient), without suicidality; CBT-ED is delivered in outpatient settings. Would be correct only with medical instability or active suicidality (Source: NSW Eating Disorders Toolkit).
rationales.E: Topiramate has some bulimia evidence but is not first-line; tolerability and teratogenicity limit use, and CBT-ED outperforms pharmacotherapy. Would be correct only after CBT-ED failure (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Olanzapine is not registered or recommended for BED and adds metabolic risk in already obese patient. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.B: Fluoxetine is evidence-based at 60 mg for bulimia, not BED; SSRIs have modest effect for BED but lisdexamfetamine has TGA-registered BED indication. Would be correct in bulimia, not BED (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Benzodiazepines not indicated for BED, carry dependence risk, and may disinhibit eating. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Quetiapine has no BED indication, causes metabolic weight gain, and worsens obesity comorbidity. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: RTA-1 produces hypokalaemic ACIDOSIS (low bicarbonate); her bicarbonate is high (34), indicating alkalosis. Would be correct with low bicarbonate (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.C: Active diuretic use produces HIGH urine chloride (over 20); her urine chloride is 8, consistent with vomiting. Would be correct with urine Cl over 20 (Source: KDIGO).
rationales.D: Conn typically has hypertension and suppressed renin; she is normotensive with no primary mineralocorticoid excess features. Would be correct with HTN and suppressed renin (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Chronic laxative abuse produces ACIDOSIS from colonic bicarbonate loss, not the alkalosis seen here. Would be correct with metabolic acidosis (Source: ANZAED).

rationales.B: AN requires fear of weight gain or persistent behaviour preventing weight gain AND body image disturbance; he has neither. Would be correct with weight fear and body image disturbance (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: Normal picky eating does not cause faltering growth, nutritional deficiency or psychosocial impairment; his anaemia, growth failure and social withdrawal exceed normal. Would be correct without nutritional impact (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: ARFID is the diagnosis when restrictive intake produces nutritional/functional consequences; specific phobia would only apply to circumscribed phobic avoidance. Would be correct in pure circumscribed food phobia without ARFID criteria (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: Pica requires persistent eating of non-nutritive non-food substances; he restricts to narrow food range without non-food consumption. Would be correct with non-food consumption (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Cosmetic surgery for BDD is a recognised harm anti-pattern; preoccupation typically migrates to a new body part and dissatisfaction recurs. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP BDD).
rationales.B: Antipsychotic monotherapy is not first-line; reserved for SSRI-resistant BDD or where delusional intensity is prominent, as augmentation. Would be correct as augmentation after SSRI failure (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Benzodiazepines are not disease-modifying in BDD and risk dependence in chronic-course illness. Would be correct only as short-term adjunct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Reassurance is ineffective and worsens BDD by reinforcing checking; evidence-based is ERP-CBT plus high-dose SSRI. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Baby blues affects up to 80 percent of mothers, peaks day 3-5, resolves by day 10, with lability/tearfulness WITHOUT psychosis or thoughts of infant harm. Would be correct in classic baby blues without psychotic features (Source: COPE).
rationales.B: Postpartum psychosis not postnatal depression; SSRI monotherapy is inadequate and outpatient is unsafe given infant safety concerns. Would be correct only in PND without psychotic features (Source: COPE).
rationales.C: Postpartum psychosis is psychiatric emergency with infant safety implications; delaying inpatient risks serious harm. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.E: Benzodiazepine sedation does not address psychosis or infant safety; admission mandatory. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

rationales.A: Abrupt lithium discontinuation precipitates relapse ~50 percent within 6 months, particularly dangerous with severe bipolar and prior ECT. Would never be correct in severe bipolar (Source: COPE).
rationales.B: Sodium valproate has HIGHEST teratogenicity of mood stabilisers (~10 percent major malformations including NTDs, IQ reduction up to 9 points); contraindicated in pregnancy. Would never be correct in pregnancy (Source: TGA Valproate PPP).
rationales.C: Carbamazepine carries ~3 percent NTD risk and other malformations; not safer than lithium in severe case with treatment-resistant history. Would be correct only if lithium intolerance and lower-risk profile (Source: COPE).
rationales.D: Episodic discontinuation increases relapse without clear advantage; current AU guidance is to continue lowest effective dose with close monitoring. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

rationales.B: EPDS 13+ is positive screen requiring further assessment; score 14 with anhedonia and tearfulness should not be dismissed. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.C: Item 10 score 1 (hardly ever) requires direct clinical inquiry to clarify intent/means but does not automatically mandate ED referral. Would be correct only with high item 10 with active suicidality (Source: COPE).
rationales.D: EPDS is screening tool not diagnostic; diagnosis requires clinical interview, and venlafaxine is not first-line in breastfeeding (sertraline preferred). Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.E: EPDS is validated antenatally and postnatally including early postpartum; COPE recommends screening at 6-12 weeks routinely. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

rationales.A: Fluoxetine and long-half-life norfluoxetine (t1/2 7-15 days) accumulate in breastmilk/infant plasma more than alternatives, producing higher relative infant dose. Would be correct only if started before delivery and well-tolerated (Source: LactMed).
rationales.C: Citalopram has TGA QT-prolongation dose cap and higher relative infant dose than sertraline; not first-line in breastfeeding. Would be correct only if sertraline not tolerated (Source: TGA; LactMed).
rationales.D: Venlafaxine is SNRI not SSRI, and not first-line in breastfeeding; reserved for SSRI failure. Would be correct only after SSRI failure (Source: LactMed).
rationales.E: Doxepin is TCA with reported neonatal sedation and respiratory depression in breastfeeding; contraindicated in lactation. Would never be correct (Source: LactMed).

rationales.A: Glucose 3.5 is within normal range for term neonate (over 2.6); jitteriness is not glycaemic. Would be correct with BSL under 2.6 (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.B: Septic screen negative, afebrile, normal sats/RR; sepsis remains differential but unlikely with this clinical picture. Would be correct with positive cultures (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.D: HIE features sentinel hypoxic event, low Apgars, abnormal cord gases with depressed consciousness or seizures; her Apgars (8,9) and cord pH (7.32) are normal. Would be correct with low Apgars and abnormal cord gas (Source: ANZNN).
rationales.E: Opioid withdrawal requires maternal opioid exposure; none reported. Picture mimics neonatal adaptation but excluded by exposure history. Would be correct with opioid exposure (Source: RCH CPG).

rationales.A: Stability does not exempt patient from PPP; TGA program is mandatory annually and requires written acknowledgement, documented contraception, and pregnancy testing. Would never be correct (Source: TGA Valproate PPP).
rationales.B: Abrupt valproate cessation in patient with two prior manic admissions carries high relapse risk; planned switch to alternative needed. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.C: Topiramate is also teratogenic (cleft palate, low birth weight) and not first-line for bipolar prophylaxis; choosing without contraception or shared decision-making fails PPP. Would be correct only with proper PPP adherence and contraception (Source: TGA).
rationales.E: Tubal ligation is one option but NOT only acceptable; LARC (implant, IUD) meets PPP threshold and preserves fertility choice. Would be correct only if patient chooses tubal ligation (Source: TGA).

rationales.A: PDD requires depressed mood most days for at least 2 years; 6-week course excludes this. Would be correct with 2+ year duration (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: Adjustment disorder is excluded once full MDE criteria are met; her symptom cluster meets MDE. Would be correct without meeting full MDE criteria (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: Meets MDE but specific cluster (loss of mood reactivity, distinct quality, marked psychomotor retardation, early morning waking, diurnal variation) indicates melancholic specifier. Would be correct without melancholic features (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: Bipolar II requires at least one prior hypomanic episode lasting 4+ days; explicitly absent. Would be correct with documented hypomanic episode (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Mirtazapine has lower sexual side-effect burden and helps sleep but causes 2-4 kg weight gain and daytime sedation, poorly suited to apprentice work at heights. Would be correct in elderly or insomniac patient with no occupational concerns (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: SNRI not first-line for uncomplicated moderate MDD; more difficult discontinuation profile and dose-dependent BP rise. Reserved for SSRI failure or specific indications. Would be correct after SSRI failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Reasonable SSRI but TGA QT-prolongation dose cap of 40 mg (20 in over-65 or on CYP2C19 inhibitor) limits future titration. Would be correct without family arrhythmia or QT concerns (Source: TGA).
rationales.E: Acceptable SSRI but long half-life (norfluoxetine 7-15 days) complicates later switch to MAOI; sertraline preferred when family history may bear on switching. Would be correct in adolescents (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Dantrolene is for malignant hyperthermia, not recommended in serotonin syndrome; intubation is for refractory hyperthermia or airway compromise, neither present. Would be correct in malignant hyperthermia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Antipsychotics with dopamine antagonism can worsen autonomic instability and risk NMS; benzodiazepines are preferred sedative in serotonin toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: ALL serotonergic agents must be ceased (SSRI, tramadol, linezolid); paracetamol does not lower temperature (hyperthermia is muscle hyperactivity, not set-point). Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: No neck stiffness, headache, focal neurology; clonus plus serotonergic drug history meets Hunter criteria; LP would delay treatment. Would be correct only with meningism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Already failed adequate trials of SSRI and SNRI; third monotherapy switch is less likely to produce remission than augmentation per STAR*D-style sequencing. Would be correct only with poor tolerability of current regimen (Source: STAR*D).
rationales.B: Combining MAOI with SNRI is contraindicated (serotonin syndrome, hypertensive crisis); SNRI must cease with washout before MAOI. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: St John's wort is serotonergic; combining with SNRI risks serotonin syndrome; also induces CYP3A4 with multiple interactions. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Abrupt venlafaxine cessation causes severe discontinuation; immediate switch to MAOI without washout (at least 1 week) risks serotonin syndrome. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: ECT has NO absolute contraindications per RANZCP; recent MI (under 3 months), raised ICP and unstable aneurysms are relative; remote stable MI is not absolute. Would be correct only with recent MI under 3 months (Source: RANZCP ECT).
rationales.C: Lack of capacity does not preclude ECT; substitute decision-makers and tribunal authorisation pathways exist under state Mental Health Act. Would never be correct as absolute bar (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Antidepressants are typically continued during ECT with case-by-case decisions; routine cessation not standard. Would be correct only for specific agents lowering seizure threshold (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Pre-ECT cognitive assessment is standard and should be repeated; MMSE 24 establishes baseline. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Multiple high-risk features (specific plan, lethal means access, chosen date/location, recent stressors, hopelessness, final acts) make discharge unsafe. Would never be correct in active high-risk suicidality (Source: RANZCP Suicide Prevention).
rationales.B: Dose escalation does not address acute 24-h risk; max escitalopram is 20 mg (10 mg elderly) and antidepressants can transiently worsen suicidality in young adults. Would never be correct as primary intervention (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: He is not agitated, aggressive or psychotic; chemical restraint is for behavioural emergencies, not suicide prevention. Would never be correct as primary strategy (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: No-suicide contracts lack evidence of efficacy and may give false reassurance; lay supervision does not equate to clinical safety with loaded firearm access. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: BPI requires manic episode at least 7 days OR requiring hospitalisation, often with marked impairment or psychosis; her elevated episodes are 5 days, non-impairing. Would be correct only with full mania (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: Cyclothymia requires 2 years of numerous hypomanic and depressive symptoms NOT meeting full criteria; she has full episodes. Would be correct only with subthreshold episodes (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: Clear observable recurrent decreased need for sleep, increased goal activity, talkativeness and disinhibition exceed hypomania threshold; requires bipolar-spectrum diagnosis. Would be correct without hypomanic episodes (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: BPD has pervasive emotional instability hours-to-days, not discrete 5-day mood states; identity disturbance and abandonment fears not described. Would be correct in classic BPD (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Antidepressants are contraindicated in acute mania (prolong/worsen episode); benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat underlying illness. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.B: Lithium has slow antimanic onset (5-7 days); monotherapy is inadequate for severe acute psychotic mania. Would be correct only in mild mania (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Valproate monotherapy is option for moderate mania but combination with antipsychotic preferred for severe psychotic mania; valproate is high-risk in reproductive-age women. Would be correct only as adjunct in non-reproductive setting (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Lamotrigine has no efficacy in acute mania; for prevention of bipolar depression with slow titration to mitigate SJS risk. Would be correct for bipolar depression prevention (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: These are not routine pre-lithium investigations; standard serum creatinine/eGFR adequate for renal screening. Would never be correct (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Baseline lithium is irrelevant before initiation; renal function plus calcium plus ECG must also be obtained. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Lithium is renally cleared, not hepatically metabolised; LFTs and ammonia not required. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: Baseline TFTs and calcium are required at initiation to establish reference; monitored at 6-12 monthly intervals. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium and is not used; lithium must be withheld not dose-reduced. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Urinary alkalinisation has minimal effect on lithium elimination; volume replacement is supportive but dialysis is for severe toxicity. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Continuing lithium in severe toxicity is dangerous; priority is removal and renal protection. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: All three (indapamide, NSAID, dehydration) must be addressed; thiazides increase proximal tubular lithium reabsorption raising levels. Would never be correct as partial (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: Postpartum psychosis NOT PND; SSRI monotherapy and outpatient do not address imminent risk to mother/baby. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.B: Afebrile, no neuro deficit, normal CRP and strong bipolar family history make PP most likely; LP not first step. Would be correct only with infective features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Discharge of psychotic mother with infant at home is unsafe; benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychosis. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.E: EPDS screens for depression not psychosis; non-urgent referral is unsafe given imminent risk. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

rationales.A: EPDS 14 with concordant clinical history of MDD-range symptoms is above COPE threshold (13+) mandating clinical assessment and intervention. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.B: Crisis referral is for acute suicide/infanticide risk, psychosis, or severe functional collapse; she has none. Would be correct only with crisis features (Source: COPE).
rationales.C: EPDS is screening tool not diagnostic; once positive with confirmed symptoms, step is structured diagnostic assessment plus intervention. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.E: Stopping breastfeeding not required for first-line SSRI (sertraline); venlafaxine not first-line for PND; psychotherapy must be considered. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

rationales.A: Benzodiazepines not first-line in GAD (dependence risk, chronic course); short courses only for acute crisis. Would be correct only as short-term acute crisis bridge (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Trauma-focused CBT is for PTSD not GAD; she has no trauma. Generic CBT for GAD first-line for mild-moderate but GAD-7 15 with significant impairment warrants combined therapy. Would be correct in PTSD (Source: Phoenix Australia).
rationales.D: Mirtazapine not first-line for GAD; SSRI/SNRI are first-line. Mirtazapine reserved for SSRI failure or specific sleep/appetite profile. Would be correct after SSRI failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Lifestyle alone insufficient for GAD-7 15 with functional impairment; mandates structured psychotherapy with/without SSRI. Would be correct only in mild GAD (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: GAD has pervasive worry on most days for 6+ months across multiple domains, not discrete episodic surges with phobic avoidance. Would be correct in pervasive worry pattern (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: Plasma free metanephrines are screening test of choice and are normal; sustained/paroxysmal HTN, headaches, flushing also absent. Would be correct with elevated metanephrines and HTN (Source: APEG).
rationales.C: Holter and ECGs in symptomatic visits have been normal; in absence of ECG documentation, panic disorder is most likely. Would be correct with ECG-documented paroxysmal AF (Source: Cardiology).
rationales.E: TSH is normal (1.8); no tremor, heat intolerance or weight loss to support thyrotoxicosis. Would be correct with suppressed TSH (Source: APEG).

rationales.A: OCPD is pervasive lifelong pattern of perfectionism/rigidity with limited insight (ego-syntonic); she lacks baseline perfectionism profile. Would be correct in lifelong perfectionism pattern (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: GAD has broad worry without ritualised compulsions or trauma re-experiencing; does not fully account. Would be correct in classic GAD (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: PTSD captures re-experiencing/avoidance/hyperarousal but not ritualised compulsions which independently fit OCD; both present. Would never be correct alone (Source: Phoenix Australia).
rationales.E: Adjustment disorder applies when stressor causes symptoms NOT meeting another disorder; she meets criteria for two specific DSM-5 disorders. Would never be correct (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Benzodiazepines not recommended in PTSD (impair fear extinction critical to trauma-focused therapy, worsen outcomes). Would never be correct (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD).
rationales.C: SSRIs and venlafaxine are second-line behind trauma-focused psychotherapy; reserved for psychotherapy unavailable, ineffective or refused. Would be correct only after psychotherapy unavailable (Source: Phoenix Australia).
rationales.D: Antipsychotics not first-line for core PTSD; low-dose quetiapine for sleep is unsupported with metabolic/movement-disorder risks. Would never be correct (Source: Phoenix Australia).
rationales.E: Single-session debriefing not recommended and may worsen outcomes; structured trauma-focused therapy over multiple sessions required. Would never be correct (Source: Phoenix Australia).

rationales.B: Benzodiazepines impair fine motor performance and cognition; not preferred where sensorimotor task required. Would be correct only in non-motor performance with high distress (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: SSRIs are first-line for generalised social anxiety but take 4-6 weeks and can paradoxically worsen anxiety in first 1-2 weeks; not appropriate for single performance. Would be correct in generalised SAD (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Quetiapine not indicated for performance anxiety; sedation impairs fine motor control and metabolic effects outweigh benefit. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: CBD does not have established evidence for performance anxiety in AU; quality control and PBS access limiting. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Onset within days of cessation, somatic and sensory features (brain zaps, dizziness) fit discontinuation syndrome not relapse (which takes weeks). Would be correct only if symptoms weeks after cessation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Afebrile, no neck stiffness, no headache, no rash, clear pharmacological explanation. Would be correct with meningism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Mild nausea without vomiting/diarrhoea and normal bloods do not warrant admission, and misses diagnosis. Would be correct only with significant GI illness (Source: RCH CPG).
rationales.E: No evidence of inadequate treatment response; reinstating previous effective dose is appropriate. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Treatment response history informs choice but is not part of melancholic specifier criteria. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).
