rationales.B: These describe atypical features (mood reactivity, increased appetite/weight, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis), the opposite specifier. Would be correct for atypical specifier (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: Demographic factors not part of specifier criteria, although melancholic is more common in older adults. Would never be a criterion (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: Suicidal ideation contributes to severity but is not melancholic-specifier feature. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Adding TCA to SSRI risks elevated TCA levels (CYP2D6 inhibition) and is not the recommended next step in atypical depression; evidence favours MAOI. Would be correct after MAOI contraindication (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Exceeding licensed maximum dose risks QT prolongation without proven benefit; bupropion not registered for depression in AU (only Zyban for smoking cessation). Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
rationales.D: Mirtazapine causes further weight gain and sedation, worsening existing weight gain and hypersomnia; lacks specific evidence for atypical. Would be correct only without weight/sleep concerns (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: T4 augmentation in euthyroid patient has no evidence for atypical features and risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis; T3 is the augmentation agent in TRD. Would be correct only with T3 (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Recurrent self-harm is consistent with BPD but not specific (also MDD, adjustment). Not strongest discriminator. Would never be the strongest discriminator alone (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline).
rationales.C: Crisis pattern in response to relational stressors is consistent with BPD but not specific; many MDD patients present in interpersonal crisis. Would never be the discriminator (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.D: Emptiness is part of BPD but also reported in chronic depression; alone does not discriminate. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.E: Mood reactivity argues AGAINST melancholic MDD but does not distinguish BPD from atypical-features MDD. Would never be the BPD-vs-MDD discriminator (Source: NHMRC).

rationales.A: Mood stabilisers including lamotrigine do not have strong evidence in BPD; NHMRC explicitly recommends against routine pharmacotherapy for core BPD. Would be correct only for comorbid bipolar disorder (Source: NHMRC BPD).
rationales.C: Second-gen antipsychotics not recommended as first-line maintenance for BPD (metabolic risk, limited evidence beyond short-term crisis). Would be correct only as short-term crisis use (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.D: Two prior SSRI trials failed and SSRIs do not treat core BPD; further antidepressant trials reserved for clearly comorbid MDD. Would be correct only with new comorbid MDD episode (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.E: Prolonged inpatient admission not recommended for BPD (reinforces maladaptive coping); brief crisis admissions only when safety cannot be managed in community. Would be correct only for brief crisis (Source: NHMRC).

rationales.A: Substance use common in both ASPD and BPD; not discriminating. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: Brief unstable relationships occur in both; ASPD ends out of exploitation/boredom, BPD out of abandonment fear or devaluation; relational pattern is the discriminator. Would never be the strongest discriminator alone (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: Irritability is part of both ASPD and BPD criteria; not specific. Would never be discriminating (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: Glibness/superficial charm is in Hare's PCL-R psychopathy construct but not DSM-5 ASPD criterion; raises probability but not most specific. Would be specific only in PCL-R framework (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Histrionic PD has dramatic, sexually provocative or shallow-affect attention-seeking; he is grandiose and contemptuous rather than dramatic/seductive. Would be correct in dramatic/seductive presentation (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: BPD requires identity disturbance, abandonment fear, splitting and impulsive self-damage - all absent. Sustained competent functioning is not BPD pattern. Would be correct in unstable identity/relationships pattern (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: ASPD requires childhood conduct disorder (criterion C) and adult pattern of disregard for others' rights/laws; no childhood conduct history, no criminal pattern. Would be correct with conduct disorder before 15 (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: OCPD has rigid perfectionism, preoccupation with rules/lists, miserliness, over-conscientiousness; he shows grandiosity and contempt, not perfectionism. Would be correct in perfectionist pattern (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Order and control feature in both OCPD and symmetry/ordering subtype of OCD; not strongest discriminator alone. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: Hoarding can occur in OCPD, hoarding disorder and occasionally OCD; without ego-syntonic/dystonic context does not discriminate. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: Reluctance to delegate is OCPD criterion but can arise from anxiety in OCD; supportive not decisive. Would never alone be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: High productivity may occur in either OCPD or unaffected high-functioning workers; not DSM-5 criterion in isolation. Would never be the discriminator (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.B: Routine short admissions for chronic low-lethality self-harm in BPD are not recommended; NHMRC favours community management with brief admissions only when essential. Would be correct only for safety crisis not manageable in community (Source: NHMRC BPD).
rationales.C: Involuntary admission requires mental illness plus risk not manageable less restrictively; she has engaged voluntarily with safety plan and trusted clinician. Would never be correct in this scenario (Source: Mental Health Act).
rationales.D: Pharmacotherapy not recommended for core BPD; antipsychotic at discharge after low-lethality overdose adds metabolic risk without benefit. Would never be correct (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.E: AU DBT is predominantly outpatient/day-programme; inpatient DBT is not standard; her existing outpatient clinician should continue care. Would never be correct as standard (Source: NHMRC).

rationales.A: MMSE detects cognitive impairment but cannot confirm delirium (does not capture acute onset, fluctuation or attentional disturbance specifically). Would be correct only as part of broader CAM-based assessment (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.C: MoCA detects MCI better than MMSE but does not specifically diagnose delirium (no acute onset/fluctuation capture). Would never alone confirm delirium (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.D: GCS measures consciousness and is insensitive to attentional and thinking disturbance characterising hyperactive delirium with normal arousal. Would be correct only for impaired conscious level (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.E: GDS screens for depression in older adults; depression can mimic delirium but is not diagnosed by fluctuating attentional picture. Would be correct only for depression (Source: ACSQHC).

rationales.A: Acute onset, fluctuating attention and disorganised thinking is hypoactive delirium not depression; starting antidepressant first misses underlying medical cause. Would be correct in confirmed depression (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.B: Antipsychotics are reserved for delirium with severe agitation or distress risking harm; hypoactive presentations should not be routinely treated with antipsychotics. Would be correct only with severe agitation (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.D: CT brain is for delirium with focal neurology, head injury, anticoagulation or unexplained drop in conscious level; routine imaging not first-line. Would be correct only with focal features (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.E: ECT not first-line in this acute medical context; diagnosis is not depression and ECT is for severe treatment-resistant or psychotic depression. Would never be correct in delirium (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Preserved interest argues against major depression but does not distinguish dementia from delirium; delirium can have preserved interest. Would never be the discriminator (Source: Dementia Australia).
rationales.B: Low MMSE shows cognitive impairment but cannot distinguish delirium from dementia or depression-related cognitive impairment alone. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: Dementia Australia).
rationales.C: Cortical atrophy on CT is common in older adults and supports neurodegeneration but is non-specific; does not distinguish from delirium in comorbid case. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.E: Getting lost is suggestive but can occur in delirium with disorientation; temporal profile is more diagnostic than single deficit. Would never alone be the strongest discriminator (Source: Dementia Australia).

rationales.A: Antipsychotics in BPSD are for severe distress or risk of harm not responsive to non-pharmacological measures; this patient has treatable pain hook and antipsychotics should follow first-line measures. Would be correct only after first-line failure with severe distress (Source: AMH).
rationales.B: Benzodiazepines worsen confusion, falls and fracture risk in older adults with dementia; not first-line for BPSD. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Donepezil not licensed at 23 mg in Australia; dose escalation is not first response to BPSD. Would never be correct in AU (Source: AMH; TGA).
rationales.D: SSRIs (especially citalopram) have modest BPSD evidence but should follow non-pharmacological measures; citalopram over 20 mg in older adults raises QT risk. Would be correct only after first-line failure (Source: AMH).

rationales.B: Olanzapine 10 mg is standard adult dose, not low-dose-elderly; indefinite continuation is contrary to guideline to review and deprescribe at 12 weeks. Would be correct only at lower dose with scheduled review (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Haloperidol has higher EPSE and QT risk in older adults; not first-line for BPSD, reserved for short-term severe delirium-related agitation. Would be correct only in short-term severe agitation (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Quetiapine has weaker evidence in BPSD outside Parkinson dementia/DLB, and 100 mg BD exceeds cautious starting dose in older adults. Would be correct only at lower dose for DLB/Parkinson dementia (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: Benzodiazepines worsen confusion, gait, falls and pneumonia risk in older adults; not appropriate for psychotic features. Would never be correct (Source: Choosing Wisely).

rationales.A: Typical antipsychotics including haloperidol can produce severe neuroleptic sensitivity reactions in DLB (worsening Parkinsonism, severe sedation, autonomic instability). Would never be correct in DLB (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Risperidone is licensed for BPSD generally but should be avoided in DLB due to severe neuroleptic sensitivity; low-dose quetiapine preferred if any antipsychotic required. Would be correct in non-DLB BPSD (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Levodopa can worsen visual hallucinations in DLB even though it may improve Parkinsonism; trade-offs must be weighed and not first response to hallucinations. Would be correct only with motor priority (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: Benzodiazepines worsen falls and confusion in DLB and do not treat the underlying neuropsychiatric symptoms. Would never be correct (Source: Choosing Wisely).

rationales.A: Major NCD (dementia) requires significant functional impairment which this man does not have; cholinesterase inhibitors not licensed for MCI. Would be correct only with functional impairment (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: Objective cognitive impairment beyond age expectation on MoCA with corroborated functional change excludes normal ageing. Would never be correct (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: Vascular dementia requires evidence of cerebrovascular disease and stepwise decline; no infarcts on imaging and gradual course; already on statin. Would be correct with imaging-confirmed cerebrovascular disease (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.E: Subjective cognitive decline applies when complaint exists without objective deficit; MoCA shows objective impairment moving classification to MCI. Would be correct with normal MoCA (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Capacity is not determined by cognitive test score; MoCA cut-off is for screening cognitive impairment, not capacity. Would never be correct (Source: state Guardianship Acts).
rationales.B: Dementia diagnosis does not equal incapacity; many with mild dementia retain decision-specific capacity. Assumption of incapacity is contrary to AU principles. Would never be correct (Source: Guardianship Acts).
rationales.C: Substitute decision-makers are engaged only when person lacks capacity; defaulting to family bypasses autonomy. Would be correct only after demonstrated lack of capacity (Source: Guardianship Acts).
rationales.E: MHA does not authorise consent to surgical procedures; for incapable patients without advance directive or guardian, guardianship pathways apply. Would never be correct (Source: MHA).

rationales.A: TCAs are avoided in older adults (anticholinergic load: cognition, falls, constipation, urinary retention) and overdose toxicity. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: AMH).
rationales.B: Citalopram doses above 20 mg in older adults not recommended (QT risk); 40 mg as starting dose is contraindicated. Would be correct only at start dose 10 mg (Source: TGA).
rationales.C: Venlafaxine has dose-dependent HTN and discontinuation effects; 150 mg starting dose in older adult with CKD and antihypertensives is too high. Would be correct only at lower starting dose (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Mirtazapine can be useful when insomnia/weight loss dominate but 45 mg is too high a starting dose in older adult (typical start 15 mg). Would be correct at 15 mg start (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: ACT has growing evidence in perinatal populations but not first-line per COPE for postnatal MDD; reserved as alternative if IPT/CBT unavailable. Would be correct only if first-line unavailable (Source: COPE).
rationales.C: Insufficient RCT evidence in postnatal depression; not first-line. Would never be correct as first-line (Source: COPE).
rationales.D: EMDR is for PTSD, not postnatal MDD without trauma history. Would be correct with PTSD (Source: Phoenix Australia).
rationales.E: DBT is for BPD and chronic self-harm, not postnatal MDD. Would be correct in BPD (Source: NHMRC BPD).

rationales.A: Precontemplation = no awareness; he has acknowledged 'might be a bit too much', which is awareness without commitment. That is contemplation. Would be correct only without any acknowledgement (Source: Prochaska/DiClemente).
rationales.B: Preparation requires intention to act within 30 days and concrete planning; he has neither. Jumping to quit date risks engagement loss. Would be correct only with stated intention and planning (Source: Prochaska).
rationales.D: Action requires active behaviour change within last 6 months; he has not yet started. Would be correct only after change initiated (Source: Prochaska).
rationales.E: Maintenance follows 6+ months of sustained behaviour change; not applicable. Would be correct only after sustained change (Source: Prochaska).

rationales.A: Two prior CBT courses with partial transient response; repeating same modality without changing formulation unlikely to produce durable change. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders).
rationales.B: Supportive counselling is adjunctive but not structured first-line therapy for recurrent depression with relational patterns. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: DBT is first-line for BPD and recurrent self-harm, not recurrent MDD with relational patterns without personality disorder features. Would be correct in BPD (Source: NHMRC BPD).
rationales.E: ACT has evidence in chronic pain and treatment-resistant anxiety; reasonable alternative but psychodynamic better matched for recurrent depression with relational patterns. Would be correct as alternative (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Enduring guardians make substitute decisions only when person lacks capacity for that particular decision; if patient has capacity, refusal stands. Would be correct only after capacity assessment confirms incapacity (Source: Guardianship Acts).
rationales.B: MHA addresses mental illness with risk not manageable less restrictively; does not authorise placement decisions for cognitive impairment without mental illness. Would never be correct (Source: MHA).
rationales.C: Discharge requires assessment of safe destination, supports, and (if capacity in question) documented capacity assessment; immediate discharge bypasses this. Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.D: Infection has resolved; ongoing antibiotics expose to harm without benefit and do not resolve discharge question. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: RANZCP schedule explicitly includes fasting glucose (or HbA1c) and lipids at 12 weeks, plus anthropometry and BP; highest-risk metabolic changes emerge in first 12 weeks. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP Metabolic Monitoring).
rationales.C: OGTT, fasting insulin and HOMA-IR are not part of routine RANZCP schedule; fasting glucose/HbA1c plus lipids sufficient at 12 weeks. Would be correct only in research protocols (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Not part of metabolic monitoring schedule; prolactin monitored only on prolactin-elevating antipsychotics (risperidone, amisulpride); 24-h ABPM not routine. Would never be correct as a metabolic panel (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Olanzapine carries highest metabolic-burden signal among SGAs; first 12 weeks is when emergent dyslipidaemia and impaired fasting glucose appear. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Abrupt clozapine cessation in responder without safety crisis risks rapid psychotic relapse and rebound psychosis; metabolic changes are significant but not life-threatening immediately. Would be correct only in life-threatening adverse event (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: 11 percent weight gain with new dysglycaemia and dyslipidaemia at 12 weeks meets RANZCP threshold for intensified monitoring and active intervention, not deferral. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Halving effective clozapine risks loss of response; not the recommended first step for metabolic adverse effects (pharmacological and lifestyle adjuncts first). Would never be correct as first step (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: No symptoms or signs of myocarditis (no chest pain, dyspnoea, fever, tachycardia, raised troponin/CRP); urgent echo with cessation is not warranted. Would be correct only with myocarditis features (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Topiramate has modest evidence for antipsychotic-induced weight gain and is third-line after lifestyle and metformin; with established T2DM, switching the offending agent is preferred. Would be correct as third-line adjunct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.B: Dose reduction has only weak effect on weight in established olanzapine-induced metabolic syndrome and risks symptomatic relapse. Would never be correct as primary intervention (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Chlorpromazine is high-burden for weight, sedation, anticholinergic load and adds postural hypotension; not a metabolically favourable switch. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: Bariatric surgery may have role in selected cases but is not first-line ahead of switching to metabolically favourable antipsychotic; continuing offending drug persists root cause. Would be correct only after failed switch and intensive lifestyle (Source: NHMRC).

rationales.A: Non-selective NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance via afferent arteriole prostaglandin effect; combined with ACE inhibitor and thiazide-like risks toxicity. Would be correct only without lithium (Source: AMH).
rationales.B: COX-2 selective agents also reduce renal lithium clearance and are not safe substitute when avoiding lithium toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Naproxen interacts strongly with lithium; topical diclofenac has some systemic absorption and does not solve interaction. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Abruptly switching long-stable mood stabiliser for short-term analgesia exposes to relapse and valproate toxicity; not proportionate. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Coarse tremor is key feature but non-specific; combination with cerebellar signs is most specific to lithium neurotoxicity. Would never alone be the most specific (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Fever is not a feature of lithium toxicity and points to NMS, serotonin syndrome, or sepsis; he is afebrile. Would be correct only in NMS/serotonin syndrome (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Lead-pipe rigidity and autonomic instability characterise NMS not lithium neurotoxicity; he has no rigidity. Would be correct in NMS (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Clonus and ocular clonus are hallmark of serotonin syndrome, not lithium neurotoxicity. Would be correct in serotonin syndrome (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: AU valproate PPP requires highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal) for ALL females of reproductive potential before/during valproate; no change is non-compliant. Would never be correct (Source: TGA Valproate PPP).
rationales.B: PPP responsibility sits with prescriber; counselling without ensuring effective contraception and signed acknowledgement does not satisfy framework. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
rationales.D: Abrupt cessation of effective mood stabiliser after recent manic episode risks relapse; safer pathway is overlap switch. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental risk are dose-related but present at all doses; halving does not satisfy PPP. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).

rationales.A: Valproate major malformation rate is ~10 percent (3-4 times background), not 1 percent. Would never be correct (Source: TGA; AMH).
rationales.B: Major structural malformations including NTDs arise from first-trimester exposure during organogenesis; neurodevelopmental harm spans pregnancy. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
rationales.C: High-dose folate is recommended for women on AEDs but does not eliminate valproate teratogenicity; mitigates not eliminates risk. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
rationales.E: Foetal valproate syndrome includes irreversible structural malformations and persistent neurodevelopmental impairment; not reversible. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).

rationales.A: Oral charcoal is contraindicated with declining GCS and unprotected airway; observation alone misses urgent indication for bicarbonate. Would be correct only in alert protected airway within 1-2 h (Source: NSW PIC).
rationales.B: Flumazenil is contraindicated in TCA overdose (precipitates seizures and arrhythmias by removing GABAergic protection). Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Class IA and IC antiarrhythmics worsen sodium channel blockade in TCA toxicity; bicarbonate is the antidote. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).
rationales.D: Magnesium is for torsades from QT prolongation; in TCA overdose the dominant lethal mechanism is QRS prolongation from sodium channel block. Would be correct in torsades (Source: NSW PIC).

rationales.B: Waiting for email response delays time-critical decisions; toxicology advice is immediately available by phone. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).
rationales.C: General reference apps may not reflect AU-specific advice; clinician-to-toxicologist captures case nuances apps cannot. Would never be correct as definitive (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Discharge of any symptomatic TCA ingestion is not appropriate; QRS can progress, dose is uncertain, minimum 6-h observation with serial ECGs is standard. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).
rationales.E: Intralipid is for refractory cardiovascular collapse from local anaesthetic or some lipophilic drug toxicities; not first-line in stable TCA. Would be correct only in refractory collapse (Source: NSW PIC).

rationales.A: ANC 1.6 is below AU green-zone threshold (>=2.0) and triggers amber-zone management. Would never be correct (Source: ClopineCentral Australia).
rationales.C: ANC 1.6 is amber, not red; immediate cessation is wrong threshold and would unnecessarily strip a TRS patient of effective drug. Would be correct only at ANC under 1.5 (Source: ClopineCentral).
rationales.D: Any drop below green requires accelerated monitoring; routine 4-weekly is unsafe at ANC 1.6 (progression to red zone risk). Would never be correct (Source: ClopineCentral).
rationales.E: Lithium augmentation to raise ANC is not standard of care; correct response is frequent monitoring per registry. Would never be correct as primary response (Source: ClopineCentral).

rationales.A: After 48+ h without clozapine, tolerance to hypotension, sedation and seizure thresholds is lost; restarting at full dose risks orthostasis, sedation, seizures. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.B: Halving then jumping to full next night skips standard retitration over several days and still risks haemodynamic compromise. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Long-term clozapine responder; switching off because of brief missed-dose period is not justified and risks loss of symptom control. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Splitting dose does not address tolerance loss; total daily exposure is excessive after 72 h off and unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: Wrong enzyme and wrong direction. Clozapine is metabolised primarily by CYP1A2 (CYP3A4 minor); PAHs in tobacco smoke induce CYP1A2. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.B: Nicotine is NOT the CYP1A2 inducer (PAHs are); NRT replaces nicotine for craving control but does not replace PAH-mediated enzyme induction. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Direction reversed: smoking INDUCES CYP1A2 (does not inhibit); cessation raises levels. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: False; this interaction is one of the most clinically important PAH-CYP1A2 interactions in psychiatry, with documented clozapine toxicity from quitting smoking. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: Thiazide contributes (and should be stopped) but temporal link (12 days after sertraline start) with euvolaemic biochemical SIADH pattern implicates SSRI. Would be correct only without temporal SSRI link (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Fluid intake is modest; biochemistry (urine osmolality 412 with serum osmolality 254 and urine Na 48) fits SIADH not primary polydipsia (which would have low urine osmolality). Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: No hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation or Addisonian features; aggressive saline could cause rapid correction and central pontine myelinolysis. Would be correct only with Addisonian features (Source: APEG).
rationales.D: CSW requires hypovolaemia and is usually after acute neuro injury; she is euvolaemic with no neuro insult. Would be correct in post-neurosurgical setting (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: NMS has lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, very high CK (often over 1000), slower onset; no rigidity here. Would be correct in NMS phenotype (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Lactate mildly raised and no clear source; focal clonus and ocular clonus point strongly to serotonin syndrome. Would be correct with documented infection (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Anticholinergic toxidrome has dry skin/mucosae, urinary retention, absent bowel sounds; he is diaphoretic with hyperactive bowels. Would be correct in anticholinergic pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: No focal neurological deficits; symmetric clonus, fever and autonomic features are not stroke. Thrombolysis would be wholly inappropriate. Would never be correct (Source: ANSCS).

rationales.A: Initiating antipsychotic without specialist assessment and formal early-intervention plan is below AU standard of care for FEP; specialist team needed. Would never be correct (Source: EPPIC; RANZCP).
rationales.C: Symptoms persist 4 weeks after last cannabis use with negative urine screen, exceeding typical resolution for substance-induced psychosis. Would be correct only with shorter symptom course (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: Routine neuroimaging and EEG are not required prior to treatment in young adult with typical FEP, normal neurology and normal screen. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Involuntary admission requires risk not manageable less restrictively; engaged with family, denies SI/HI, willing to accept treatment. Would never be correct (Source: MHA).

rationales.B: Risperidone causes intermediate weight gain and highest prolactin elevation of common atypicals, with sexual dysfunction and gynaecomastia that often drive non-adherence. Would be correct only without metabolic concerns (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Quetiapine is highly sedating with significant weight and metabolic risk; short half-life and high doses for antipsychotic efficacy. Would be correct only when sedation desired (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Amisulpride is effective and metabolically neutral but causes marked hyperprolactinaemia and is renally excreted; sedation and metabolic burden criteria favour aripiprazole. Would be correct in alternative settings (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after failure of at least 2 adequate trials; patient has not had 2 trials. Would be correct only in TRS (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.B: Benzodiazepines are second-line and slower onset; oral absorption is unreliable with oromandibular dystonia; definitive parenteral anticholinergic preferred. Would be correct only when anticholinergic contraindicated (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Propranolol treats akathisia not acute dystonia; akathisia is restless inability to sit still vs sustained muscle contractions. Would be correct in akathisia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Dantrolene with cooling is NMS management (hyperthermia, generalised rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness). Would be correct in NMS (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Cessation alone does not abort established dystonia (persists hours to days); targeted anticholinergic is standard. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Anticholinergics are effective for acute dystonia and drug-induced parkinsonism but only modest benefit in akathisia; may worsen cognition and cause urinary retention. Would be correct in dystonia/parkinsonism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Lithium does not treat akathisia; higher level increases tremor, nephrotoxicity and toxicity risk; mania is responding. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Clozapine is for TRS after failure of two adequate trials; not appropriate for first antipsychotic side effect. Would be correct only in TRS (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Untreated akathisia is strongly associated with suicidal ideation and non-adherence; expectant management is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Both NMS and severe serotonin syndrome cause hyperthermia above 40; temperature alone does not discriminate. Would never be the discriminator (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Rhabdomyolysis and AKI occur in severe forms of both syndromes; CK is supportive but not discriminating. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Both syndromes feature altered conscious state and autonomic instability; necessary but non-specific. Would never be discriminating (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Posturing can occur in NMS, malignant catatonia, and extreme serotonin toxicity with substantial overlap with catatonia; not the cleanest discriminator. Would never be the strongest (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Bromocriptine is dopamine agonist used in some NMS protocols, not serotonin toxicity; this patient's hyperreflexia, clonus and serotonergic medication history indicate serotonin syndrome. Would be correct in NMS (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Benztropine treats acute dystonia and parkinsonism from D2 blockade; no role in serotonin toxicity and anticholinergic effects can worsen hyperthermia. Would be correct in dystonia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Dantrolene and neuromuscular blockade are reserved for life-threatening hyperthermia over 41.5 not responding to supportive measures; aggressive escalation here is premature. Would be correct only at life-threatening hyperthermia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Both venlafaxine and tramadol are serotonergic; switching to another SNRI while continuing tramadol perpetuates toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Antipsychotic polypharmacy is not first-line for TRS and increases adverse effects without consistent efficacy benefit. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Deferring clozapine after two adequate trials prolongs symptom burden, functional impairment and suicide risk. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: ECT is for catatonia, severe depression with psychosis, or augmentation in clozapine-resistant; not first-line for TRS. Would be correct after clozapine failure (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: LAIs address adherence not efficacy; adherence has been confirmed, so issue is treatment resistance. Would be correct only with non-adherence (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Continuing clozapine in suspected myocarditis is contraindicated (progression to fulminant HF); aspirin alone is inadequate. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: ANC 4.6 is normal; agranulocytosis is under 0.5; clozapine is interrupted (Amber) at 1.0-1.5 and ceased at under 1.0. Would never be correct (Source: ClopineCentral).
rationales.D: Dose reduction without cessation does not address myocarditis; beta blockade can mask haemodynamic deterioration. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Gallop, raised troponin and normal lung exam point to myocarditis not pneumonia; treating CAP without addressing cardiac picture is wrong. Would be correct only with documented CAP (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Outpatient management with bulk laxative in established ileus risks bowel perforation; bulking agents can worsen impaction. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP; AMH).
rationales.C: Abrupt permanent cessation of clozapine in stable treatment-responsive patient risks severe relapse; standard is to manage GI complication. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Colonoscopy in suspected ileus or megacolon carries high perforation risk; AXR and CT abdomen preferred. Would never be correct as first investigation (Source: ANZ Gastro).
rationales.E: Metoclopramide is D2 antagonist that can cause EPSE and unlikely to reverse established large-bowel hypomotility; discharge unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: CIWA-Ar 22 is severe withdrawal; observation alone risks progression to seizures or DTs; oral thiamine is insufficient prophylaxis. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Anticonvulsants other than benzodiazepines are second-line and inferior for severe withdrawal; benzodiazepines are first-line. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Propofol infusion is for refractory DT in ICU; not appropriate on ward and not first-line. Would be correct in refractory DT in ICU (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Alcohol tapering is not used in AU inpatient practice; pharmacokinetic variability, hepatotoxicity and impaired titration make benzodiazepine-based withdrawal safer. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Standard 100 mg IM thiamine is inadequate for established Wernicke's; high-dose parenteral B-complex BD-TDS for several days required. Would be correct only for prophylaxis not established WE (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Correcting hypoglycaemia must continue with concurrent thiamine; magnesium without thiamine does not reverse Wernicke's. Would be correct as part of full regimen, not as monotherapy (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Wernicke's is clinical diagnosis; delay for imaging risks permanent Korsakoff amnesia; treatment must be empirical. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Oral absorption is unreliable in chronic alcohol use and inadequate for established encephalopathy; parenteral high-dose B-complex required. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Acamprosate is equally valid first-line (best for sustaining abstinence already achieved); naltrexone has stronger craving evidence. Would be correct when abstinence already achieved (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Disulfiram requires high motivation, supervised dosing and acceptance of aversive reaction; second-line. Would be correct after first-line failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Benzodiazepines are for acute withdrawal only; ongoing use risks cross-dependence and is not maintenance therapy. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Topiramate has some evidence but is off-label in AU and third-line; cognitive adverse effects notable in working woman. Would be correct only after first-line failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Severe hepatic impairment is contraindication; compensated Child-Pugh A is caution requiring LFT monitoring, not absolute bar. Would be correct only with severe impairment (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Metoprolol has no specific contraindication with disulfiram. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Age alone is not contraindication; risk-benefit individualised. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: No clinically significant interaction precluding co-prescription of atorvastatin with disulfiram. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.B: Methadone induction starts low (20-30 mg, no more than 40 mg day 1) due to long half-life and accumulation risk; 60 mg day 1 has caused fatal overdoses. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Health Methadone Guideline).
rationales.C: Oral naltrexone has very high relapse rates in opioid dependence and is not first-line maintenance; cannot start until 7-10 days opioid-free. Would be correct in highly motivated abstinence-focused patients (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Naloxone reverses overdose, not for confirming dependence; deliberate precipitation of withdrawal is unethical. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Health).
rationales.E: Day-1 buprenorphine should start at 2-8 mg with cautious top-up; 16 mg risks oversedation. Would be correct only at later titration (Source: NSW Health).

rationales.B: QTc 510 ms carries significant torsades risk and demands prompt action; passive review is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Increasing methadone worsens QT prolongation and overdose risk, particularly with co-ingested benzodiazepines. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Abrupt methadone cessation precipitates withdrawal and relapse; naltrexone cannot start until 7-10 days opioid-free. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Health).
rationales.E: Beta-blockers reduce risk in congenital LQTS but not standard response to drug-induced QT prolongation; correct approach is to identify and remove QT-prolonging drugs. Would be correct only in congenital LQTS (Source: AMH).

rationales.B: Oral medication is unsafe and impractical for severe ABD with imminent harm; parenteral in monitored area required. Would be correct only for mild agitation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Ketamine is reserved for refractory cases when droperidol and benzodiazepine have failed (laryngospasm, hypertension, airway risks). Would be correct only as refractory (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: IV haloperidol carries higher QT/torsades risk than IM droperidol and is not preferred in AU ABD protocols. Would be correct only if droperidol unavailable (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Prolonged physical restraint without sedation risks asphyxia, hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis and sudden death in stimulant intoxication. Would never be correct (Source: ANZICS).

rationales.B: Severe sleep deprivation, escalating manic behaviour and refusal of treatment make outpatient unsafe in next 24 h. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MHA).
rationales.C: Common-law restraint is for emergency intervention to prevent immediate harm, not primary framework for ongoing detention and treatment. Would be correct only briefly during emergency (Source: NSW MHA).
rationales.D: CTOs are made by MHRT following inpatient assessment with specific procedural steps; cannot be made directly from ED. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MHA).
rationales.E: She lacks capacity to refuse treatment due to grandiose delusions and lack of insight; MHA exists to address this. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MHA).

rationales.A: Anticholinergics do not improve and may worsen TD; benztropine helps acute dystonia and drug-induced parkinsonism, not TD. Would be correct in acute dystonia or parkinsonism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Polypharmacy increases adverse-effect burden including TD; combining second D2 antagonist worsens pathology. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Increasing offending antipsychotic transiently masks TD but worsens long-term course; contraindicated as management. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Abrupt cessation can cause withdrawal dyskinesia and risks psychotic relapse; switching to lower-risk agent preferred. Would never be correct as planned strategy (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: After 48+ h without clozapine, tolerance is lost; resuming previous full dose markedly increases orthostatic hypotension, seizure and sedation risk. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.C: Halving does not safely re-establish tolerance and still carries significant risk; protocol requires retitration from 12.5 mg. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Switching from clozapine should be planned (relapse risk high); unplanned switch is unsafe and not indicated for missed-dose period. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Anticonvulsant prophylaxis is considered for higher-risk patients during retitration but does not replace requirement to retitrate from start. Would be correct as adjunct, not replacement (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.B: Oral diazepam in confused, seizing, vomiting patient is unreliable; severe DT requires parenteral therapy and high-acuity monitoring. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and don't treat underlying GABA deficit; benzodiazepines are first-line in DT with low-dose antipsychotic only as adjunct. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Phenytoin is ineffective for alcohol-withdrawal seizures (GABA-mediated); benzodiazepines are both treatment and prophylaxis. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Naloxone has no role in alcohol withdrawal; it is opioid antagonist for opioid overdose. Would never be correct in alcohol withdrawal (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Illness anxiety disorder requires preoccupation with HAVING or acquiring a serious illness with minimal or absent somatic symptoms; her dominant feature is the symptoms themselves. Would be correct only with minimal somatic symptoms (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: GAD requires excessive worry across multiple domains for 6+ months with 3+ of restlessness/fatigue/concentration/irritability/muscle tension/sleep; somatic symptom focus is the discriminator. Would be correct in multi-domain worry pattern (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: FND requires altered voluntary motor/sensory function with positive incompatibility signs (Hoover's, tremor entrainment); abdominal pain and fatigue do not fit. Would be correct with positive incompatibility signs (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: Factitious requires intentional falsification or induction of signs/symptoms with deceptive intent without obvious external reward; absent here. Would be correct with deception (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Stroke is unlikely with normal MRI, symmetric reflexes, flexor plantar, positive Hoover's indicating functional weakness; antiplatelets in this setting harms without benefit. Would be correct in confirmed stroke (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: MS is excluded by normal MRI and positive functional sign; steroids would entrench illness behaviour. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Telling FND patients 'nothing is wrong' worsens distress, increases relapse and is associated with poorer outcomes; FND is a positive diagnosis to be communicated. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Antipsychotics are not first-line for FND and inpatient psychiatric admission is rarely indicated; physiotherapy is the evidence-based primary treatment. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.A: SSD requires one or more distressing somatic symptoms as focus; his somatic symptoms are minor and dominant feature is fear of having pancreatic cancer. Would be correct with predominant somatic symptoms (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.B: GAD requires worry across multiple domains for 6+ months; his worry is narrowly focused on single feared disease (illness anxiety pattern). Would be correct in multi-domain worry (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: OCD requires obsessions and/or compulsions consuming over 1 hour daily; his behaviours are health-focused reassurance-seeking, not classical OCD. Would be correct with classical OCD obsessions (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: Delusional disorder somatic type requires fixed false beliefs of bodily disease with delusional intensity; he accepts reassurance partially. Would be correct with delusional intensity (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Malingering requires EXTERNAL incentive (financial, legal, evasion of duty, drug-seeking); he has no claim, litigation, forensic process. Would be correct with external incentive (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.C: SSD involves genuine somatic symptoms with disproportionate thoughts/feelings/behaviours; deliberate falsification takes case out of SSD. Would be correct with genuine symptoms (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.D: Conversion requires neurological symptoms (weakness, tremor, sensory loss) without organic cause; biochemical hypoglycaemia is not neurological. Would be correct with neurological symptoms (Source: DSM-5).
rationales.E: Delusional disorder requires fixed false belief; he is engaged in covert behaviour (sharps disposal) implying awareness of deception, inconsistent with delusional intensity. Would be correct with delusional intensity (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Switching antidepressants without first excluding medical mimic risks treating thyroid disease as treatment-resistant depression; AMH and RANZCP mandate exclusion of medical cause. Would never be correct without thyroid workup (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.B: Cushing screening is for features suggestive of cortisol excess (central adiposity, striae, proximal myopathy, hypertension, hyperglycaemia); absent here. Would be correct with Cushing features (Source: APEG).
rationales.C: MRI not first-line with clear thyroid-pointing physical findings and no focal neurology; low pre-test probability. Would be correct with focal neuro findings (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.E: AMH requires confirmed biochemical hypothyroidism before levothyroxine to avoid iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.B: Escalating antipsychotic in suspected autoimmune encephalitis worsens autonomic instability and dyskinesias, may precipitate NMS-overlap, and delays diagnosis. Would never be correct (Source: Titulaer 2013).
rationales.C: HSV cover is appropriate but failing to send autoimmune encephalitis antibodies delays diagnosis driving first-line immunotherapy. Would never be correct alone (Source: Titulaer 2013).
rationales.D: NMS requires antipsychotic exposure; she has had none. CK is only mildly raised. EEG useful adjunct but not the priority. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Ovarian teratoma screening is ONCE anti-NMDAR antibodies confirm syndrome (then pelvic US/MRI first-line, not PET CT). Would be correct after antibody confirmation (Source: Titulaer 2013).

rationales.A: Adding antipsychotics without first deprescribing the precipitating drugs is wrong order; lorazepam and oxycodone are independent deliriogens. Would never be correct as initial step (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.B: Cholinesterase inhibitors are not delirium treatment; discharge of actively delirious patient with unsafe medications breaches standard. Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.C: Delirium is medical emergency managed on medical/geriatric ward; psychiatric ward transfer inappropriate and worsens outcome. Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC).
rationales.E: Asymptomatic pyuria in 84-year-old should not be treated; ciprofloxacin adds delirium risk (CNS effects). Would never be correct (Source: ACSQHC; Choosing Wisely).

rationales.B: Switching to third antidepressant monotherapy has lower evidence than lithium or atypical-antipsychotic augmentation in TRD. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP CPG).
rationales.C: Bupropion combination has weaker evidence than lithium/aripiprazole/quetiapine augmentation and is not PBS-listed for depression in Australia. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
rationales.D: Benzodiazepines are not disease-modifying augmentation in TRD and add dependence risk; not supported. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: ECT is for life-threatening (catatonia, food refusal, immediate suicide risk, severe psychotic depression) or after failure of multiple augmentations. Would be correct after augmentation failure (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Direct switch from any serotonergic agent to MAOI risks fatal serotonin syndrome. Contraindicated. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.B: Overlapping serotonergic agents with irreversible MAOI is contraindicated; cross-tapers are for SSRI-to-SSRI/SNRI switches. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: 5 days is grossly inadequate for fluoxetine because of norfluoxetine accumulation; 5-week wait required. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: Co-administration of any SSRI with MAOI is contraindicated due to serotonin syndrome risk; no low-dose is safe. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: Anticholinergic has DRY skin/mucosae, urinary retention, absent bowel sounds; patient's skin is MOIST with hyperreflexia. Would be correct with anticholinergic features (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: NMS has lead-pipe rigidity, HYPOreflexia, autonomic instability, hyperthermia AND recent antipsychotic; patient has none of those. Would be correct in NMS phenotype (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Sympathomimetic shares tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, diaphoresis but lacks prominent neuromuscular features (hyperreflexia, clonus). Would be correct only with documented stimulant use (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Malignant hyperthermia requires exposure to volatile anaesthetic or depolarising NMB; no exposure here. Would be correct only with anaesthetic exposure (Source: ANZCA).

rationales.A: Lithium 2.1 with neurological features is moderate-to-severe toxicity; continuing risks progression to seizures, coma, irreversible nephrotoxicity. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Acute toxicity with neuro features and AKI requires immediate cessation and inpatient management; week delay risks fatal progression. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium (an ion); ineffective. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Loop diuretics WORSEN lithium toxicity (reduce volume, increase proximal tubular reabsorption); volume repletion with isotonic saline is correct. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Adding valproate in woman of reproductive potential who has stopped contraception is contraindicated under TGA Valproate PPP. Would never be correct (Source: TGA).
rationales.C: Carbamazepine is teratogenic (NTDs), induces hepatic enzymes (OCP interactions) and has weaker mania prophylaxis evidence. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: Lithium absolute Ebstein anomaly risk is ~1-2 per 1000 (modest); stopping without replacement risks relapse. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.E: Lamotrigine has efficacy for bipolar DEPRESSION prophylaxis but is INFERIOR to lithium for mania prophylaxis. Would be correct only in depression-predominant bipolar (Source: AMH).

rationales.B: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed/unknown overdose (precipitates refractory seizures, especially in TCA where seizures already likely). Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).
rationales.C: Class III antiarrhythmics including amiodarone are contraindicated in TCA-induced wide-complex rhythms (further prolong QT, precipitate torsades). Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).
rationales.D: Gastric lavage is no longer recommended; charcoal at 90 min has limited benefit and unprotected airway in GCS 12 raises aspiration risk. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).
rationales.E: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose (associated with asystole and seizures); anticholinergic features are manifestation of broader toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: NSW PIC).

rationales.A: Relapse of MDD typically develops over weeks not 48 h; includes core symptoms (low mood, anhedonia, neurovegetative) absent here. Would be correct with classic depressive features (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Serotonin syndrome features hyperreflexia, clonus, hyperthermia and follows ADDITION of serotonergic agent, not dose REDUCTION. Would be correct with serotonergic addition (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: SNRIs do not cause hypothyroidism; no thyroid symptoms and no temporal link with dose change. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.E: GAD requires 6+ months of pervasive worry with characteristic features; syndrome here is acute, sensorimotor and time-locked to dose reduction. Would never be correct (Source: DSM-5).

rationales.A: Family history is non-modifiable distal risk factor; cannot be acted on to reduce acute risk. Would never be the modifiable answer (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.B: Identity is not a risk factor to be 'modified'; culturally-safe care and ACCHS linkage are the appropriate response, not pathologising identity. Would never be correct (Source: NACCHO).
rationales.C: Past self-harm is strong non-modifiable historical risk factor; cannot be addressed acutely to change risk. Would never be the modifiable answer (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.D: Hopelessness is strongest psychological correlate of suicide and modifiable through therapy and treating mood disorder but in the discharge-window timeframe it cannot be eliminated; means restriction is the immediately modifiable factor (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.A: Routine admission of BPD self-harm without acute suicide intent or psychosis worsens long-term outcomes (regression, role-reinforcement, iatrogenic harm). Would be correct only with acute suicide intent or psychosis (Source: NHMRC BPD).
rationales.B: 'No-suicide contracts' have NO evidence for reducing suicide risk and may worsen outcomes by substituting paper artefact for genuine engagement. Would never be correct (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.D: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for BPD; medications target specific comorbidities not the disorder itself. Would be correct only for documented comorbid depression/psychosis (Source: NHMRC).
rationales.E: No alcohol or substances on board tonight and no documented substance-use comorbidity; mis-referral pathologises non-existent problem. Would be correct only with documented substance comorbidity (Source: NHMRC).

rationales.B: PHQ-9 17 reflects moderate-to-severe depression with functional impact (refusing rehab, passive death thoughts); delaying treatment 3 months risks worse outcomes. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Amitriptyline (TCA) has significant anticholinergic, orthostatic, cardiac risks in older post-stroke patients; sertraline or citalopram preferred. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
rationales.D: ECT is for severe depression with catatonia, food/fluid refusal, high suicide risk, or treatment resistance; not first-line in moderate post-stroke depression. Would be correct in severe/treatment-resistant (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.E: Benzodiazepines in older adults increase falls, cognitive impairment and second stroke risk; not appropriate. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

rationales.A: Her presentation goes beyond uncomplicated grief: EPDS 18 with item 10 positive, neurovegetative features, anhedonia, worthlessness, 6 weeks of impairment. Would be correct in uncomplicated grief (Source: COPE).
rationales.B: Prolonged grief disorder requires symptoms at least 12 months after loss in adults; she is at 6 weeks. Would be correct at 12+ months (Source: DSM-5-TR).
rationales.C: Benzodiazepines are not disease-modifying in MDD or grief, risk dependence, and may impair cognitive grief processing. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).
rationales.E: Replacement child framing is contrary to perinatal bereavement guidance and risks complicated grief and PTSD in subsequent pregnancy. Would never be correct (Source: COPE).

rationales.A: Specific plan, available means at home, intended timing within 48 h with unsupervised window, recent bereavement by suicide, escalating bullying - discharge unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).
rationales.C: Fluoxetine is first-line for adolescent MDD but starting in ED with discharge into high acute-risk environment does not address immediate risk. Would be correct as part of inpatient plan (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders).
rationales.D: Mandatory reporting applies where significant harm risk from a parent; mother is supportive and immediate risk is to self, not parental. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MRG).
rationales.E: Specific plan, means, timing, recent peer suicide, active bullying are one of the highest-acuity adolescent presentations; minimising is dangerous. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

rationales.B: Vascular dementia can cause cognitive/mood symptoms but does NOT cause macrocytic anaemia, glossitis, hypersegmented neutrophils, or selective dorsal column signs. Would be correct in pure vascular cognitive impairment (Source: RANZCR).
rationales.C: Autoimmune encephalitis presents sub-acutely with psychiatric symptoms plus seizures, movement disorder, autonomic instability; macrocytic anaemia and dorsal column signs do not fit. Would be correct with autoimmune encephalitis features (Source: Titulaer 2013).
rationales.D: TSH 1.8 within reference; hypothyroidism does not produce macrocytosis of this degree, hypersegmented neutrophils, glossitis, or dorsal column signs. Would be correct with biochemically active hypothyroidism (Source: APEG).
rationales.E: Weight loss has plausible nutritional explanation (long-term veganism, B12 deficiency, glossitis impairing eating); occult malignancy workup is reasonable later if B12 replacement does not resolve. Would be correct after B12 trial without response (Source: RACGP).
